name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads featured wedding planning content and shows shortcuts into key planning tools and saved areas, using data from getHomePageContent.

core_features: Load homepage data using getHomePageContent and distribute the returned articles, projects, palettes, templates, layouts, and shortcut_counts to the relevant sections.

Display featured editorial articles grouped by category (Budget & Money, Venues, Food & Drink, and other categories available in the data) with navigation to article detail pages and category listing pages.
Display featured DIY & decor projects with navigation to the DIY & Decor listing page.
Display featured color palettes with navigation to the Inspiration: Color Palettes listing page.
Display featured planning tools (timeline templates and seating layouts) and provide navigation into the dedicated planning tools pages.

Display a shortcuts summary area that surfaces counts from shortcut_counts (Reading List, Moodboards, Planning Board, Budget Planner related articles, Projects, and To-Dos) and links to their respective pages.

Provide main navigation links to major content hubs (Home, Budget & Money, Venues, Food & Drink, DIY & Decor, Inspiration: Color Palettes, Planning Tools) that update the URL with appropriate parameters.
Support optional URL parameters on the homepage to focus or expand specific sections without affecting the API call to getHomePageContent.
user_workflows: Discover featured budget or venue content from the homepage: 1) User navigates to index.html. 2) home-data-loader component calls getHomePageContent with no parameters. 3) Once data is returned, featured-articles-section binds to featured_articles and renders article cards. 4) User clicks a category link in the main navigation or within the featured-articles-section (for example a Budget & Money or Venues category link). 5) The click triggers navigation to articles.html with a categoryId URL parameter derived from the selected category (e.g., categoryId=budget_money).

Open a featured article from the homepage: 1) User lands on index.html and data is loaded via getHomePageContent. 2) featured-articles-section renders article cards based on featured_articles. 3) User clicks on an article card. 4) The click handler reads the Article.id bound to the card and navigates to article.html with articleId set to that id in the query string. 5) The article detail page can then load its own data using articleId from the URL (outside the scope of this page).

Jump from the homepage into DIY & Decor: 1) User loads index.html; data is fetched via getHomePageContent. 2) featured-diy-section is populated with DIYProject items from featured_diy_projects. 3) User clicks on the DIY & Decor category link in the main navigation or a section-level call-to-action in the featured-diy-section. 4) The click handler navigates to diy_projects.html without additional parameters.

Explore color palette inspiration from the homepage: 1) User loads index.html; featured-color-palettes-section binds to featured_color_palettes and renders palette cards. 2) User clicks either the Inspiration: Color Palettes link in the main navigation or a section-level call-to-action. 3) The click handler navigates to color_palettes.html. 4) If a specific palette card is clickable, that click handler can also navigate to color_palettes.html with an optional paletteId or filter-related parameter in the URL, if that is supported by the target page.

Access planning tools via homepage teasers: 1) User loads index.html; planning-tools-teaser-strip binds to featured_timeline_templates and featured_seating_layouts from getHomePageContent for teaser content. 2) User clicks the Timeline Templates teaser area. 3) The click handler navigates to timelines.html. 4) Alternatively, user clicks the Seating & Floorplans teaser area and is navigated to seating_layouts.html. 5) User can then use the respective tools on those pages.

Use shortcut tiles to jump into saved areas: 1) User lands on index.html; shortcuts-summary-strip reads shortcut_counts from getHomePageContent. 2) Each shortcut tile displays the relevant count (e.g., reading_list_count) and label. 3) User clicks on a shortcut tile such as the Reading List tile. 4) The click handler navigates to the corresponding page (e.g., reading_list.html). 5) The target page uses its own logic to load detailed saved data.

Deep-link into a specific section of the homepage: 1) Another page or external link directs the user to index.html with an optional section query parameter (for example, index.html?section=shortcuts or index.html?section=tools). 2) On page initialization, homepage-state-manager reads the section parameter from the URL. 3) After home-data-loader has loaded data, homepage-state-manager scrolls or focuses the requested section (e.g., shortcuts-summary-strip or planning-tools-teaser-strip) and updates internal state to mark that section as active or expanded. 4) If the user navigates within the page (e.g., clicks a local anchor link to another section), homepage-state-manager updates the section parameter in the URL to reflect the new focused section without re-calling getHomePageContent.
interactions: Click main navigation links (Home, Budget & Money, Venues, Food & Drink, DIY & Decor, Inspiration: Color Palettes, Timeline Templates, Seating & Floorplans, Checklists) to navigate between pages, setting URL parameters as required (for example, articles.html?categoryId=budget_money).
Click article cards within featured-articles-section to navigate to article.html with the articleId parameter equal to the bound Article.id.

Click category-level call-to-action buttons or links inside featured-articles-section to navigate to the appropriate category listing view (for example, articles.html?categoryId=venues when the user activates a Venues-related CTA).
Click DIY & Decor teaser links or section-level CTAs in featured-diy-section to navigate to diy_projects.html.
Click color palette teaser links or section-level CTAs in featured-color-palettes-section to navigate to color_palettes.html.
Click Timeline Templates teaser area in planning-tools-teaser-strip to navigate to timelines.html.
Click Seating & Floorplans teaser area in planning-tools-teaser-strip to navigate to seating_layouts.html.
Click Checklists teaser area in planning-tools-teaser-strip to navigate to checklists.html.

Click shortcut tiles in shortcuts-summary-strip (Reading List, My Moodboards, Planning Board, Budget Planner, My Projects, My To-Dos) to navigate to the corresponding pages (reading_list.html, moodboards.html, planning_board.html, budget_planner.html, projects.html, todos.html).

Interact with internal in-page navigation (for example, tab controls or section links managed by homepage-state-manager) that update the section query parameter in the URL to reflect which homepage section is currently active or in focus, without affecting the call to getHomePageContent.
Reload or share the page URL containing a section query parameter so that when the page is opened again, the same section is activated or focused using the stored parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page calls getHomePageContent without any URL-based parameters, so API data retrieval is the same for any URL variant of index.html. URL parameters are used only for client-side UI state and navigation. The primary supported query parameter on index.html is section, which indicates which portion of the home content should be considered active or focused after load. Supported values can map to internal section identifiers such as shortcuts, tools, budget, venues, food_drink, diy, or inspiration. On initial load, homepage-state-manager reads window.location.search and extracts the section value. After home-data-loader completes the getHomePageContent request and the DOM for all sections has been rendered, homepage-state-manager scrolls to, highlights, or expands the section whose identifier matches section. If section is absent or does not match a known section, the page uses its default layout without special focus. When users click internal in-page navigation to move between sections, homepage-state-manager updates the section parameter in the URL using history.pushState or history.replaceState. This ensures that refreshing the page or sharing the link preserves which section is active, while not changing the data returned by getHomePageContent. All outgoing navigations to other pages encode their state exclusively via URL parameters (for example, article.html?articleId=<Article.id> or articles.html?categoryId=<Article.category_id>). No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used for state on this page.

components.functionality: On page initialization, call getHomePageContent with no parameters, await the returned object, and distribute the featured_articles, featured_color_palettes, featured_diy_projects, featured_timeline_templates, featured_seating_layouts, and shortcut_counts to child components via a shared state or properties.

Provide main navigation links for Home and the primary content categories/tools. Each link updates window.location to the appropriate target HTML file and includes required URL parameters, such as categoryId for article listing pages.

Render a set of article cards using the featured_articles array returned by home-data-loader. Optionally group or label articles by their category_id. Each card displays key Article fields and allows the user to open the article detail. Category-level controls within this section let users navigate to the full article listing for a given category.

Display DIY & decor project teasers based on featured_diy_projects, showing a subset or all returned DIYProject items. Provide a section-level control to navigate to diy_projects.html for full project browsing.

Display color palette inspiration cards using featured_color_palettes. Each card shows basic ColorPalette data (such as name and any preview image). A section-level control navigates to color_palettes.html. Card clicks can also navigate to color_palettes.html, optionally including identifying URL parameters if the target page supports them.

Use featured_timeline_templates and featured_seating_layouts to render teaser content for key planning tools. Provide prominent click targets for Timeline Templates, Seating & Floorplans, and Checklists that navigate to timelines.html, seating_layouts.html, and checklists.html respectively.

Display shortcut tiles for Reading List, Moodboards, Planning Board, Budget Planner, Projects, and To-Dos. Each tile shows its corresponding value from shortcut_counts and links to the associated detailed page upon click.

Manage URL-based UI state on the Home page. On initialization, read the section query parameter and store it as the active section identifier. After home-data-loader populates child components, ensure the active section is scrolled into view or visually marked as active. When in-page navigation elements are used to change focus between sections, update the section query parameter in the URL via the history API.

Provide a Home link and other global navigation links in the footer that mirror the behavior of primary-navigation, ensuring users can return to index.html or navigate to other main sections from the bottom of the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a top-to-bottom editorial flow. At the very top sits a slim primary navigation bar, followed immediately by a large, asymmetric hero-like area embedded in the featured-articles section: logo block on the left, title and context block on the right. Directly below this hero, still within the same section, the layout recenters into a full-width tagline band and then a centered social icon bar. Under that, the main content rail continues down the page as a single, wide central column containing the featured article feed, followed by stacked feature sections: DIY, color palettes, a full-width planning tools teaser band, and a summary strip of shortcuts. The footer navigation anchors the bottom of the page as the final horizontal band.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a top-to-bottom editorial flow. At the very top sits a slim primary navigation bar, followed immediately by a large, asymmetric hero-like area embedded in the featured-articles section: logo block on the left, title and context block on the right. Directly below this hero, still within the same section, the layout recenters into a full-width tagline band and then a centered social icon bar. Under that, the main content rail continues down the page as a single, wide central column containing the featured article feed, followed by stacked feature sections: DIY, color palettes, a full-width planning tools teaser band, and a summary strip of shortcuts. The footer navigation anchors the bottom of the page as the final horizontal band.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On large screens, content is constrained to a wide central column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with 80–120px side margins creating an airy feel. The hero portion of the featured-articles section takes up about 40–50% of the initial viewport height, with generous 64–80px vertical padding. The tagline band and social bar together use roughly another 10–15% of that initial viewport, while subsequent sections (DIY, color palettes, planning tools, shortcuts) are spaced with 48–64px gaps between them and 24–32px internal padding. Cards and strips maintain 16–32px internal padding to preserve the editorial spaciousness. The footer occupies a low but broad band at the bottom with generous top margin to clearly separate it from the main content.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is low to medium. Each section is clearly separated with ample vertical whitespace, and individual components (hero text, tags, section titles, cards) have generous breathing room. Large imagery and typographic blocks are allowed to dominate their space, with minimal supporting copy and discreet UI elements (buttons, icons) to maintain a refined, romantic editorial tone. Multi-item rows like social icons or shortcut tiles are loosely spaced, avoiding crowding and preserving a calm, scrollable reading experience.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the bold blog title in the right side of the hero, then moves left to the distinctive logo block, creating a reverse Z-pattern. From there, attention drops down to the centered tagline framed by lines, then to the social icon row. The large, portrait-oriented featured article image that follows draws the eye further down into the main feed. As the user scrolls, the flow continues vertically through the main content rail: featured articles, DIY, and color palettes. After the inspirational content, the planning tools teaser strip acts as a soft pivot into more functional content, followed by the shortcuts summary strip that provides quick access to saved/planning areas. The footer finally closes the visual journey with lower-contrast navigation and meta links.

layout.overall_description: The homepage presents as a modern, editorial wedding blog framed within a wide, centered content column. A slim, understated navigation bar sits flush at the very top, stretching full width but visually anchored to the same central grid as the content below. Immediately beneath, the screen opens into a spacious hero-like composition: on the left, a softly framed square panel showcasing the circular gold logo; on the right, a taller, light-gray vertical panel behind a large, high-contrast blog title and supporting labels, occupying slightly more than half the width. Below this asymmetric hero, the design recenters: a thin full-width line carries a short tagline in the middle, forming a delicate band that bridges hero and content. A minimalist, centered row of social icons sits directly under the tagline, lightly spaced. From there, the layout resolves into a single, image-led central rail of content: first, an oversized portrait featured article card nearly filling the content width, followed by additional featured and DIY posts stacked with ample separation. Further down, a color palettes section introduces a gentle grid of palette cards while still respecting the central column. Near the bottom, a softly tinted horizontal planning tools strip spans full width behind the content, highlighting key tools, and just below, a row of summary tiles offers shortcuts to saved planning areas. The page ends with a generous gap leading into the footer navigation bar, which mirrors the top nav’s width and alignment, giving the page a calm, cohesive start-and-end frame.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not render visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page hierarchy, initializing before any visual sections. It occupies no visual space, introduces no padding or margins, and does not disrupt the vertical editorial flow; all visible components (navigation, hero, sections, footer) are positioned as if this loader were not present in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, occupies no layout space; executes data loading before or alongside initial render without affecting positioning of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation forms a slim, full-width horizontal bar at the very top of the page. It is aligned to the same central grid as the content below, with the main content container centered and capped at roughly 1120–1200px wide. Within this bar, the brand mark or wordmark appears on the far left, and navigation links are grouped on the right, spaced with 16–24px gaps. The bar has a height of about 64–72px with 16–24px vertical padding, and a subtle bottom border or shadow to lightly separate it from the hero below. There is a generous 48–64px vertical gap between the bottom of this bar and the start of the hero area within the featured-articles section, maintaining the airy editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section at the top of the document flow (non-sticky by default), spanning 100% width; shrinks gracefully on smaller screens while keeping logo and key links visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
This is the primary visual anchor of the page and contains both the hero composition and the initial article feed. The section starts just below the primary navigation, with a top padding of about 64–80px to create a calm introduction. The content is constrained to a centered container approximately 1120–1200px wide. The upper half of this section behaves as the hero: horizontally, it is split into two asymmetric columns on large screens. The left column occupies roughly 40–45% of the container width and houses a near-square logo panel. This panel sits on a soft, off-white or very light-toned background, with 32px internal padding, and prominently features the circular gold mark centered within it, leaving generous space around it. The right column occupies about 55–60% of the width and extends vertically slightly taller than the left block, sitting on a light gray panel that visually frames the text. Within this right panel, a small uppercase category label or “BLOG” tag appears near the top right, followed by a large, bold, uppercase blog title centered vertically in the panel. Below the main title, a soft peach-colored subheading or descriptive line sits with ample line spacing, creating a tall, text-driven focal block. The two columns are horizontally aligned with a 32–40px gap between them and are vertically centered within the hero’s height, which takes roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport on desktop.

Immediately below this asymmetric hero, still within the featured-articles section but spanning full width of the container, a tagline band appears after about 32–40px of vertical space. A thin, light horizontal line runs across the width, with a short tagline phrase centered within it; the line breaks on either side of the text, creating two em-dash-like segments that frame the phrase. The text is set in thin, spaced-out type, reinforcing the editorial tone. About 16–24px below this band, a centered row of social icons appears, each icon spaced 16–24px apart. The row itself is narrow and light, with ample whitespace above and below so it reads as a delicate element rather than a dense toolbar.

Below the tagline and social bar, the section transitions into the featured articles feed, which forms the start of the main central content rail. The column narrows slightly to about 60–70% of the viewport width (still within the 1120–1200px container), centered. The first article is a prominent card with a tall, portrait-oriented hero image that nearly spans the available column width. The card has 24–32px internal padding around its text content, with the image on top and title, date, and short excerpt beneath, all well spaced. Additional featured articles follow in a vertical stack with generous 40–56px separation between cards. On smaller screens, the hero’s left and right columns collapse into a single column: the logo panel appears first, centered, followed by the title panel, then the tagline band, then the social row, then the article feed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section within the normal scroll flow; hero columns use a responsive 12-column grid (approx. 5/12 left, 7/12 right) that collapse to a single column on smaller screens. No fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section appears directly below the featured-articles section, separated by a generous 56–64px vertical gap to clearly signal a new content chapter. It continues the same centered content column and maximum width (around 1120–1200px) but relies on a single main column to preserve the editorial flow. At the top of the section, a simple, uppercase heading such as “FEATURED DIY” sits left-aligned within the main column, with ample space (24–32px) below it. Below the heading, 2–4 DIY project cards are stacked vertically. Each card is image-led, with a horizontally oriented photo spanning nearly the full width of the column, followed by a title and a short one- or two-line description. Each card has 24–32px internal padding and 32–40px spacing between cards, maintaining a light density. On larger screens, an occasional subtle asymmetry can be introduced—for example, alternating cards with the image slightly inset left or right within the column—while still keeping everything aligned to the central grid. On smaller screens, cards simply stack full-width with consistent spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked section in the main scroll, aligned to the central column. No special fixed or floating behavior; cards reflow vertically for smaller breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured color palettes section sits below the DIY section with about 56–64px of vertical separation, acting as another distinct editorial chapter. It shares the same centered container width (around 1120–1200px) but introduces a slightly more structured grid feel while still respecting the airy aesthetic. A refined section title like “COLOR PALETTE INSPIRATION” is centered at the top of the section, with generous 24–32px padding above and below it. Beneath the title, color palette cards are arranged in a loose grid: on large screens, expect 2–3 cards per row, each card occupying roughly 30–45% of the container width with 24–32px horizontal and vertical gaps. Each card is relatively shallow in height, showing a row or grid of color swatches that nearly fill the card’s width, with the palette name and a very short descriptor beneath. Cards have 16–24px internal padding, and the total number of rows is kept modest to maintain the low-to-medium density. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to one card per row, effectively returning the layout to a clean vertical stack while preserving card spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive grid section anchored within the main scroll. Cards wrap to fewer columns as the viewport narrows; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This strip acts as a gentle transition from inspirational content to practical planning tools and is positioned below the color palettes section with about 64px of vertical spacing. Visually, it appears as a low-contrast, softly tinted horizontal band that spans the full width of the viewport, creating a subtle background break from the white page body. Within this band, the content is still constrained to the central container width. The band has 32–48px of vertical padding. Inside, the layout adopts a simple two-column composition on large screens: on the left (about 55–60% of the container width), a concise heading such as “Plan Your Day” and a short line or two of explanatory copy are stacked, left-aligned with comfortable line spacing. On the right (about 40–45% width), one or two key tool teasers or a small illustrative icon cluster appears, right-aligned within the band. A primary call-to-action button sits near the left column’s text, with 12px vertical by 24px horizontal padding to match the spacing system, but still surrounded by ample white space so it does not feel crowded. On smaller screens, the layout collapses to a single vertical column: heading and copy first, CTA below, and any icon or secondary content following, all centered within the tinted band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width horizontal band within the natural scroll flow; maintains fixed vertical padding and responsive internal columns that stack on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortcuts summary strip appears directly beneath the planning-tools teaser, separated by roughly 48–56px of vertical space. It returns to the neutral page background and the central content column, functioning as a compact yet airy overview of key saved areas (e.g., favorites, checklist, guest list). At the top of this strip, a small, understated label like “YOUR SHORTCUTS” or “QUICK ACCESS” can be left-aligned within the column with 16–24px clearance beneath it. Below, a row of 3–4 summary tiles spans across the width of the central column. Each tile is a lightly bordered or softly shaded card occupying roughly 22–30% of the container width on large screens, with 16–24px horizontal gaps between them. Within each tile, an icon or small emblem appears at the top, followed by a short tile title and a simple numeric or brief text summary (e.g., “8 saved venues”). Tiles have around 16–24px internal padding and are vertically aligned so their content sits comfortably in the upper half of the tile, leaving breathing room below. On smaller screens, these tiles stack in a single column with 16–24px vertical spacing, maintaining the low-density, easy-scan feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard horizontal row that behaves like a responsive flex/grid: tiles align in a single row on wider screens and wrap/stack vertically on smaller screens. No fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual controller that manages UI state for the homepage. It does not produce any visible element or occupy physical space in the layout. Conceptually, it surrounds or sits beneath the data-loader and above the visual sections in the component tree, orchestrating what appears in the featured-articles, planning tools strip, and shortcuts strip based on user state. From a layout standpoint, all visible components are positioned as if this manager were not present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic component; no effect on spatial behavior or document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer navigation anchors the bottom of the page as a wide, calm horizontal band. It begins after a generous 80–96px vertical gap below the shortcuts summary strip, allowing the main content to feel complete before the footer appears. The band spans the full viewport width and uses a slightly darker or more muted background tone than the main body, while its internal content is constrained to the same 1120–1200px centered container, keeping alignment consistent with the rest of the page. Vertical padding is around 32–48px, giving it a substantial but not heavy presence. Inside the container, footer content may be arranged into 2–4 columns: brand or logo and a short description on the left, and grouped navigation or utility links on the right, with each group vertically spaced by 8–16px. Text is smaller and lighter in weight than the primary navigation to indicate a tertiary hierarchy. At the very bottom, a slim line of legal or copyright text may run full width within the container, with extra 16–24px spacing separating it from the main footer links. On smaller screens, the multi-column layout collapses into a single column, with sections stacked and centered while maintaining generous vertical spacing between link groups.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width block-level footer at the end of the natural scroll; no sticky behavior. Internal columns reflow responsively into a vertical stack on smaller devices.

name: Articles List
title: Articles
description: Listing page that shows wedding planning articles for a selected category, with category-specific filters, sorting, and pagination.

core_features: Load and display category-specific filter and sort options using the getArticleFilterOptions interface based on the current categoryId URL parameter.
Fetch and display a paginated list of articles for the current category using the listArticles interface, applying all active filters and sorting options from URL parameters.
Allow users to adjust filters (overall budget, published date range, guest count, venue styles, budget per guest) and automatically update the article list and URL parameters.
Allow users to change sorting (e.g., by popularity or date) and automatically refresh the article list and URL parameters.
Provide pagination controls so users can navigate between pages of results while keeping filters and sorting intact via URL parameters.
Allow users to open an article detail page by clicking on an article title or card, navigating to article.html with the selected articleId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arriving on a category from the main navigation:
1. User clicks a category link in the site header.
2. Browser navigates to articles.html with categoryId set in the URL.
3. On page load, the page reads categoryId (and any other query parameters if present).
4. The page calls getArticleFilterOptions with category_id derived from categoryId.
5. The page calls listArticles with category_id, a filters object derived from URL parameters, sort_by from sortBy, and pagination from page and pageSize.
6. The filter controls are rendered with options returned by getArticleFilterOptions and current selections derived from URL parameters.
7. The article list is rendered from the articles array returned by listArticles.

Workflow 2 – Applying or changing filters:
1. User adjusts a filter control (e.g., selects an overall budget, date range, guest count range, venue style, or budget per guest) in the filter component.
2. The filter component emits an event that constructs updated URL parameters reflecting the new filter values while preserving categoryId and other active parameters.
3. The page updates the browser URL (e.g., using history APIs) without a full reload, or triggers a reload that includes the updated query parameters.
4. The page re-derives the filters object from the updated URL parameters.
5. listArticles is called again with the same category_id and updated filters; page is reset to the first page if filters changed.
6. The article list rerenders with the new results, and the filter controls reflect the current selected values from the URL.

Workflow 3 – Changing sort order:
1. User selects a different sort option from the sort control component.
2. The sort control updates the sortBy parameter in the URL while preserving categoryId, filters, pageSize, and resetting page to the first page.
3. The page reads the updated URL, reconstructs the filters and sort_by, and calls listArticles again.
4. The article list rerenders based on the new sort order.

Workflow 4 – Paginating through results:
1. User clicks a pagination control (next page, previous page, or a specific page index).
2. The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving categoryId, filters, sortBy, and pageSize.
3. The page reads the updated URL, reconstructs the filters and sort_by, and calls listArticles with the new page value.
4. The article list rerenders with the new slice of results, and the pagination controls reflect the current page.

Workflow 5 – Opening an article detail:
1. User clicks on an article title or card within the article list component.
2. The article list component navigates to article.html, setting articleId in the target URL to the id of the selected Article.
3. The article detail page (outside the scope of this design) uses articleId to load and display the full article content.

Workflow 6 – Changing category from within articles.html:
1. User navigates to a different category (e.g., via a category switcher or header navigation while already on articles.html).
2. Browser navigates (or the page updates the URL) so that categoryId reflects the new category; filter-specific parameters may be cleared or preserved depending on relevance.
3. On navigation or URL change, the page re-calls getArticleFilterOptions with the new category_id.
4. The page constructs a new filters object from relevant URL parameters for this category and calls listArticles.
5. Filter UI is re-rendered with new options appropriate to the new category, and the article list updates accordingly.
interactions: On initial page load, read URL parameters (categoryId, overallBudgetKey, publishedDateRangeKey, guestCountRangeKey, venueStyleSlugs, budgetPerGuestKey, sortBy, page, pageSize) and call getArticleFilterOptions and listArticles using derived values.
When the categoryId URL parameter changes (via navigation), re-fetch filter options with getArticleFilterOptions and refresh the article list via listArticles, resetting pagination as needed.
When a user selects or clears an overall budget filter, update overallBudgetKey in the URL, reset page to the first page, and re-call listArticles with the updated filters.
When a user selects or clears a published date range, update publishedDateRangeKey in the URL, reset page, and refresh the article list via listArticles.
When a user selects or clears a guest count range, update guestCountRangeKey in the URL, reset page, and refresh the article list via listArticles.
When a user toggles venue style filters, update venueStyleSlugs in the URL (comma-separated list), reset page, and refresh the article list via listArticles.
When a user selects or clears a budget-per-guest range, update budgetPerGuestKey in the URL, reset page, and refresh the article list via listArticles.
When a user changes the sort option, update sortBy in the URL, reset page, and call listArticles with the new sort_by value.
When a user changes the page size control (if available), update pageSize in the URL, reset page, and re-call listArticles using the new page_size value.
When a user clicks next/previous or a specific page number in pagination, update page in the URL and re-call listArticles with the updated page while preserving all other URL parameters.

When the browser back/forward buttons are used, listen for URL changes, re-derive filters and sort_by from the URL parameters, and re-fetch data via listArticles and getArticleFilterOptions (if categoryId changed).
When a user clicks an article title or card, navigate to article.html with articleId in the URL based on the selected article's id.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The URL query parameters are the single source of truth for the page state. The required categoryId parameter determines which category of articles is displayed and is passed directly as category_id to getArticleFilterOptions and listArticles. Optional URL parameters map to filter and sorting state as follows:
- overallBudgetKey: Controls the overall budget filter. If present, it is matched to one of the options returned in overall_budget_options from getArticleFilterOptions. The filters object passed to listArticles includes a corresponding budget filter derived from this key.
- publishedDateRangeKey: Controls the published date range filter. If present, it is matched to one of the options in published_date_ranges from getArticleFilterOptions. The filters object passed to listArticles includes a corresponding date range filter.
- guestCountRangeKey: Controls the guest count range filter. If present, it is matched to one of the options in guest_count_ranges from getArticleFilterOptions. The filters object passed to listArticles includes a corresponding guest count filter.
- venueStyleSlugs: Represents one or more venue style filter selections as a comma-separated list of slugs. On read, this parameter is split into an array, intersected with venue_style_options from getArticleFilterOptions, and added to the filters object passed to listArticles.
- budgetPerGuestKey: Controls the budget-per-guest filter. If present, it is matched to a range in budget_per_guest_ranges from getArticleFilterOptions and mapped to the filters object passed to listArticles.
- sortBy: Controls the sort order. If present, it is matched to one of the values in sort_options from getArticleFilterOptions and passed as sort_by to listArticles. If absent, a default from sort_options may be used.
- page: Controls the current zero-based or one-based page index (implementation-specific but consistent with listArticles). If absent or invalid, a default first page value is used. It is passed to listArticles as page.
- pageSize: Controls the number of articles to show per page. If absent or invalid, a default page size is used. It is passed to listArticles as page_size.
When any filter or sorting interaction occurs, the corresponding URL parameter is updated. Pagination interactions update only the page (and optionally pageSize) parameters. Category changes update categoryId and may clear or ignore filters that are not relevant for the new category. On any URL change, the page reconstructs the filters object and sort_by from the current parameters and calls listArticles so that the UI always reflects the state encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Initializes page state by reading categoryId and other query parameters from the URL, fetching category-specific filter options via getArticleFilterOptions, and fetching the initial article list via listArticles.

Displays and manages all filter controls relevant to the current category, including overall budget, published date range, guest count range, venue style selection, and budget-per-guest range. It initializes its controls based on URL parameters and available options returned by getArticleFilterOptions and emits filter-change events which update URL parameters and trigger listArticles calls.

Provides a control for selecting the sort order of the article list using options returned by getArticleFilterOptions. It reflects the current sortBy URL parameter, and when changed, updates sortBy and page in the URL and triggers a refresh of the article list via listArticles.

Summarizes the currently active filters in a compact list, allowing users to see which filters are applied and optionally remove them individually or all at once. It derives its state purely from URL parameters mapped to the filter options returned by getArticleFilterOptions.

Renders the list of articles returned by listArticles for the current category and filter state, showing key metadata such as title, summary, category-specific labels, and published date as provided by the Article data. Each article entry is clickable and navigates to article.html with the corresponding articleId.

Displays pagination controls based on the page, page_size, and total_count values returned by listArticles. It allows navigation between pages and updates the page (and optionally pageSize) URL parameters while preserving currently active filters and sortBy.

Determines when there are no articles to display for the current filters based on listArticles.total_count and shows functional guidance such as suggesting that the user adjust or clear filters. It does not use hardcoded messaging content but controls whether the article-list or empty-state message is rendered.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in the main content area that comes immediately after the existing hero, centered tagline, and social bar from the mockup. The layout continues the editorial pattern by using a wide, centered content rail framed by large side margins. At the top of this rail, a slim controls band holds the applied-filters summary and sort control. Directly below, the main area splits into a light asymmetric two-column layout on larger screens: a narrow filter sidebar on the left and a wider article list column on the right, echoing the hero’s 5/7 split. On smaller screens, this collapses into a single vertical column: filter entry point (collapsed sidebar), then applied filters, then sort, then the article list, followed by pagination and any empty-state messaging where relevant.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in the main content area that comes immediately after the existing hero, centered tagline, and social bar from the mockup. The layout continues the editorial pattern by using a wide, centered content rail framed by large side margins. At the top of this rail, a slim controls band holds the applied-filters summary and sort control. Directly below, the main area splits into a light asymmetric two-column layout on larger screens: a narrow filter sidebar on the left and a wider article list column on the right, echoing the hero’s 5/7 split. On smaller screens, this collapses into a single vertical column: filter entry point (collapsed sidebar), then applied filters, then sort, then the article list, followed by pagination and any empty-state messaging where relevant.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content rail spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on large screens, with 80–120px gutters on each side. Vertical spacing between the hero/ tagline/social and the start of the article controls region is generous (around 64px), and between subsequent major bands (controls, filter/article band, pagination) is around 48–64px. Within components, padding stays within 16–32px: filter cards and article cards feel roomy and air-filled, while controls like sort dropdowns and filter chips are compact but never cramped. The filter sidebar takes about 30–35% of the central rail width on desktop, with the article list occupying the remaining 65–70%. Pagination is a shallow band at the bottom, centered and visually light.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a low-to-medium content density consistent with an editorial wedding blog. There is clear separation between the controls band, filter sidebar, article list, and pagination. Text labels and chips are spaced so no area feels cluttered; line heights are generous and components are not stacked tightly on top of one another. The article list is image-forward: each card has ample padding, generous spacing between cards, and comfortable line spacing, ensuring the content looks inspirational rather than utilitarian. Filters, summaries, and sort controls are visually quiet helpers rather than dominating blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the hero/title/tagline/social (handled elsewhere), the eye is drawn first to the applied-filters summary and sort control at the top of the content rail, which act like a slender editorial subheader for the list. From there, on desktop, the eye moves left into the vertical filter sidebar, then naturally back right to the large article images and titles, establishing a gentle reverse Z-pattern that mirrors the hero. The user then follows the strong vertical rhythm of stacked article cards downward. Pagination controls form a subtle visual stop at the bottom, inviting navigation to more content. When no results exist, the empty state replaces the article list region, centering attention on the message while keeping filters above for immediate correction.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing hero, tagline divider, and social icons, the page settles into a wide, centered content column framed by generous white space on the left and right. At the very top of this column, a slim, airy controls band spans the width: applied filter chips align to the left, while a refined sort selector sits to the right, all with plenty of vertical breathing room. Beneath this band, the column gently divides into two: on the left, a narrow, vertical filter sidebar with soft cards and generous padding; on the right, a taller, wider article list where large, portrait-oriented images and elegant typography echo the blog’s editorial style. The filter column feels like a quiet companion, while the article list is clearly the star. Each article card is separated by ample vertical space, creating a steady, scrollable rhythm of inspiration. At the bottom, centered pagination controls form a delicate horizontal punctuation mark that concludes the page. When loading, a subtle loader overlays the article area; when no articles match, a soft, centered empty state takes over the article list region, still framed by the same spacious margins, maintaining the calm, romantic aesthetic throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-context loader appears within the central content rail, anchored to the top of the article zone directly beneath the applied-filters summary and sort control. When active, it occupies a shallow band that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (the width of the content rail) and is centered horizontally on the page. Vertically, it uses generous padding (around 24–32px) above and below a subtle loading indicator and category label, creating a calm, unhurried feel. On larger screens, the loader can also softly veil the filter sidebar and article list region with a translucent wash, while keeping the hero and tagline above untouched. On smaller screens, it simply fills the full available width of the single column, pushing other content downward or overlaying just the article area in a centered, unobtrusive way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally hidden; when active, it sits as a centered overlay or inline band at the top of the main content area, aligned with the article list and filter sidebar. The overlay behavior is non-sticky and scrolls with the page, but visually dims the article region to indicate loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On larger screens, the filter-bar is a vertical sidebar positioned on the left side of the main content band beneath the controls strip. It begins approximately 48px below the applied-filters summary and sort control, aligning its top edge with the top of the first article card on the right. The filter-bar occupies about 30–35% of the central content rail’s width and runs vertically down the page next to the article list. Inside, filter groups are presented as stacked, lightly bordered cards with 24px internal padding and 24–32px vertical spacing between groups, maintaining the airy editorial feel. The right edge of the filter sidebar aligns to an internal grid line so that the article list column on the right feels cleanly delineated. On smaller screens, the filter-bar collapses into a full-width, horizontal control at the top of the content area: a slender bar with a left-aligned 'Filters' button or label and possibly a filter count, placed above the applied-filters summary with around 24px vertical separation. When expanded on mobile, the filter panel slides down as a full-width block, pushing the article list downward while preserving at least 16–24px padding on all sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, it is a static sidebar that scrolls with the page and remains visually anchored to the left of the article list; on taller pages it simply continues upward off-screen as the user scrolls. On mobile/tablet, it behaves as a collapsible panel: initially a slim horizontal bar, expanding into a vertical block that pushes content downward rather than overlaying it, in keeping with the calm editorial style.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control sits within a dedicated controls band at the top of the main content rail, directly below the tagline and social icons and above both the filter sidebar and article list. It is right-aligned within this band, positioned horizontally opposite the applied-filters summary. The controls band itself spans the full width of the central content rail (around 60–70% of the viewport, centered), with 24–32px padding above and below the elements and at least 16–24px of horizontal breathing room between the summary and the sort control. The sort selector is compact but not cramped, occupying roughly 10–15% of the content rail width, and visually aligns with the right edge of the article list column below. On smaller screens, the sort-control shifts to sit immediately below the applied-filters summary, centered or right-aligned, with 16–24px of vertical spacing and maintaining clean left/right margins so the controls stack in a single column without feeling dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the controls band; it scrolls away with the rest of the content. No sticky behavior, ensuring the focus remains on the article imagery as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters summary is the left anchor of the top controls band, directly below the tagline/social section and on the same horizontal line as the sort-control on the right. It begins roughly 64px beneath the social icon row, maintaining the generous section spacing from the mockup. Within the content rail, it occupies the left 50–60% of the width, leaving space on the right for the sort-control. Filter chips or textual tags are left-aligned, laid out on one or more rows with 8–16px horizontal spacing between chips and 8–12px vertical spacing between lines, so the summary forms a loose, breathable cluster rather than a solid block. The right edge of the summary aligns visually with the left edge of the article list column below, reinforcing the grid. On smaller screens, the applied-filters summary sits directly beneath the filter-bar’s collapsed control (if present) and above or alongside the sort-control, spanning almost the full width of the column with ample side padding (around 16–24px) and 16–24px vertical spacing to adjacent elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the controls band. Its height can grow as more filters are applied; surrounding vertical spacing allows it to expand without crowding neighboring elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list forms the primary column of the page’s main content band. On larger screens, it is positioned to the right of the filter sidebar, starting at the same vertical level so that the top edge of the first article card aligns horizontally with the topmost filter group. It occupies roughly 65–70% of the central content rail’s width, with a clear vertical boundary between it and the filter sidebar. The article column is centered within the overall page frame, with large left/right margins from the viewport edges (around 80–120px), and it follows a simple single-column stack of article cards. Each article card spans nearly the full width of the article column with 24–32px internal padding and at least 32–48px of vertical spacing between cards. Featured images are large and dominant, often portrait-oriented, spanning the full width of the card, with generous whitespace around titles, excerpts, and metadata below. This creates a calm, scrollable editorial feed. On smaller screens, the article-list becomes a full-width column below the controls and any expanded filter panel, maintaining the same vertical rhythm of cards and spacing but using the full device width minus modest side padding (around 16–24px) to keep things airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable; it defines the main vertical flow of the page. It does not fix or pin in place, allowing the user to smoothly scroll through articles while filters and controls scroll out of view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the article-list column, acting as a visual endpoint to the content feed. They are centered horizontally within the same width as the article-list, with a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px above them to separate the last article card from the controls. The pagination row itself is relatively shallow: discrete page numbers, next/previous arrows, or a 'Load more' style control arranged in a single horizontal line. There is ample horizontal spacing (12–16px) between page numbers and 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the clickable elements, so the controls feel light and unobtrusive. The entire pagination block remains within the central content rail, framed by the same large left/right page margins, creating a subtle horizontal punctuation mark at the bottom of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the list; it scrolls into view after the user reaches the end of the article cards. It does not stick to the viewport, preserving the clean, uninterrupted scroll experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state handler occupies the same spatial region as the article-list when no articles are available for the selected filters or category. It appears directly below the controls band (applied filters and sort) and next to the filter sidebar on larger screens, or below the filters on smaller screens. Its content is centered within the article column: an illustration or icon, a primary message, and a secondary suggestion, all stacked vertically with generous spacing (24–32px between elements and 48–64px top margin from the controls band). Horizontally, it spans the width of the article column but maintains large margins on the sides, matching the article cards; text is center-aligned to emphasize the message. The filter sidebar remains visible on the left on desktop, making it easy to adjust settings, while the empty state sits like a calm, open space where the articles would normally appear. On mobile, the empty state is centered in the single column, with the same airy padding and spacing, and appears immediately after the filter controls so users see it without scrolling far.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Only rendered when there are no articles. It replaces the article-list content within the same layout slot; it scrolls normally with the page and does not overlay filters or controls. As filters are changed, it disappears in favor of the article list when results become available.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single wedding planning article with full content, metadata, and actions to save it to the Reading List or link it to the Budget Planner.

core_features: Load and display full article details based on the articleId URL parameter using getArticleDetail.
Show article metadata such as category name, published date, estimated budget ranges, guest count guidance, venue style tags, per-guest budget, and estimated read time.
Render the full article body, including headings, sections, images, and any structured content (such as tables).
Allow users to save the article to their Reading List, with an optional personal note, using saveArticleToReadingList.
Confirm successful saving and provide navigation to the Reading List page (reading_list.html).
For articles that support it, allow users to add the article to the Budget Planner using addArticleToBudgetPlanner.
After adding to the Budget Planner, provide navigation to budget_planner.html, including a focusCategorySlug URL parameter when the article is categorized under catering.
Conditionally show budget-planner-related actions only when can_add_to_budget_planner is true from getArticleDetail.
Handle loading, success, and error states for all interface calls without using local storage, relying on URL parameters and in-memory page state only.
user_workflows: View article details: 1) User navigates to article.html with an articleId URL parameter from another page. 2) On page load, the page reads articleId from the URL. 3) The page calls getArticleDetail with articleId. 4) While waiting, a loading state is displayed for the article area. 5) On success, the article metadata and content are rendered. 6) If getArticleDetail indicates can_add_to_budget_planner is true, budget-related actions are shown; otherwise they remain hidden. 7) If the interface call fails, an error message is shown with an option to retry the request.

Save article to Reading List: 1) With the article loaded, the user clicks the 'Save to reading list' action. 2) An input control (inline field or dialog) allows the user to optionally enter a note. 3) The user confirms the save action. 4) The page calls saveArticleToReadingList with the current articleId and the entered note (or an empty note). 5) While the request is in progress, the save control is disabled and a saving state is indicated. 6) On success, the component updates to reflect that the article has been saved (e.g., toggling button state) and shows feedback including the total_saved_count from the response if needed. 7) A 'View reading list' navigation control becomes available and links to reading_list.html. 8) If the request fails, an error message is shown and the user can retry.

Add article to Budget Planner: 1) With the article loaded and can_add_to_budget_planner=true, the user clicks the 'Add to budget plan' action. 2) The page calls addArticleToBudgetPlanner with the current articleId. 3) While the request is in progress, the action control is disabled and a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the component updates to indicate the article has been linked to the Budget Planner and stores the returned budget_article_link in local page state. 5) A 'View in budget planner' navigation control becomes available. 6) When the user clicks 'View in budget planner', the page navigates to budget_planner.html; if the article’s budget_category_slug from getArticleDetail is 'catering', the navigation includes the focusCategorySlug=catering URL parameter. 7) If addArticleToBudgetPlanner fails, an error message is shown and the user can retry the action.

Navigate back to Article Detail with different articleId: 1) User arrives at article.html with a specific articleId in the URL. 2) The user follows an internal link (for example, from related content or from their Reading List) that points to article.html with a different articleId. 3) The browser loads the page (or updates it if navigation is handled client-side) and the page reads the new articleId from the URL. 4) The component re-runs getArticleDetail with the new articleId, resets action states (e.g., clears previous success messages), and renders the newly loaded article data.
interactions: On page load or when the articleId URL parameter changes, the page initiates a call to getArticleDetail with the current articleId.
If articleId is missing from the URL, the page shows a functional error state indicating that no article can be displayed and does not call getArticleDetail.
Clicking the 'Save to reading list' button opens or focuses a note input control and, upon confirmation, triggers saveArticleToReadingList with articleId and the note value.
While saveArticleToReadingList is pending, the 'Save to reading list' button is disabled or shows a loading state to prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful saveArticleToReadingList, the UI reflects success (e.g., marking the article as saved in the current session) and reveals a 'View reading list' link that navigates to reading_list.html when clicked.
If saveArticleToReadingList returns an error, an error message is displayed and a retry option re-invokes saveArticleToReadingList with the same parameters.
If getArticleDetail.can_add_to_budget_planner is true, clicking the 'Add to budget plan' button triggers addArticleToBudgetPlanner with articleId.
While addArticleToBudgetPlanner is pending, the 'Add to budget plan' button is disabled or shows a loading state and cannot be clicked again.
On successful addArticleToBudgetPlanner, the UI shows confirmation that the article is linked to the Budget Planner and reveals a 'View in budget planner' link.
Clicking 'View in budget planner' navigates to budget_planner.html; when the loaded article’s budget_category_slug is 'catering', the navigation appends a focusCategorySlug=catering URL parameter.
If addArticleToBudgetPlanner fails, an error message is shown and a retry control allows the user to re-trigger addArticleToBudgetPlanner.

Article content interactions (such as clicking internal links to other articles) update the browser location to article.html with a new articleId parameter, which in turn re-triggers the article loading behavior.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by the articleId URL parameter. When the page loads, it reads articleId from the query string (e.g., article.html?articleId={value}). If articleId is defined, the page calls getArticleDetail(articleId) and enters a 'loading' state for the article data. When getArticleDetail succeeds, the state transitions to 'loaded', storing the returned article object, category_name, can_add_to_budget_planner, and budget_category_slug in memory for binding in child components. If the call fails, the state transitions to an 'error' state, and no subsequent actions (save to reading list or add to budget plan) are enabled until a retry succeeds. Save-to-reading-list state is local to the page: after saveArticleToReadingList succeeds, the page marks the current article as 'saved' in its transient state and exposes the 'View reading list' navigation. Budget integration state is also ephemeral to the page: after addArticleToBudgetPlanner succeeds, the page stores the returned budget_article_link object and exposes 'View in budget planner'. Navigation to reading_list.html and budget_planner.html uses standard URL-based navigation. When navigating to budget_planner.html from this page, if the article’s budget_category_slug returned by getArticleDetail equals 'catering', the page includes focusCategorySlug=catering as a query parameter (e.g., budget_planner.html?focusCategorySlug=catering); otherwise, it navigates to budget_planner.html without a focusCategorySlug parameter. At no point is localStorage or similar client-side persistence used; all persisted data is represented through backend interfaces and the current URL.

components.functionality: Reads the articleId from the URL query string and fetches article details via getArticleDetail. Manages loading, success, and error states and provides the resulting data to other components.

Displays high-level article metadata such as title, category name, published date, estimated overall budget range, per-guest budget range, guest count guidance, venue style tags, and estimated read time.
Renders the full article body, including headings, paragraphs, images, and any structured content such as tables, using the Article.content and hero_image fields.

Provides controls to save the current article to the Reading List. Handles note entry, triggers saveArticleToReadingList, and shows success or error feedback along with a link to view the Reading List.

Shows the 'Add to budget plan' action when can_add_to_budget_planner is true. Calls addArticleToBudgetPlanner, manages in-progress and success/error states, and exposes a 'View in budget planner' navigation that includes focusCategorySlug=catering when appropriate.
Displays error messages and retry options when getArticleDetail, saveArticleToReadingList, or addArticleToBudgetPlanner fail. Only visible when the corresponding error state is active.
Shows loading indicators while article details or actions are in progress, such as during getArticleDetail, saveArticleToReadingList, or addArticleToBudgetPlanner calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup. The article detail page opens with a hero-style band anchored by the article-metadata-panel, using an asymmetric two-column composition within the central content rail (roughly 5/12 for secondary labels on the left and 7/12 for the title and key info on the right on large screens). Beneath this, a centered action band replaces the tagline + social icons from the mockup: the reading-list-actions and budget-planner-integration appear as a horizontal, lightly framed bar in the center of the page. Below that, the article-content-viewer takes over as a long, vertically scrolling single-column narrative, maintaining the central editorial rail. The loader, loading indicator, and error state are layered into this structure: they occupy the same central column and hero area, either overlaying or replacing the main content when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup. The article detail page opens with a hero-style band anchored by the article-metadata-panel, using an asymmetric two-column composition within the central content rail (roughly 5/12 for secondary labels on the left and 7/12 for the title and key info on the right on large screens). Beneath this, a centered action band replaces the tagline + social icons from the mockup: the reading-list-actions and budget-planner-integration appear as a horizontal, lightly framed bar in the center of the page. Below that, the article-content-viewer takes over as a long, vertically scrolling single-column narrative, maintaining the central editorial rail. The loader, loading indicator, and error state are layered into this structure: they occupy the same central column and hero area, either overlaying or replacing the main content when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero/metadata band occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport on desktop, with generous padding above and below (48–64px) to echo the airy mockup. The centered action band (reading-list-actions + budget-planner-integration) uses about 10–15% of the visible vertical space right below the hero but feels visually light due to thin typography and ample whitespace. The article-content-viewer then extends downward, using the full height needed but constrained horizontally to an editorial rail of about 60–70% of the page width, centered with large side margins (80–120px on wide screens). Major vertical separations between sections (hero, actions band, body content, error states) follow the 48–64px rhythm, while inner paddings in panels and cards use 16–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low to medium across the page. The metadata hero surfaces only essential information—title, subheading, tags, date, location, read time—in a clean, spacious arrangement with generous line-height and letter spacing, especially for labels. The action band contains just two clear actions, separated but not crowded, with ample padding around them. In the article-content-viewer, paragraphs, images, and pull quotes are given breathing room, never crammed edge-to-edge. Loading and error states respect this same spaciousness, appearing as centered, minimally worded panels with generous margins rather than dense blocks of text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s reverse Z-pattern and then vertical descent. On load, the eye first lands on the large article title within the metadata hero on the right side of the central rail. From there, it moves left to the supporting metadata labels, creating a subtle leftward pull. The gaze then drops slightly to secondary information (category, date, location, read time) and continues down into the centered action band (reading-list-actions and budget-planner-integration) which acts like the tagline + social icons line from the mockup—a thin, elegant pause. Finally, the eye is guided downward into the article-content-viewer, where large imagery and clean typography maintain a strong vertical reading flow. When present, the loading indicator appears in the center of this central rail, briefly becoming the main focal point; error states are also centered, replacing the hero and early content to clearly signal the issue.

layout.overall_description: The article detail page reads like a modern editorial spread tailored to wedding inspiration. At the top, a wide hero band stretches almost the full width of the central content column, set within generous left and right margins that keep everything feeling airy. Within this band, the article-metadata-panel is arranged on an implicit 12-column grid: on large screens, the left ~5 columns are reserved for compact metadata (category, tags, location, date, read time), while the right ~7 columns showcase a tall, light-gray vertical panel behind a bold, large article title and an optional romantic subheading—visually echoing the listing page’s title block. The whole hero has substantial white space around the text, occupying roughly the upper half of the initial viewport so the title can breathe. Just below, spanning the same central width, a slim, centered action band presents the reading-list-actions and budget-planner-integration side by side, framed by a subtle horizontal divider line, much like the mockup’s centered tagline flanked by lines with a social icon row underneath. These actions feel like delicate controls rather than heavy UI, using slim text and light icons. Underneath, the layout resolves into a single, centered content rail where the article-content-viewer flows vertically: a large hero image or portrait-style photo near the top, followed by well-spaced headings, copy, and images. Line heights are generous, and each content block has clear separation, maintaining a tranquil, romantic atmosphere. When the article is loading, a soft, centered loading indicator occupies the middle of this central rail while the data-loader conceptually covers the hero and content area; if an error occurs, a simple, centered feedback panel replaces the main content with an empathetic message. On smaller screens, the asymmetric hero collapses into one column: title first, then metadata, then actions band, all stacked with consistently generous vertical spacing, preserving the editorial feel while remaining fully readable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-loader conceptually wraps the main article area—from the top of the hero/metadata band down through the entire article-content-viewer region. When active during the initial load, it dims or gently blurs the central content column without affecting the site’s global header or outer chrome. Visually, it can be imagined as a soft, semi-transparent veil spanning the width of the main content rail (about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered) and extending from roughly 80px below the site header down past the fold. This veil preserves the generous side margins and vertical padding defined by the editorial layout, ensuring that even the loading state feels harmonious with the page design. Non-essential interactions inside this region (like reading-list-actions and budget-planner-integration) appear disabled or muted under this overlay while the loader is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-height overlay within the central content column when active, positioned relative to the top of the article area. It scrolls with the page content (not fixed to the viewport) so that loading always appears associated with the article region. When inactive, it occupies no visible space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-metadata-panel forms the hero band at the top of the article, occupying the full width of the central content rail and roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport on desktop. It is vertically padded with about 64px above and below, creating a calm, spacious introduction. Inside, it adheres to the implicit 12-column grid: on large screens, the left ~5 columns host a vertically centered stack of compact metadata—small uppercase labels for category, tags, date, location, and estimated read time, arranged with generous 8–16px gaps. These appear left-aligned and aligned mid-height relative to the title block for a balanced asymmetry. The right ~7 columns form a tall, light-gray vertical panel that sits behind the main article title. This panel has 32px internal padding and extends slightly taller than the text, echoing the mockup’s typographic block. The article title is large, bold, and left-aligned within this panel, taking up 2–3 lines, with a softer, pastel-toned romantic subheading directly beneath, separated by about 16px. A small, uppercase label such as “ARTICLE” or a tag can sit in the upper-right corner of this panel, mimicking the small ‘BLOG’ chip in the mockup. On tablets and smaller screens, this panel collapses into a single column: the title block appears first, full width, followed by the metadata stack below it, maintaining generous vertical spacing (24–32px) between the two.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow. It sits immediately below any global site header and does not stick or float; as the user scrolls, it exits the viewport, allowing focus to move naturally into the article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content-viewer occupies the main body area directly below the action band (reading-list-actions and budget-planner-integration). It aligns to the same central content rail as the metadata hero, spanning approximately 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, centered with generous 80–120px side margins. The top of the content viewer begins about 48px below the action band to honor the section spacing rhythm. Within this viewer, content flows vertically: typically a large lead image or hero photo appears near the top, spanning the full width of the content rail, similar in scale to the tall portrait image seen in the mockup’s first article card. Beneath the image, headings, paragraphs, lists, and inline images are stacked with 24–32px spacing between blocks and comfortable line-height, creating a relaxed reading cadence. Any pull quotes or callouts are given extra breathing room (32–48px above and below) and may be slightly indented or centered while still respecting the central rail. On smaller screens, the content viewer becomes a single column with narrower side padding (around 16–24px), but keeps the same generous vertical spacing to maintain the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable main content region. It grows vertically as the article length increases and defines the primary scroll experience. It does not fix or float; it simply continues the vertical reading flow started by the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-actions panel appears immediately below the article-metadata-panel, as part of a centered, slim action band that visually replaces the tagline + social icons row from the mockup. It is horizontally centered within the same content rail and sits about 32–40px beneath the bottom of the metadata hero, separated by a subtle horizontal rule that spans roughly 60–70% of the content rail. The reading-list-actions occupy the left half of this action band on desktop: think of it as a refined, compact group containing a main button (e.g., “Save to Reading List”) with 12px vertical × 24px horizontal padding, plus a light, text-link–style label beside or just below it (such as “View my reading list”), spaced by 16px. The panel’s elements are aligned centrally along the vertical axis of the band, with plenty of white space above and below (about 16–24px), so the strip feels light and unobtrusive. On smaller screens, this panel stacks above the budget-planner-integration within the same band, each centered and separated by 16–24px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow. It sits as a horizontal strip between the metadata hero and the article-content-viewer, scrolling away with the page as users move down. It should not be sticky by default to preserve the calm, editorial nature of the layout; its visibility relies on its prominent but light placement near the top of the article.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-planner-integration shares the same centered action band as the reading-list-actions, mirroring the social icon row placement in the mockup. On desktop, it sits to the right of the reading-list-actions within that band, creating a balanced two-part horizontal composition: reading-list-actions on the left, budget-planner-integration on the right, both aligned along the same baseline and centered within the content rail. This panel may contain a primary or secondary button (e.g., “Add to Budget Planner”) and possibly a subtle descriptive line beneath, with each internal element spaced by 8–16px and padded with around 16–24px internally. The overall width of this right segment is visually similar to the left, so the band feels symmetrical even if text lengths differ. Generous negative space separates this band from both the hero above and the content viewer below, keeping it feeling delicate and unobtrusive. On smaller screens, the budget-planner-integration stacks beneath the reading-list-actions, centered and full-width within the content rail, with about 16–24px vertical gap between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow, sharing the same strip as the reading-list-actions. It scrolls away with the content and is not sticky. Its placement directly under the hero ensures it is seen early without competing with the article title.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-error-state appears only when the article fails to load and replaces both the article-metadata-panel and the article-content-viewer within the central content rail. Visually, it is a centered feedback panel occupying the same width as the main content rail (about 60–70% of the viewport), positioned starting roughly where the metadata hero would be. It uses generous vertical padding (32–40px inside the panel, with 64px above and 64px below the panel) and plenty of white space so that the message feels calm rather than alarming.
