The panel can be imagined as a soft, off-white or light pastel card with a thin border or subtle drop shadow, containing a concise title (e.g., “We couldn’t load this article”), a short explanatory line, and a clear call-to-action (like “Try again” or “Back to blog”). All text is center-aligned, reinforcing the idea of a central, editorial note on the page. On small screens, the card still spans the full available width minus modest side padding (16–24px), maintaining an airy, approachable appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a full replacement of the article area in the normal page flow. It is not fixed; it scrolls naturally if other global page elements exist above it. When active, it occupies the vertical space that would otherwise be used by the hero and article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-loading-indicator is a focused visual cue that appears within the central content rail while the article-detail-loader is active. It is positioned at the vertical center of the viewport within the article area (roughly mid-screen), horizontally centered, and surrounded by ample blank space. Imagine a small, elegant spinner or minimal progress ring with a thin stroke, accompanied by a short, light-weight label (“Loading your article…”) beneath it with about 12–16px spacing. The indicator itself occupies only a small footprint (around 48–64px in diameter), but it is the only active element within a large field of negative space, making it the clear focal point without feeling heavy. It does not stretch full width and is visually detached from the edges, aligning with the soft, minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered above the article’s hero and body within the central column, typically layered on top of the dimmed content via the article-detail-loader. It remains centered relative to the viewport as the user scrolls during loading, either by being fixed to the center of the viewport or by appearing in the initially visible region and preventing scroll until load completes, depending on implementation. When loading finishes, it disappears entirely and frees the underlying layout.

name: Timeline Templates List
title: Timeline Templates
description: Page for browsing, filtering, and sorting wedding day timeline templates and opening a selected template's detail view.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options from the timeline template filters API and render controls for guest count range, season, location type, timeline length, and ceremony time.
List timeline templates using the listing API according to the currently selected filters, sort option, and pagination settings.
Allow users to adjust filters and sort order, with all selections reflected in the URL and used to reload the timeline templates list.
Provide pagination controls so users can move between pages of results and adjust how many templates are shown per page.
Enable navigation to a specific timeline template detail page when a user clicks a template title or card.
Show applied filters in a concise summary with options to clear individual filters or reset all filters.
Handle loading and error states for both filter metadata and timeline template listings.
user_workflows:
1) Landing on the page from navigation:
   - User opens timelines.html without URL parameters via site navigation.
   - The page reads the empty URL state and initializes default filter and sorting values (no filters selected, default sort, default page and page size).
   - The page calls getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions with no parameters.
   - After receiving filter options, it populates all filter controls and sort options.
   - The page then calls listTimelineTemplates with filters derived from the current (default) state, sort_by set to the default, and page/page_size from the initial state.
   - The returned timeline_templates are displayed in the list, along with pagination based on total_count, page, and page_size.

2) Landing on a pre-filtered, shareable URL:
   - User opens timelines.html with URL parameters such as guestCountRangeKey, season, locationType, lengthHours, ceremonyTime, sortBy, page, and pageSize.
   - The page parses these URL parameters into internal state for filters, sort, and pagination.
   - The page calls getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions and renders filter controls, pre-selecting options that match the values from the URL.
   - The page calls listTimelineTemplates with a filters object populated from the URL parameters, sort_by from the sortBy parameter, and page/page_size from the URL.
   - The list, sort control, filter summary, and pagination reflect the URL state, producing a shareable filtered view.

3) Applying or changing filters:
   - User changes one or more filter controls (for example, selects a guest count range or a season) in the filter component.
   - On filter change, the page resets the current page to the first page and updates the corresponding URL parameters (guestCountRangeKey, season, locationType, lengthHours, ceremonyTime) while preserving other parameters (sortBy, pageSize) where appropriate.
   - The URL update triggers a refresh sequence (directly or via an internal state observer): listTimelineTemplates is called with the updated filters and current sort_by, page set to 1, and current page_size.
   - The list, filter summary, and pagination update to reflect the new filter state.

4) Changing sort order:
   - User selects a different sort option from the sort control, which is populated from sort_options returned by getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions.
   - The new sort value is written to the sortBy URL parameter and the current page is reset to the first page.
   - listTimelineTemplates is called with existing filters, the new sort_by value, page = 1, and the current page_size.
   - The templates list re-renders in the new sort order while preserving selected filters.

5) Paginating through results:
   - User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number) or changes the number of results per page.
   - The page and/or pageSize URL parameters are updated accordingly.
   - listTimelineTemplates is called with current filters, current sort_by, and the new page and/or page_size values.
   - The list re-renders to show templates for the selected page, and pagination controls update to reflect the new current page.

6) Viewing a timeline template detail:
   - User clicks on a timeline template card or its title in the list.
   - The click handler reads the selected template's id from the listTimelineTemplates response data.
   - The browser navigates to timeline.html with timelineTemplateId set to the selected id in the query string.
   - The current filters and sort state remain in the URL of timelines.html, making it easy for the user to return to the list with the same configuration via the Back button.

7) Clearing filters and handling empty states:
   - If the filters applied result in no matches (total_count equals zero), the page displays an empty-state component bound to the current filters.
   - User can click a control in the empty-state component to clear all filters, or they may clear individual filters via the applied filter summary.
   - Clearing filters updates or removes the relevant URL parameters, resets the page to the first page, and triggers a new listTimelineTemplates call.
   - Once results exist again, the empty-state component is hidden and the list is shown.
interactions: Filter selection/change: When a user selects or changes a value in any filter control (guest count range, season, location type, timeline length, ceremony time), the filter component updates the corresponding URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers a new call to listTimelineTemplates with an updated filters object.

Filter reset (all): When a user clicks a 'clear all filters' control, all filter-related URL parameters (guestCountRangeKey, season, locationType, lengthHours, ceremonyTime) are removed, the page is reset to the first page, and listTimelineTemplates is called with an empty or default filters object.

Filter reset (single): When a user clears an individual filter chip from the applied filter summary, only that specific filter’s URL parameter is removed or set to an empty value, page is reset to the first page, and listTimelineTemplates is called with the remaining filters.

Sort selection/change: When a user selects a sort option from the sort control, the sortBy URL parameter is updated, page is reset to the first page, and listTimelineTemplates is called with the existing filters and the new sort_by value.

Pagination navigation: When a user moves between pages using pagination (next, previous, or specific page number), the page URL parameter is updated and listTimelineTemplates is called with the new page while preserving current filters, sort_by, and page_size.

Page size change: When a user changes how many templates to show per page, the pageSize URL parameter is updated, page is reset to the first page, and listTimelineTemplates is called with the new page_size and existing filters and sort_by.

Template selection: When a user clicks a timeline template card or title, a navigation event is triggered that builds a URL of the form timeline.html with timelineTemplateId set to the selected template's id.

Initial page load: On initial load or when the browser's Back/Forward buttons are used, the URL-state manager reads current URL parameters, initializes internal state, calls getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions, and then calls listTimelineTemplates with a filters object and sort_by, page, and page_size derived from the URL.

Error handling: If getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions or listTimelineTemplates returns an error, the data-loader component displays an error message with a retry action. When the user clicks retry, the same interface is called again with the current URL-derived state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page treats the URL query parameters as the single source of truth for current filters, sorting, and pagination. On every load or history navigation, the URL is parsed to extract guestCountRangeKey, season, locationType, lengthHours, ceremonyTime, sortBy, page, and pageSize. These values are mapped into an internal state object, which is used to form the `filters` and other parameters passed into listTimelineTemplates. The `filters` object includes keys corresponding to the filter-related URL parameters (guestCountRangeKey, season, locationType, lengthHours, ceremonyTime), while sortBy is translated to the sort_by argument, and page and pageSize are passed as page and page_size respectively. If some parameters are missing, internal defaults (such as an unset filter, default sort option, and default page/pageSize) are applied before calling listTimelineTemplates. Whenever the user changes filters, sort order, or pagination, the page updates the URL query string to reflect the new state by adding, modifying, or removing these parameters. The URL update is then used to trigger or coordinate a fresh call to listTimelineTemplates so that the displayed data always matches the URL. Because the state is fully encoded in the URL, any link to timelines.html with a specific set of parameters will reconstruct the same view, and browser Back/Forward navigation will correctly restore prior configurations without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Parses URL parameters on page load and history changes, initializes internal state for filters, sort, and pagination, and updates the URL when the user interacts with filters, sorting, or pagination.

Displays filter controls for guest count range, season, location type, timeline length, and ceremony time using options from getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions, and updates URL parameters and list state when filters change.
Renders a sort selector populated from sort_options returned by getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions and updates the sortBy URL parameter and listing when the user changes the sort option.
Shows a concise summary of currently selected filters based on URL parameters and provides controls for clearing individual filters or all filters, which updates the URL and triggers data reloads.
Displays the list of timeline templates returned from listTimelineTemplates, showing key attributes for each template and handling clicks on a template card or title to navigate to its detail page.
Shows pagination controls and page size options using page, page_size, and total_count from listTimelineTemplates, and updates the URL and listing when the user changes page or page size.

When listTimelineTemplates returns zero results (total_count indicates no matches), shows an empty-state message bound to current filters and offers an action to reset filters, which clears filter URL parameters and reloads the listing.

Manages loading and error states for calls to getTimelineTemplateFilterOptions and listTimelineTemplates, showing loading indicators while data is being fetched and error messages with retry controls if API calls fail.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: All page-specific components sit in the central, single-column content rail that begins directly beneath the global hero / tagline / social bar pattern from the mockup. The top of this rail is a functional control band (filters + sort), followed by a light, editorial-style applied-filters summary, then the vertically stacked timeline template cards. Pagination anchors the bottom of the rail. Conditional states (empty state, loading overlay) replace or overlay the list region but preserve the same vertical structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All page-specific components sit in the central, single-column content rail that begins directly beneath the global hero / tagline / social bar pattern from the mockup. The top of this rail is a functional control band (filters + sort), followed by a light, editorial-style applied-filters summary, then the vertically stacked timeline template cards. Pagination anchors the bottom of the rail. Conditional states (empty state, loading overlay) replace or overlay the list region but preserve the same vertical structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On large screens, the main content rail is centered and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width with 80–120px side margins, staying within a max content width that preserves the editorial feel. The filter/sort band and applied-filters summary together use the upper ~15–20% of the scrollable content area below the hero, the template list dominates the middle ~60–70%, and pagination and bottom whitespace occupy the remaining ~10–15%. Vertical spacing between major bands (filter area, applied summary, list, pagination) follows the 48–64px rhythm, while inner padding for controls and cards stays in the 16–32px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Controls and list items are arranged with low-to-medium density: clear grouping, generous padding, and ample white space between rows. The filter bar reads as a light band with enough breathing room around inputs to avoid a 'dashboard' feel. Timeline cards are image-forward with comfortable spacing between them (24–32px) and generous internal padding for text. Applied filters and pagination use lighter typography and more whitespace to keep them visually understated and unobtrusive.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the shared hero/title area, the eye is drawn first to the filter/sort band at the top of the central rail, which functions like the page-level anchor. From there, the gaze steps down to the applied-filters summary (which subtly contextualizes the current view), then flows into the prominent vertical stack of image-driven template cards. Each card encourages a downward reading progression. At the end of the list, the eye naturally lands on the centered pagination controls. Loading and empty states appear in the same central column region as the list, so they don’t disrupt the established top-to-bottom flow—only what is seen within that block.

layout.overall_description: Below the romantic, asymmetric hero and centered tagline/social band, the page transitions into a calm, editorial content rail dedicated to browsing wedding day timeline templates. This rail is centered on the page, occupying roughly two-thirds of the viewport width with generous white margins on both sides. At its top, a horizontal control band presents filters aligned to the left and a sort control aligned to the right, all resting within a softly padded area that feels like a light, functional chapter heading. Just beneath, a delicate line of text and small filter chips summarize the active criteria, stretching no wider than the main column and maintaining the airy typographic style. The main body is a vertical series of large, image-led template cards, each spanning the width of the rail and separated by comfortable white space so each feels like an individual editorial feature. At the bottom, centered pagination controls sit with ample breathing room, acting as a gentle endpoint before the page’s lower whitespace. If there are no results, a serene empty state occupies the center of the rail where the list would be, while loading states subtly overlay the central column, ensuring the layout’s structure and vertical rhythm remain recognizable and consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the top level of the page structure, tying together the filter-controls, sort-controls, applied-filters-summary, timeline-templates-list, pagination-controls, empty-state, and data-loader. It should not create any margins, padding, or visual artifacts. Its influence is only perceived through synchronized URL parameters and restored UI state when the user returns to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; behaves as an invisible controller attached to the page root or main content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is the first visible, page-specific element in the main content rail below the global hero/tagline/social sections. It sits centered horizontally within the page, matching the width of the central column (approximately 60–70% of viewport width, with max-width constraints to keep it from feeling too wide). The bar itself is a horizontal band with 24–32px of vertical padding and 24px of horizontal padding inside, spanning the full width of the column. Filter inputs (dropdowns, pills, checkboxes) are left-aligned within this band, arranged in a single row on large screens, with items spaced by 16–24px and wrapping gracefully to a second line if necessary. On small screens, the entire filter group collapses into a stacked arrangement, still left-aligned but now reading top-to-bottom with 12–16px gaps. The band maintains generous top and bottom margins (48–64px from the tagline/social section above and roughly 32px down to the applied-filters-summary), keeping the density light and consistent with the editorial aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow. It scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport, preserving the calm, editorial reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls share the same horizontal band as the filter-controls, living within the same central content rail. On large screens, they are right-aligned in the filter band, directly opposite the primary filter group: filters on the left, sort on the right, creating a balanced left-right tension reminiscent of the mockup’s asymmetric hero. The sort control occupies roughly 20–25% of the content rail’s width, with 16–24px of internal padding and clear spacing from the right edge. It appears on the same vertical baseline as the primary filter elements, maintaining visual alignment. On smaller screens, the sort control drops below the filters, still within the same band but aligned left so that the entire control area reads as a single vertical block. In all cases, there is at least 24px of breathing room above and below the sort control inside the band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, embedded in the same band as the filter-controls. It adapts from right-aligned (desktop) to stacked (mobile) but does not become sticky or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-summary sits directly beneath the filter/sort band, centered within the same main content rail. It is separated from the band above by a comfortable vertical gap of about 24–32px, creating a clear but gentle distinction. The summary spans the full width of the central column but uses a typographically light treatment: a single line or short paragraph of text (e.g., “Showing 12 timeline templates for summer weddings”) centered horizontally or subtly left-aligned within the column, with any filter chips arranged in a loose horizontal row beneath or inline. Chips have 8–12px of internal padding and 8px gaps between them, maintaining delicacy. Overall vertical height of this section is modest—roughly 10–15% of the visible content area before the list begins—helping it feel like a fine editorial caption before the main features. There is a 32–48px gap below this summary before the first timeline card, reinforcing the page’s vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the main content rail. When there are no active filters, it can collapse to a minimal line height or be hidden to avoid unnecessary blank space, allowing the list to sit slightly closer to the filter band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline templates list occupies the central portion of the page and is the primary content block of the layout. It begins approximately 32–48px below the applied-filters-summary and stretches down the page as a vertical stack of cards within the centered content rail. Each card spans nearly the full width of the rail (leaving 24px internal horizontal padding so content never touches the edges) and features a prominent image area at the top—ideally a tall, portrait-leaning thumbnail or evocative banner—followed by the template name, key metadata (duration, style, guest count), and a subtle call-to-action. Cards have 24–32px internal padding and are separated from one another by 24–32px of vertical space, giving each the feeling of a standalone editorial story. On larger screens, the list remains primarily single-column to match the blog-like feed from the mockup, though the underlying grid is aligned to a 12-column system so that image and text within each card can internally observe a consistent structure. On smaller screens, cards simply scale to the narrower column, preserving spacing and typography. The entire list together takes up the majority of the vertical space between the filter band and the pagination, reinforcing its role as the main area of engagement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content rail. If infinite scrolling or lazy loading is used, new cards appear appended at the bottom of this vertical stack while preserving the established spacing and editorial rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls are positioned directly beneath the timeline-templates-list, aligned to the center of the same content rail. They are separated from the last card by a generous vertical margin of 48–64px, creating a clear transition between the content and navigation. The pagination row itself is compact, occupying about 10–15% of the vertical space visible at the bottom of a typical viewport. Page numbers or next/previous buttons are horizontally centered, with 12–16px gaps between elements and 12px vertical padding around clickable targets to keep them touch-friendly yet visually delicate. Surrounding white space above and below ensures that the pagination feels like a calm endpoint rather than a crowded control strip. Beneath the pagination, an additional 64px or more of bottom padding keeps the page feeling open and prevents controls from sticking to the browser edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and part of the normal document flow, appearing only when the list has multiple pages. It does not stick to the bottom of the viewport; users scroll naturally from cards down to the pagination.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state display appears in place of the timeline-templates-list (and effectively pushes pagination-controls out of view) whenever filters yield no results. It occupies the same central column and is vertically centered within the portion of the page that would normally contain the list. There is at least 48–64px of clear space below the applied-filters-summary before the empty state begins, allowing it to feel like a distinct, calm feature. The content is center-aligned: a small, elegant illustrative icon or soft graphic sits at the top, followed by a concise heading and a short explanatory sentence, all constrained to roughly 50–60% of the column width for readability. A primary action—such as a button to clear filters or broaden the search—appears beneath with comfortable 16–24px spacing. The whole block is surrounded by generous white space (minimum 64px padding above and below the main message) to maintain the airy, editorial tone and avoid any sense of crowding, even in a no-results scenario.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the visual space of the timeline-templates-list (and hides pagination-controls). It is static within the flow, not overlaying other components, and scrolls normally if content height exceeds the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader represents the loading state for the timelines list region. Visually, it manifests within the bounds of the main content rail where the list or empty state would normally appear, starting roughly 32–48px below the applied-filters-summary. A centered spinner or subtle skeleton cards appear, aligned to the center of the column, with ample white space around them to avoid adding visual noise. If implemented as an overlay, a semi-transparent, soft white layer covers just the list area (from the top edge where cards would begin down to just above the pagination zone), slightly dimming any underlying content while keeping the surrounding filter controls and header areas fully visible and interactive contextually or optionally disabled. The loading indicator itself is positioned slightly above the vertical center of this region (around 25–35% down from its top) so it’s immediately noticeable without feeling aggressively central.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the timeline-templates-list area while data is being fetched. It is constrained to the main content rail and should not block the filter-controls or sort-controls unless explicitly desired, ensuring that the overall layout and vertical structure remain intact during loading.

name: Timeline Template Detail
title: Timeline Template Detail
description: Displays the full schedule for a selected wedding day timeline template and allows saving it to the Planning Board or exporting/printing it.

core_features: Load and display a specific wedding day timeline template using the timelineTemplateId URL parameter and the getTimelineTemplateDetail interface.
Show the full schedule for the selected timeline template, including all timeline blocks from the TimelineTemplate.schedule field.
Provide a primary action to save the current timeline template to the user’s Planning Board via the saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard interface with optional notes.
After a successful save, show a link that navigates to planning_board.html with the saved PlanningBoardItem highlighted using the highlightItemId URL parameter.
Offer printing and export options that call the exportTimelineTemplate interface with a user-selected format and use the returned export_data for offline use.
user_workflows:
View a timeline template in detail:
1. User navigates from timelines.html or planning_board.html to timeline.html with a timelineTemplateId URL parameter.
2. On page load, the page reads timelineTemplateId from the URL query string.
3. The page calls getTimelineTemplateDetail(timelineTemplateId) and receives a timeline_template object.
4. The page displays the timeline_template title, description, metadata (such as guest_count_label, season, location_type, length_hours, ceremony_time when available), and a structured schedule view based on timeline_template.schedule.
5. The Save and Export/Print actions become available once the timeline_template is successfully loaded.

Save the timeline to the Planning Board:
1. User clicks the primary "Save this timeline" action.
2. A save interaction (inline or modal) lets the user optionally enter notes associated with the saved PlanningBoardItem.
3. On submission, the page calls saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard with parameters timelineTemplateId (from URL or current template) and notes (from user input).
4. While the save is in progress, the save action is disabled or shows a loading state.
5. If the call returns success: true and a planning_board_item object, the page stores planning_board_item.id in local page state, shows a confirmation message, and reveals a "View on Planning Board" navigation link.
6. If the user clicks the "View on Planning Board" link, the browser navigates to planning_board.html with highlightItemId set to planning_board_item.id in the URL query string.
7. If the save call fails (success: false or network error), an error state/message is shown and the user can retry.

Export or print the timeline template:
1. User opens export/print controls associated with the current timeline template.
2. User selects a desired export format (for example, a print-optimized view or other supported formats provided as UI options) without hardcoded assumptions in the logic.
3. On confirming the export, the page calls exportTimelineTemplate with parameters timelineTemplateId (current template) and format (the format selected by the user in the UI control).
4. The page receives an object containing format and export_data with title and schedule derived from the backend.
5. The page uses the returned export_data to build an exportable representation (for example, a print layout or downloadable content) and then triggers the appropriate browser-level print or download behavior.
6. If the export call fails, the page displays an error state and allows the user to reattempt export.

Switching between different timeline templates:
1. User may navigate to another timeline template (e.g., via internal links) by changing the timelineTemplateId parameter in the URL.
2. When the URL changes and a new timelineTemplateId is detected, the page clears the previous timeline state (including any prior save/export transient messaging) and calls getTimelineTemplateDetail with the new ID.
3. The page updates the displayed timeline details and schedules to match the newly loaded template.
4. The save/export controls always operate on the currently loaded timelineTemplateId.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- Trigger: Page load or URL change that includes a timelineTemplateId parameter.
- Behavior: Call getTimelineTemplateDetail(timelineTemplateId) and populate the page with the returned timeline_template data.
- Error handling: If timelineTemplateId is missing or the interface call fails, display an error state and do not show save/export actions until resolved.

"Save this timeline" button click:
- Trigger: User clicks the primary save action.
- Behavior: Open or focus the save interaction, allowing the user to add notes. On form submission, call saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard(timelineTemplateId, notes).
- States: idle, saving (disabled button/loading indicator), success (confirmation plus planning board link), error (retry option).

Save notes input:
- Trigger: User types into a notes field associated with saving the timeline.
- Behavior: Update in-memory notes state. This value is passed as the notes parameter to saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard when the user submits.

"View on Planning Board" link click after save:
- Trigger: Link appears only after saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard returns success with a planning_board_item.
- Behavior: Navigate to planning_board.html with the URL parameter highlightItemId set to planning_board_item.id.
- Result: The Planning Board page can use highlightItemId to focus or emphasize the corresponding item.

Export format selection:
- Trigger: User selects a format from an export/print control (e.g., a dropdown or radio group). The UI options are defined by the front-end without hardcoded assumptions about backend content.
- Behavior: Store the selected format in the component’s state to be used as the format parameter in exportTimelineTemplate.

Export/Print action submit:
- Trigger: User clicks an export or print confirmation button.
- Behavior: Call exportTimelineTemplate(timelineTemplateId, format) using the current template ID and user-selected format.
- After success: Use export_data.title and export_data.schedule to construct a printable/exportable view and then trigger printing or downloading.
- Error handling: Show a message and allow retry if exportTimelineTemplate fails.

Schedule interaction (read-only):
- Trigger: User scrolls through or expands schedule entries.
- Behavior: The schedule view is read-only and reflects timeline_template.schedule exactly as returned by getTimelineTemplateDetail, without local modifications.

Retry loading timeline details:
- Trigger: User clicks a retry control after an error loading timeline details.
- Behavior: Re-read timelineTemplateId from the URL and re-call getTimelineTemplateDetail(timelineTemplateId).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the timelineTemplateId URL parameter. On initial load, the page reads timelineTemplateId from the query string (e.g., timeline.html?timelineTemplateId=<value>). If timelineTemplateId is present, the page calls getTimelineTemplateDetail(timelineTemplateId) and stores the returned timeline_template in page state. All display components (title, metadata, schedule, save/export controls) bind to this timeline_template. If the URL parameter changes (for example, via internal navigation or browser navigation), the page detects the new timelineTemplateId, clears the previous timeline-specific state (including any prior save or export success/error messaging), and calls getTimelineTemplateDetail for the new ID. The save operation uses the same timelineTemplateId from the URL or current timeline_template.id; after saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard succeeds, the returned planning_board_item is stored in transient page state solely for this page instance. That ID is then used to construct a navigation URL to planning_board.html with highlightItemId set to planning_board_item.id; the actual navigation passes this via the browser’s address bar without using localStorage. Export operations likewise depend on the current timelineTemplateId and the user-selected format maintained in component state. No cross-page persistent state is stored outside of what is encoded in URL parameters; all long-term persistence (saved Planning Board items, etc.) is handled by the backend via the provided interfaces.

components.functionality: Reads the timelineTemplateId from the URL, calls getTimelineTemplateDetail to retrieve the corresponding TimelineTemplate, and exposes the loaded data and loading/error states to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read timelineTemplateId from URL query string and invoke getTimelineTemplateDetail(timelineTemplateId).
onUrlChange: When timelineTemplateId in the URL changes, clear existing timeline_template state and re-invoke getTimelineTemplateDetail with the new ID.
onLoadSuccess: Store the returned timeline_template object in component/page state and notify dependent components to render.
onLoadError: Store error information and expose an error state plus a retry handler.
onRetryClick: Re-read timelineTemplateId from the URL and call getTimelineTemplateDetail again.

components.functionality: Displays key information about the loaded timeline template, such as title, description, suggested guest range, season, location_type, length_hours, and ceremony_time when available.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When the bound timeline_template changes, update displayed fields (title, description, metadata) to match the latest data.
onMissingData: If no timeline_template is available (for example, while loading or on error), display a loading or empty state as directed by parent.

components.functionality: Renders the full wedding day schedule for the current timeline template using the timeline_template.schedule array, showing each time block’s time, label, and description in order.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When timeline_template updates, re-render the schedule using the latest schedule array.
onScroll: Allow scrolling through the schedule without mutating data.
onSectionToggle: If the view groups schedule entries into collapsible sections, toggling sections only affects UI state and does not modify schedule data.

components.functionality: Provides the primary "Save this timeline" action with an optional notes input, calls saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard using the current timelineTemplateId and notes, and handles success/error states.
event_handlers: onClickSaveButton: Open or focus the save interaction and prepare a notes input tied to local component state.
onNotesChange: Update the notes value in component state as the user types.
onSubmitSave: Call saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard with parameters timelineTemplateId (from URL or current timeline_template.id) and notes (from component state), then enter a saving state.

onSaveSuccess: When saveTimelineTemplateToPlanningBoard returns success and a planning_board_item, store planning_board_item in component/page state, switch to a success state, and expose the planning_board_item.id for navigation.
onSaveError: If the save call fails, display an error state and keep the notes so the user can retry.
onDisableWhileSaving: While the save action is in progress, disable repeated submissions until a result is returned.

components.functionality: After a successful save, displays a link that navigates to planning_board.html with the saved PlanningBoardItem highlighted via the highlightItemId URL parameter.
event_handlers: onSaveSuccess: Receive planning_board_item.id from the timeline-save-form component and construct a URL to planning_board.html including highlightItemId set to this ID.
onClick: Navigate the browser to planning_board.html with the constructed query string, without using localStorage.
onVisibilityChange: Only render the link when a PlanningBoardItem from the current save operation is available.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose an export or print format for the current timeline template and triggers exportTimelineTemplate with the selected format and current timelineTemplateId.
event_handlers: onFormatChange: Update the selected format value in component state based on user interaction with the export format control.
onClickExport: Call exportTimelineTemplate with parameters timelineTemplateId (from URL or current timeline_template.id) and format (from component state), then enter an exporting state.
onExportSuccess: Receive the response containing format and export_data (with title and schedule), and forward this data to a print/download handler component.
onExportError: Show an error state and allow the user to retry export.
onDisableWhileExporting: While export is in progress, disable repeated export submissions until a result returns.

components.functionality: Takes export_data returned from exportTimelineTemplate and presents it in a layout suitable for printing or downloading, then triggers the appropriate browser-level action.
event_handlers: onExportDataReceived: When timeline-export-controls provides export_data, generate a structured view using export_data.title and export_data.schedule.
onTriggerPrint: Use the browser’s print mechanism on the export view when the selected format corresponds to printing behavior.
onTriggerDownload: When the chosen format corresponds to a downloadable format, package export_data appropriately and trigger a download via the browser.
onClearExportView: Reset or hide the export layout when the user closes or finishes the export action.

components.functionality: Displays error messages related to loading the timeline template, saving to the Planning Board, or exporting, and allows the user to retry the relevant action.
event_handlers: onLoadError: When timeline-detail-loader reports an error, show a message and a retry control that calls the loader’s onRetryClick handler.
onSaveError: When timeline-save-form reports an error, display the error alongside the save controls and allow the user to re-submit onClickSaveButton or onSubmitSave.
onExportError: When timeline-export-controls reports an error, show a message and provide a control to re-trigger onClickExport.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow. At the top, a hero-like detail header uses the same asymmetric two-column pattern: a left meta/logo-style panel and a right tall typographic block with the template title and labels. Beneath this, the layout recenters into a single, narrow central rail. First comes a lightweight action band (save form + subtle navigation link) that visually echoes the tagline/social band. Below that, the main timeline schedule occupies the key content block as a large, image-card-equivalent. Under the schedule, export controls are arranged as a centered horizontal row, mirroring the social icon bar, followed by an optional export/print preview panel that behaves like a secondary content card. Data loader and error handler sit on top of or in place of the main content area as states rather than permanent structural elements.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow. At the top, a hero-like detail header uses the same asymmetric two-column pattern: a left meta/logo-style panel and a right tall typographic block with the template title and labels. Beneath this, the layout recenters into a single, narrow central rail. First comes a lightweight action band (save form + subtle navigation link) that visually echoes the tagline/social band. Below that, the main timeline schedule occupies the key content block as a large, image-card-equivalent. Under the schedule, export controls are arranged as a centered horizontal row, mirroring the social icon bar, followed by an optional export/print preview panel that behaves like a secondary content card. Data loader and error handler sit on top of or in place of the main content area as states rather than permanent structural elements.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero/detail header occupies roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height with generous top and side padding (48–64px) inside a max-width central container. The left hero sub-panel takes about 40–45% of the content width; the right title block takes 55–60%, matching the asymmetric grid logic. Below, the central column is constrained (roughly 960–1120px max width) with wide lateral gutters (~80–120px) on large screens. Vertical spacing between bands (header, save band, schedule, export controls, export preview) is generous at 48–64px. Cards and functional panels use 24–32px internal padding. Export controls and navigation link occupy a relatively shallow vertical space, while the schedule and optional print/download view extend vertically as needed, similar to tall editorial cards.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium. Each band is clearly separated with ample white space. The hero area uses large typography and open line spacing. The save form band and export controls are visually light, with minimal elements centered and surrounded by whitespace. The schedule view is more information-dense but still structured with generous row height and padding so time slots are easy to scan. Export previews and error states are presented in spacious cards with centered content and comfortable margins. No component appears cramped; spacing follows an 8px scale with typical paddings of 16–32px inside containers.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the bold timeline title in the right hero column, then drifts left to the meta/logo-style panel, creating the same subtle reverse Z-pattern. From there, the gaze naturally moves downward to the centered save band, where the primary action to save the template is clearly framed. Next, attention flows straight down into the main schedule card as the primary detailed content. After reading or scanning the schedule, the eye continues to the centered export controls row, and then, if active, into the print/download preview area. Secondary elements like the Planning Board navigation link are placed near the central rail but with lighter styling so they are discoverable without competing for attention. Loader and error states temporarily become the focal points by overlaying or replacing the main content while preserving the overall structural hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: The Timeline Template Detail page reads like a modern editorial feature within a wedding blog. At the top, a spacious hero band stretches across the page, with a soft, square-ish meta panel on the left and a tall, light-gray typographic block on the right that announces the timeline template in large, confident type. Wide white margins frame this central composition. As you scroll just a little, the content recenters into a single vertical rail: a fine horizontal rule and a short, centered save band introduce the key action, followed by a generous, card-like schedule view that lists the day’s events in a calm, readable rhythm. Beneath the schedule, a thin line of export controls sits in the middle like a row of elegant icons, with an optional print/download preview panel appearing below as another airy card when needed. All elements are vertically stacked with consistent, generous spacing, so the page feels like a curated layout in a wedding magazine—light, romantic, and easy to follow—while still offering clear, functional controls for planning and exporting the timeline.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline-detail loader appears as a full-viewport, soft overlay that sits on top of all page content when data is being fetched. It covers the hero, central rail, and any underlying elements with a semi-opaque white veil (around 80–90% opacity) to maintain the airy feel while dimming the background. At the exact center of the viewport—both vertically and horizontally—a small, circular loading indicator and a single line of light, uppercase text (e.g., “LOADING TIMELINE…”) are stacked with 16px spacing, matching the minimalist aesthetic. The central loader block is relatively compact (about 240–320px wide) with generous internal padding (24–32px) and no heavy borders, ensuring it feels like a subtle, editorial interstitial rather than a harsh system dialog. On all screen sizes, this loader is fixed in place relative to the viewport and remains centered as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, centered overlay that covers the entire viewport and visually masks the underlying layout while allowing its structure to faintly show through; appears only during loading states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline-header occupies the topmost content band and is designed as an asymmetric hero. It sits within a max-width container (approximately 960–1120px) centered on the page, with very generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) and wide left/right gutters (80–120px) on large screens. Inside this container, the content is split into two columns on a 12-column grid: the left panel spans roughly 5 columns (about 40–45% of the content width), and the right panel spans about 7 columns (55–60%). The left panel forms a softly prominent, roughly square block—with a warm, muted background tone—containing the circular wedding mark or timeline icon and key meta details (e.g., day length, wedding style) centered or slightly top-left aligned, with 24–32px padding. The right panel is a tall, vertical light-gray rectangle that begins slightly below the top of the left panel and extends down to create a layered editorial feel. Within this right block, the small uppercase label (e.g., “TIMELINE TEMPLATE”) is aligned to the top-right, spaced 16px from the edges. Below, the main template title (e.g., “Full-Day Wedding Timeline”) is set in large, bold type taking up most of the width, with generous line-height and about 16–24px margin below. A softer, peach-accent subtitle or summary line runs beneath the title, aligned left, with ample breathing room. Overall, the header visually occupies around 40–50% of the initial viewport height. On tablet and mobile, this layout collapses into a single column: the left meta block sits on top, full width, with the right typographic block stacked below, also full width, maintaining 24–32px padding and 24–40px vertical gaps between elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-of-page hero section following the normal document flow; the internal two-column layout responds to screen size, collapsing into a stacked single column on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline-schedule-view is the main content block and appears below the save/action band, aligned to the central column. It is placed approximately 64px beneath the save form band, maintaining the generous vertical rhythm. The schedule view is presented as a large, card-like panel centered on the page, constrained to about 70–80% of the viewport width with a max width of around 960px, and flanked by wide white margins. The card has a clean white background with soft edges (subtle rounding, if used elsewhere in the system) and internal padding of 32px on all sides, echoing the editorial card style. Inside, the schedule is arranged as a vertical list of time blocks: times are aligned along a left column, and event descriptions are aligned to the right of each time, with 16–24px vertical spacing between entries and generous line-height to keep the density comfortable. Section headings within the schedule (e.g., “Morning”, “Ceremony”, “Reception”) are slightly larger and separated from the previous section by 24–32px to maintain a structured, chapter-like feel. The schedule card is tall and may extend beyond the initial viewport; the top of the schedule is visible above the fold, inviting scrolling down the central column. On smaller screens, the schedule card remains centered but spans nearly the full width (minus 16–24px side padding) and the time and description columns stack or compress to maintain readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the central vertical flow; scrollable with the page and expanding vertically based on content length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline-save-form occupies a distinct, lightweight band immediately beneath the hero header, echoing the tagline band in the mockup. It begins about 48–64px below the bottom edge of the timeline-header. The band is centered and constrained to a narrower max width (about 720–840px) within the same central column. At the top of this band, a fine, low-contrast horizontal rule runs across the full width of the content column, visually anchoring the section. Centered over or just beneath this line sits a short line of text such as “Save this timeline to your Planning Board”, set in small uppercase or delicate sans-serif type with generous letter spacing. Below this text, after 16–24px of vertical space, the actual save form elements (e.g., a select/dropdown for choosing a board and a primary button) are arranged in a single horizontal row aligned to the center on larger screens. The button is slightly more prominent but still minimal, with 12px vertical x 24px horizontal padding. On narrower screens, the form elements stack vertically, with 8–16px gaps, maintaining the centered alignment. The entire band has about 32–40px of vertical padding, ensuring it feels visually light and not like a dense form section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered band in the main vertical flow; the form controls reflow from a single centered row to a stacked arrangement on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planning-board-navigation-link is positioned directly beneath the timeline-save-form within the central column, offset by a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px to keep it visually associated but clearly separate. It is aligned to the left edge of the central content column (not centered), creating a subtle visual contrast with the centered save action above and reinforcing its role as a navigational utility rather than a primary call to action. The link is styled as understated text—possibly small caps or a light sans-serif—with a simple underline or soft accent color. The link occupies minimal vertical space, effectively forming a single line with generous whitespace above and below, so it does not compete with the hero or the schedule. On smaller screens, the link remains left-aligned within the available content width but still sits directly under the save form, preserving the sequential relationship.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline text link within the central flow; no special behavior beyond standard navigation, though it remains anchored in the same relative position regardless of viewport size.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline-export-controls appear directly below the timeline-schedule-view, separated from the schedule card by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to create a clear break and mimic the gap between content cards and social bars in the mockup. This band is centered within the same max-width central column and is visually light. The controls—buttons or icon+label combinations for actions like PDF export, CSV download, or sharing—are arranged in a single horizontal row centered on the page. Each control is given equal spacing (24–32px between items) and uses compact, elegant buttons with 12px vertical x 24px horizontal padding, minimal borders, and subtle pastel accents. The overall row occupies about 50–60% of the central column’s width, leaving airy margins on either side. On smaller screens, the controls wrap to a second line or stack vertically with 8–16px vertical spacing, remaining center-aligned. The band’s top and bottom padding are modest (24–32px) to keep it visually connected to the schedule while still allowing breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered horizontal control row following the schedule; its internal layout is responsive, wrapping or stacking the buttons as the viewport narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline-print-download-view resides beneath the export controls as an optional, secondary content card that appears when a user chooses to preview or adjust print/download settings. When visible, it is placed about 32–48px below the export controls, centered within the same max-width central column and matching the schedule card’s width for visual alignment. The panel uses a very light gray or off-white background to subtly differentiate it from the pure white schedule card, with 24–32px padding inside and softly rounded corners. At the top of this panel, a simple, centered heading (e.g., “Print Preview”) and a short explanatory line of text are stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing. Below, the main preview area might show a scaled-down, portrait-oriented representation of the timeline that is centered horizontally and occupies roughly 60–80% of the panel width, with generous gutters around it to maintain the editorial feel. If configuration controls (e.g., paper size, orientation) exist, they are grouped beneath the preview in a compact row or short stack, aligned center. The card extends vertically as needed but remains visually lighter and slightly less tall than the main schedule view to keep its secondary status clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the vertical flow that appears or collapses based on user interaction with export controls; when present, it pushes content below it down rather than overlaying anything.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline-error-retry component appears in the central column in place of the timeline-schedule-view when there is a loading or data error. The hero header at the top of the page remains visible, preserving context, and the error panel is positioned where the schedule card would normally begin—roughly 64px below the save band. The panel is centered and uses the same width and card style as the schedule view (white background, 24–32px internal padding, subtle corner rounding), ensuring consistency. Inside, a simple error icon or subtle emblem sits at the top center, followed by a short, human-friendly error message with comfortable line spacing, and then a primary “Retry” button centered beneath, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space. The content is center-aligned and occupies only a modest vertical height, with generous whitespace above and below inside the card so the panel feels calm rather than alarming. If additional explanatory text is needed, it can appear in a smaller, lighter type below the main message. On all screen sizes, this card remains centrally located within the main content rail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static replacement for the main schedule content within the central flow; it does not overlay the hero but occupies the content area where the schedule view would normally appear.

name: Seating Layouts List
title: Seating Layouts
description: Page for browsing, filtering, sorting, and paginating seating and floorplan layout templates, and navigating to individual layout details.

core_features: Load available guest count ranges, layout type options, and sort options via getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions and render them as interactive filter and sort controls.
Read current URL parameters (guestCountRangeKey, layoutTypeSlugs, sortBy, page, pageSize) and convert them into the filters, sort_by, page, and page_size arguments for listSeatingLayouts.
Call listSeatingLayouts with the current filter and sort state to retrieve a paginated list of SeatingLayout records and display them as clickable cards with key metadata.
Provide interactive controls to change guest count range and layout type selections; reflect these changes in URL parameters and refresh the list via listSeatingLayouts.
Provide interactive sorting controls (e.g., by usage or downloads) using options from getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions; update sortBy in the URL and re-fetch results via listSeatingLayouts.
Provide pagination controls that update page and pageSize URL parameters and request the corresponding slice of results via listSeatingLayouts.
Allow the user to open a specific seating layout detail by clicking on a layout card or title, navigating to seating_layout.html with seatingLayoutId set to the selected layout’s id.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load
1) On navigation to seating_layouts.html, the page reads URL parameters guestCountRangeKey, layoutTypeSlugs, sortBy, page, and pageSize.
2) The page calls getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions to obtain the available guest count ranges, layout type options, and sort options.
3) The page validates URL parameter values against the options returned by getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions and, where needed, selects appropriate defaults (e.g., the first available option) without hardcoding specific values.
4) Using the resolved filter and sort state, the page constructs the filters and sort_by arguments and calls listSeatingLayouts with page and page_size.
5) The returned SeatingLayout list, total_count, and pagination metadata are rendered as cards and pagination controls.

Workflow: Filter by guest count and layout type
1) The user interacts with the guest count control (e.g., dropdown or list) bound to getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions.guest_count_ranges and selects one range.
2) The selection triggers an onGuestCountChange handler, which updates the guestCountRangeKey parameter in the URL and resets page to the first page.
3) If the user also selects one or more layout types from options provided by getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions.layout_type_options, an onLayoutTypeChange handler updates layoutTypeSlugs in the URL as a comma-separated list of selected slugs and resets page.
4) The URL change causes the page logic to recompute the filters object for listSeatingLayouts and call listSeatingLayouts with the new filters and current sortBy/pageSize.
5) The layouts list and pagination controls update to reflect the filtered results.

Workflow: Change sort order
1) The user opens the sort control populated from getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions.sort_options and chooses a sort option.
2) An onSortChange handler updates sortBy in the URL and resets page to the first page.
3) The page calls listSeatingLayouts with the unchanged filters, updated sort_by, page, and page_size.
4) The list re-renders in the new sort order while preserving the current filters.

Workflow: Paginate through results
1) The user interacts with pagination controls (e.g., next/previous or specific page numbers).
2) An onPageChange handler updates the page parameter in the URL to the selected page index.
3) The page calls listSeatingLayouts with the same filters and sort_by, the new page value, and the current page_size.
4) The list updates to show the corresponding slice of SeatingLayout items; pagination controls reflect the new page and available page range based on total_count.
5) If supported, when the user changes page size, an onPageSizeChange handler updates pageSize in the URL, resets page to the first page, and re-calls listSeatingLayouts.

Workflow: Open a seating layout detail
1) The user clicks on a seating layout card or its title in the list.
2) An onCardClick handler reads the id of the selected SeatingLayout from the current listSeatingLayouts.seating_layouts data.
3) The handler navigates to seating_layout.html, adding seatingLayoutId as a URL parameter set to the selected id.
4) The detail page (seating_layout.html) uses seatingLayoutId to load and display the selected layout.
interactions:
Interaction: Guest count filter selection
- Trigger: User selects a guest count range option.
- Behavior: Update guestCountRangeKey in the URL; reset page to the first page; recompute filters for listSeatingLayouts; call listSeatingLayouts and re-render results.

Interaction: Layout type filter selection
- Trigger: User selects or deselects one or more layout type options.
- Behavior: Serialize selected layout type slugs into a comma-separated value in layoutTypeSlugs; update URL; reset page; call listSeatingLayouts with updated filters; update list and pagination.

Interaction: Clear filters
- Trigger: User clicks a "clear" control for filters (if present).
- Behavior: Remove guestCountRangeKey and layoutTypeSlugs from the URL (or set them to default/empty values based on getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions); reset page; call listSeatingLayouts with no specific filters; re-render list.

Interaction: Change sort option
- Trigger: User changes the sort selection.
- Behavior: Update sortBy in URL to the selected option value; reset page; call listSeatingLayouts with the same filters and new sort_by; re-render list in new order.

Interaction: Pagination controls
- Trigger: User clicks next/previous page or a specific page number.
- Behavior: Update page in URL; call listSeatingLayouts using existing filters and sort_by with the new page; update list and highlight active page.
- Trigger (optional): User changes page size.
- Behavior: Update pageSize in URL; reset page; call listSeatingLayouts; re-render with new page size.

Interaction: Click seating layout card/title
- Trigger: User clicks on any part of a layout card designated as clickable (e.g., image, title).
- Behavior: Read the id of the selected SeatingLayout from the bound data; navigate to seating_layout.html?seatingLayoutId={id}.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses, with no reliance on localStorage. On initialization and on every URL change, the page performs the following state logic: (1) Parse guestCountRangeKey, layoutTypeSlugs, sortBy, page, and pageSize from the current URL. (2) Call getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions to obtain the valid filter and sort options. (3) Validate parsed guestCountRangeKey against the returned guest_count_ranges keys; if invalid or missing, choose a default based on the available options (e.g., the first item) without hardcoding a particular key, or leave it unset to represent "no specific guest count" according to product rules. (4) Parse layoutTypeSlugs as a comma-separated list and validate each slug against layout_type_options; discard invalid slugs and, if none remain, treat as no layout type filter. (5) Validate sortBy against sort_options values; if invalid or missing, choose a default based on the options (for example, the first option). (6) Normalize page and pageSize: convert to numbers, ensure they are within allowed ranges (e.g., page ≥ 1 and pageSize within application-defined limits); if invalid, replace with application defaults defined in configuration rather than hardcoded in the page logic. (7) Construct the filters argument for listSeatingLayouts from the normalized guestCountRangeKey and layoutTypeSlugs, and pass sortBy as sort_by, with page and pageSize as page and page_size. (8) Call listSeatingLayouts and use the returned seating_layouts array, total_count, page, and page_size to render the list and pagination; any change to filters, sorting, or pagination updates the URL parameters first and then triggers this evaluation flow again, ensuring URL and UI remain synchronized.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads and writes URL parameters, orchestrates calls to getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions and listSeatingLayouts, and provides normalized state (current filters, sort, page, pageSize, result set) to child components.
Displays and manages filter controls for guest count range and layout types, using options from getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions and reflecting current selections based on URL state.
Shows available sort options returned by getSeatingLayoutFilterOptions, reflects the current sortBy value from URL state, and allows the user to change the sorting of the seating layout list.
Displays a summary of the current result set, such as the number of layouts found and which filters are active, derived from listSeatingLayouts.total_count and the normalized filter state.

Renders the list of SeatingLayout items as cards or rows using the seating_layouts array returned by listSeatingLayouts. Each card shows key attributes (e.g., title, guest_count_label, table_summary) and is clickable to navigate to the detail page.

Provides pagination UI based on current page, page_size, and total_count from listSeatingLayouts and allows the user to change page or page size, updating the URL and triggering a new listSeatingLayouts call.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow: a functional ‘hero’ band for controls (filters) at the top, followed by a centered, light-weight informational band (results summary + sort), and then a vertically scrolling list of large, image-forward layout cards in a central column, ending with centered pagination. The filter bar adopts the asymmetric two-column hero pattern (left/right split) while everything beneath re-centers into a single, strong central rail.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow: a functional ‘hero’ band for controls (filters) at the top, followed by a centered, light-weight informational band (results summary + sort), and then a vertically scrolling list of large, image-forward layout cards in a central column, ending with centered pagination. The filter bar adopts the asymmetric two-column hero pattern (left/right split) while everything beneath re-centers into a single, strong central rail.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The filter bar occupies the upper ~35–40% of the initial viewport, with a generous padded panel that visually acts as the hero. Beneath it, the combined results-summary + sort band uses about 10–15% of the visible height but remains visually light. The layout list then takes the remaining vertical space with large cards arranged in a low-density grid. Horizontal space is constrained to a central content column (max-width ~1100–1200px) with wide side margins (80–120px on large screens). Vertical spacing between these bands is generous (48–64px), preserving the airy editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium. Controls are grouped but not crowded: filters are given room to breathe within the hero panel, and results/sort elements are minimally styled and spaced. The layout cards use large imagery, substantial white space, and clear separation between rows (24–32px gaps), echoing an editorial magazine spread rather than a utilitarian dashboard. Typography is slightly oversized with generous line-height and letter-spacing for labels and meta information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the filter bar’s structured hero panel at the top, particularly the left/right arrangement which immediately communicates control and organization. From there, attention moves downward to the centered results summary, framed by thin lines acting as a visual anchor. Next, the eye drops to the sort control directly under or near the summary, functioning like the social bar in the mockup—small but noticeable. Finally, the visual focus flows down into the large, image-driven layout cards in the central column and concludes at the centered pagination controls at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a wide, softly framed control ‘hero’ that spans the central content column, with filters arranged in an asymmetric two-column layout that feels organized yet airy. This panel floats in white space with generous top and bottom padding, echoing the original blog hero’s sense of calm structure. Directly beneath, the composition recenters: a slim horizontal band runs across the content column, with a delicately styled results summary line in the middle, flanked by faint rules that visually echo the mockup’s tagline treatment. A small, understated sort dropdown sits just below, centered, acting as a functional counterpart to a social bar. Below this, the main content rail emerges: large, image-driven seating layout cards flow down the page in a spacious grid (typically two columns on desktop, collapsing to one on mobile) with ample white space between them, preserving a refined editorial mood. The page culminates in a centered pagination control at the bottom, given breathing room above and below so it reads as a calm, deliberate end to the scroll rather than a cramped utility strip. Overall, the layout feels like a curated gallery of seating templates presented with the softness and sophistication of a wedding inspiration magazine.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. It lives behind the scenes, managing the synchronization between filter selections, sort choice, pagination state, and the URL query parameters. It conceptually wraps the main content area—from the filter hero at the top through the pagination at the bottom—ensuring that all visible state (current filters, sort order, and active page) is reflected in the URL without introducing any visible UI elements or affecting spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No spatial presence; it does not render in the visual flow and does not influence layout dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned at the top of the Seating Layouts List page’s main content, functioning as a hero-like control band. It sits within the central content column, centered horizontally on the page with wide side gutters of approximately 80–120px on large screens. The filter bar itself is a rectangular panel with a soft, very light neutral background (e.g., a warm light gray) that subtly contrasts with the white page. It uses generous padding: around 32px on all sides, consistent with the hero text block padding from the mockup. Inside, it adopts an asymmetric two-column layout mirroring the original hero: the left column occupies roughly 5/12 of the internal width and the right column about 7/12. In the left column, primary filters (such as capacity, table shape, or layout type) are stacked vertically or grouped into 1–2 rows, left-aligned, with 16–24px vertical spacing between control groups. In the right column, secondary filters (such as style, indoor/outdoor, or venue type) are arranged in a vertically stacked pattern, also left-aligned but visually balanced with the left side so the overall block feels tall and elegant rather than dense. There is a clear vertical rhythm between filter rows, with around 16px of spacing between individual controls and 24px between logical groups. The entire filter panel sits with a generous top margin from any page header (about 64px) and a bottom margin of about 48px before the next band (results summary), reinforcing its role as the visual and functional anchor of the page. On tablet and mobile, this asymmetric layout collapses into a single column: the left (primary) filters appear first, full width, followed by the right (secondary) filters beneath, still within the same padded panel but now stacked. Horizontal padding remains around 24–32px to preserve the spacious, editorial feel even on small screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In normal flow, the filter bar sits at the top of the content area. Optionally, it can become sticky after the user scrolls past its initial position: the panel gently adheres to the top of the viewport (with its height reduced slightly if needed) so filters remain accessible while scrolling the list, without overlapping the central content column margins or breaking the airy feeling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control appears just below the results summary band, occupying a slim, unobtrusive strip in the central column. Vertically, it is placed about 16–24px beneath the results summary line, preserving their visual association as a single conceptual band: ‘what you’re seeing’ (summary) and ‘how it’s ordered’ (sort). Horizontally, the sort dropdown is centered within the content column to echo the mockup’s centered social icon bar. The control itself is relatively compact: a pill- or capsule-shaped select field with around 12px vertical and 24px horizontal internal padding, matching the button padding guidelines. Its label (e.g., ‘Sort by’) uses small uppercase typography with subtle letter spacing, while the chosen sort option uses a slightly larger, but still refined, sans-serif style. The entire sort control spans only as wide as necessary for its label and options—roughly 20–30% of the column width—leaving ample white space to the left and right. On hover and focus, the control uses delicate borders and soft shadows rather than heavy fills, maintaining the minimalist wedding aesthetic. On smaller screens, it remains centered and full-width up to a comfortable max (for example, 280–320px) so it is easy to tap without feeling heavy or dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It flows with the document, moving down naturally as content shifts. If the filter bar is implemented as sticky, the sort control scrolls normally and does not remain fixed; it is intended to be a lightweight, inline element rather than a persistent bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary sits immediately below the filter bar, acting as the page’s ‘tagline’ band. It is centered horizontally within the central column and given breathing room: approximately 48px of space above (from the bottom of the filter bar) and 16–24px below (before the sort control). The summary text (e.g., ‘24 seating layouts for intimate garden weddings’) is placed in the center of a horizontal rule treatment that visually mirrors the mockup’s tagline between two lines. A single, very thin line (1px) runs from the left edge of the central column toward the text, stops a small distance before it; the text sits, then another short line continues from the right side, extending toward the opposite edge. This makes the summary feel like it is embedded in the line, subtly anchoring the center of the page. The text itself uses a refined, slightly letterspaced style—often small caps or light uppercase—to enhance the editorial mood. The total width of the summary text block is moderate (roughly 50–60% of the column width), with the flanking lines filling the remaining space, ensuring the entire band reads as a single horizontal element. On smaller screens, the lines may shorten and the text may wrap to two lines with increased line-height, but the central alignment and line-framing pattern remain intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It remains in normal flow as a static band between the filter hero and the main content list, with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The seating layout list is the main content section and resides directly beneath the sort control. It begins about 48px below the sort dropdown to create a clear visual transition from control band to content gallery. The list occupies the full width of the central content column but is presented as a spacious grid of cards rather than a dense catalog. On large screens, use a two-column grid: each card spans approximately 5–5.5 columns of a 12-column system with a 1–2 column gap between them, leaving generous white space around the cards. Horizontal gaps between columns are about 32px, and vertical gaps between rows are 32–48px, supporting the airy editorial tone. Each card is vertically oriented with a large hero image at the top (often close to a 3:4 or portrait ratio to echo the tall first article image in the mockup), followed by textual details (layout name, key attributes, brief description) with comfortable padding inside the card (24px on all sides). The card edges are clean with subtle shadows or faint borders, ensuring they feel like elegantly framed layout previews rather than heavy tiles.
