All cards align to the same visual column edges, so the grid reads as a cohesive central rail. On medium screens (tablets), the layout remains two-column but the card width adjusts slightly, maintaining at least 24px side gutters. On small screens (phones), the grid collapses to a single column: each card takes the full width of the content area with 24px side padding and 24–32px vertical spacing, becoming scrolling editorial blocks that mirror blog posts. Throughout, the list is designed for comfortable scanning down the page, with typography and imagery sized to feel generous rather than cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The list scrolls naturally as the main content body. It does not fix or float; instead, it extends vertically as pagination pages are loaded or changed, always occupying the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed at the bottom of the seating layout list, clearly separated from the final row of cards to read as a distinct closing section. There is approximately 48–64px of vertical space between the last row of layout cards and the pagination band, echoing the generous section spacing elsewhere on the page. The pagination itself is centered horizontally within the central column and set to a moderate width so it feels balanced and unobtrusive. The controls (previous/next arrows and page numbers) are arranged in a single horizontal row, similar in visual weight to the social icon row in the mockup: compact, with ample space between each element (8–16px) and minimal borders. The current page number may be indicated with a soft pill background or underline, with around 8–12px internal horizontal padding, keeping the look light. The full control bar spans only as wide as needed for the numbers (typically 25–35% of the column width), leaving comfortable white space on both sides. A small amount of padding (16–24px) around the pagination elements ensures touch-friendly targets on mobile while maintaining the refined aesthetic. Below the pagination, an additional 48–64px of bottom padding is maintained before the end of the page or footer, allowing the pagination to breathe and preventing it from feeling cramped against the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Pagination remains in normal flow at the bottom of the list. When the user navigates between pages, the list and pagination simply refresh or update in place within the central column, with the pagination control remaining at the bottom of the visible result set.

name: Seating Layout Detail
title: Seating Layout Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected seating layout and lets the user view a sample seating chart and save the layout to the Planning Board.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a seating layout based on the seatingLayoutId URL parameter using getSeatingLayoutDetail.
Show a detailed seating chart description, including table layout characteristics drawn from the SeatingLayout data returned by getSeatingLayoutDetail.
Provide a "View sample seating chart" action that retrieves a detailed sample chart view using getSeatingLayoutSampleChart and displays the sample_chart_image.
Allow the user to enter optional notes and save the current seating layout to the Planning Board using saveSeatingLayoutToPlanningBoard.
After saving, show a confirmation state with a "View on Planning Board" navigation link that goes to planning_board.html with highlightItemId set to the created PlanningBoardItem.id.
user_workflows:
View seating layout details:
1) User opens seating_layout.html with a seatingLayoutId URL parameter (from seating_layouts.html or planning_board.html).
2) The page reads seatingLayoutId from the URL and calls getSeatingLayoutDetail.
3) While data is loading, a loading state is shown.
4) On success, the page displays the layout title, guest range label, layout type information, and any descriptive fields from the SeatingLayout object.
5) If the interface returns additional table configuration details, they are listed in the seating layout details area.

View sample seating chart:
1) While viewing the seating layout, the user clicks the "View sample seating chart" button.
2) The page calls getSeatingLayoutSampleChart with the same seatingLayoutId.
3) A loading state is shown inside the sample chart viewer component.
4) On success, the component displays the sample_chart_image in an enlarged or focused view.
5) The user can close the sample chart viewer to return to the main layout details view.

Save layout to Planning Board and navigate there:
1) The user clicks a "Save layout" or equivalent button.
2) A small form or inline area appears allowing the user to optionally add notes for this Planning Board item.
3) The user submits the form, which triggers saveSeatingLayoutToPlanningBoard with seatingLayoutId and the entered notes.
4) A saving state is shown until the interface returns.
5) On success, the page stores the returned planning_board_item.id as the createdPlanningBoardItemId in page state.
6) A confirmation message is shown indicating the layout is saved, along with a "View on Planning Board" link.
7) If the user clicks the "View on Planning Board" link, the browser navigates to planning_board.html with the highlightItemId URL parameter set to createdPlanningBoardItemId.

Handle missing or invalid seatingLayoutId:
1) If seatingLayoutId is missing from the URL on initial load, the page shows an error state explaining that a seating layout cannot be displayed without an ID and does not call any interfaces.
2) If getSeatingLayoutDetail returns an error (for example, the ID does not exist), the page shows an error state with a retry action that re-calls getSeatingLayoutDetail using the same seatingLayoutId.
3) The user can navigate back to the source page (e.g., seating_layouts.html or planning_board.html) using their browser navigation or any provided generic navigation links outside this page's core scope.
interactions: Reading URL parameter seatingLayoutId on initial page load to determine which seating layout to fetch.
Automatic invocation of getSeatingLayoutDetail when a valid seatingLayoutId is present.
Display of a loading indicator while waiting for getSeatingLayoutDetail to complete, and an error message with a retry control if it fails.
Clicking the "View sample seating chart" button to open the sample chart viewer and trigger getSeatingLayoutSampleChart.
Display of a loading indicator inside the sample chart viewer while getSeatingLayoutSampleChart is pending, and an error state if it fails.
Closing the sample chart viewer via a close button or overlay click, which hides the viewer without changing the URL.
Clicking the "Save layout" button to reveal or focus a notes input and enable submission to saveSeatingLayoutToPlanningBoard.
Typing into the notes input to update the notes value in page state before submission.
Submitting the save layout form, which triggers saveSeatingLayoutToPlanningBoard with seatingLayoutId and the current notes value.
Display of a saving state (e.g., disabled button or spinner) during the saveSeatingLayoutToPlanningBoard call, and handling success or error results.

On successful save, updating page state to indicate that the layout is saved, storing the returned PlanningBoardItem, and showing a confirmation message and the "View on Planning Board" navigation link.
Clicking the "View on Planning Board" link, which navigates to planning_board.html with highlightItemId set to the saved PlanningBoardItem.id via the URL.
Optional retry interactions for failed data loading or saving calls, reusing the same seatingLayoutId and notes currently in state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven by URL parameters and the results of the assigned interfaces. The primary URL parameter is seatingLayoutId. On initial load, the page reads seatingLayoutId from the URL query string (e.g., seating_layout.html?seatingLayoutId={value}). If seatingLayoutId is present and non-empty, getSeatingLayoutDetail is called with that value. If it is missing or empty, the page enters an 'invalid ID' error state and does not call any interfaces. While getSeatingLayoutDetail is pending, the page shows a loading state. When it resolves successfully, a SeatingLayout object is stored in page state and all detail components bind to this object. If it fails, the page stores an error state and exposes a retry control that calls getSeatingLayoutDetail again using the same seatingLayoutId.

The 'sample chart viewer' state is controlled internally by the page (e.g., open/closed) and does not change the URL. When the viewer is opened by clicking 'View sample seating chart', the page calls getSeatingLayoutSampleChart with the same seatingLayoutId and stores the result (including sample_chart_image) in its local state for display. Errors in this call are handled within the viewer, which can be closed at any time without affecting the main layout detail state.

When the user attempts to save the layout, the page uses the current seatingLayoutId from state and the notes value from the save form. It calls saveSeatingLayoutToPlanningBoard with these values. While pending, the saving state is tracked in page state. On success, the returned PlanningBoardItem is stored in state, including its id. This id becomes createdPlanningBoardItemId for this page, which is used in the 'View on Planning Board' navigation link. That link navigates to planning_board.html with the URL parameter highlightItemId set to createdPlanningBoardItemId. The current page's URL is not required to change when saving; the saved state (including createdPlanningBoardItemId) is maintained only in memory until the user leaves the page.

All behavior that depends on which seating layout is displayed must read from the current seatingLayoutId value. If the user lands on this page from planning_board.html, the source page is expected to include seatingLayoutId in the URL; the Seating Layout Detail page behaves the same regardless of source, using only the URL parameters for its logic.

components.functionality: Reads the seatingLayoutId URL parameter on initial page load, validates its presence, and triggers the initial data load for the seating layout or shows an error state if the parameter is missing or invalid.

Calls getSeatingLayoutDetail using the seatingLayoutId provided by the route initializer, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded SeatingLayout object and any load errors to child components.

Displays key high-level information about the current seating layout such as its title, description, guest count label, and any general table layout summary using the SeatingLayout object provided by the loader.

Shows a structured summary of the seating configuration drawn from the detailed SeatingLayout data returned by getSeatingLayoutDetail, including layout type options and any available information about table shapes, counts, and capacities.
Provides the "View sample seating chart" button that, when clicked, opens the sample chart viewer component and triggers retrieval of the sample chart for the current seatingLayoutId.

When open, calls getSeatingLayoutSampleChart using the current seatingLayoutId, manages loading and error states for the sample chart, and displays the returned sample_chart_image in an enlarged or focused view.
Displays the primary call-to-action button for saving the current seating layout to the Planning Board and reveals or focuses the save layout form when activated.

Collects optional user notes and initiates saving the current seating layout to the Planning Board by calling saveSeatingLayoutToPlanningBoard with seatingLayoutId and the entered notes, while managing its own submitting and error states.

Shows feedback to the user after attempting to save the layout, including success confirmation, error messages, and on success, exposes a 'View on Planning Board' navigation link bound to the returned PlanningBoardItem.id.

Handles navigation to planning_board.html after a successful save by constructing a URL that includes highlightItemId set to the created PlanningBoardItem.id and triggering a browser navigation to that URL.

Centralizes presentation and control for loading and error states related to both layout detail (getSeatingLayoutDetail) and sample chart loading (getSeatingLayoutSampleChart), ensuring consistent feedback and retry behaviors.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the asymmetric hero + centered content rail pattern from the mockup. At the top, a two-column hero introduces the seating layout: a left visual/logo-like panel and a right typographic panel with the layout name and key meta. Below, the layout recenters into a single, narrow content column: first a centered tagline-style band summarizing the layout, then a centered horizontal action bar (view sample chart, save layout), followed by a tall, image-and-text data card presenting table details. Supporting elements (status banner, navigation link) sit within this central column, stacked vertically with generous spacing.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the asymmetric hero + centered content rail pattern from the mockup. At the top, a two-column hero introduces the seating layout: a left visual/logo-like panel and a right typographic panel with the layout name and key meta. Below, the layout recenters into a single, narrow content column: first a centered tagline-style band summarizing the layout, then a centered horizontal action bar (view sample chart, save layout), followed by a tall, image-and-text data card presenting table details. Supporting elements (status banner, navigation link) sit within this central column, stacked vertically with generous spacing.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 40–45% of the initial viewport on desktop, with 40–45% width for the left panel and 55–60% for the right. Page side margins are generous (about 80–120px) to form a broad white frame. Vertical gaps between bands (hero → tagline → action bar → main details → footer/nav) follow a 48–64px rhythm. Internal padding for the hero panels and main data card sits around 24–32px. Modals (sample chart viewer and save form) center in the viewport, floating above with dimmed background and 24–32px internal padding, never touching screen edges on large screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays low to medium. Each major block (hero, tagline band, action bar, main details card, status banners) has clear vertical breathing room and ample internal padding. Text uses comfortable line-height and subtle letter spacing, with headings allowed to run large and airy. Buttons are not crowded; they have space around them and between them. Even in modals, fields and labels are well-spaced to preserve the calm, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: On load, the eye first catches the bold layout name in the right hero panel, then drifts left to the layout’s emblem/thumbnail, echoing the reverse Z-pattern. From there, attention naturally moves down to the centered tagline band, then to the action bar where the primary task (view sample chart) and secondary task (save layout) sit side by side. The gaze then flows vertically into the main details card (table counts, capacities), and finally to any status banner and navigation link. When opened, modals interrupt this flow intentionally, dimming the background and pulling focus into the center of the viewport.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a wide, airy hero band framed by generous left and right margins. On the left, a softly tinted, nearly square panel holds a refined circular mark or simplified seating sketch, echoing the gold logo from the original mockup. On the right, a taller, light-gray vertical panel stretches down, carrying the bold seating layout name, a delicate uppercase label (like “SEATING LAYOUT”), and a muted sublabel describing layout style and guest capacity. This asymmetric hero fills nearly half the first viewport, with plenty of white space above and below the text blocks. Beneath it, the layout recenters: a fine horizontal line runs across the central column, with a one-line, romantic tagline about the layout centered between two dash-like segments. A bit lower sits a horizontal action bar: two elegantly styled buttons, centered on the page, invite the user to view a sample seating chart or save the layout to the Planning Board. Below this light band, the main content rail begins—a tall, card-like block that almost spans the central column, showing key table and seating details with optional supporting imagery or diagrams. Status messages appear within this same column as a soft, pastel banner, and a small, understated link to the Planning Board rests near the bottom as a quiet exit. When the user opens the sample chart or save form, a softly dimmed overlay appears, and a centered modal card floats above the page content, still respecting the roomy, editorial spacing. The entire composition feels modern, minimal, and romantic, with a clear vertical reading flow and one primary decision at each step of the scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not render a visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the page tree, before the hero section. It does not consume layout space or alter spacing; instead, it ensures that when visible components mount, they appear directly in their intended positions (hero at the top, followed by tagline, action bar, and main content).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure component with no footprint in the layout; it does not affect spacing or flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader occupies the same structural space as the hero and main details card, preserving the editorial composition during loading. A full-width, but centrally framed, skeleton layout appears: at the top, a two-column shimmering block mimics the hero—on the left, a square-ish placeholder (about 40% width of the central column, with a 1:1 aspect ratio) and on the right a taller rectangular block (about 60% width) with two or three horizontal bars indicating title and subtext. Below this, after about 48px of vertical space, a narrower centered skeleton bar stands in for the tagline band, and a horizontal pair of pill-shaped skeletons marks the future action buttons. Further down, a tall skeleton card fills the central column (about 60–70% of the page width), hinting at the upcoming seating details. Side margins and vertical gaps remain generous, matching the final layout so that when data arrives, content simply fades into the same positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, occupying the hero and primary content rail region. It scrolls with the page and disappears once data is loaded, yielding its space to the header and details components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The detail header forms the top hero band of the page. On desktop, it spans the full content width between the generous page side margins (about 80–120px from the viewport edges). Vertically, it occupies approximately the top 40–45% of the initial viewport, with 64px padding above and below its internal blocks. Inside, it divides into two asymmetric columns aligned to an invisible 12-column grid: the left visual panel uses roughly 5 of 12 columns (about 40–45% of the width) and sits flush left within the content area; the right typographic panel uses about 7 of 12 columns (55–60%) and aligns flush right. The left panel is a near-square card with a soft pastel background and 24–32px internal padding, holding a circular mark or small schematic of the seating layout centered both horizontally and vertically. The right panel is a tall, light-gray card that extends slightly lower than the left, creating a subtle staggered edge. Inside this right panel, the layout name appears as a large, bold title occupying the top half of the block, left-aligned with generous line-height. Above or just to the top-left of the title is a small uppercase label like “SEATING LAYOUT” in thin letterspaced type. Below the title, in a muted peach or taupe tone, sits a single-line descriptor (style, capacity) that runs no wider than two-thirds of the panel. Overall, ample white space surrounds these typographic elements, particularly beneath the sublabel, reinforcing the airy, editorial feel. On tablets and phones, these two panels stack vertically: first the left visual panel at full width, then the right typographic panel beneath it, each maintaining 24px internal padding and 32–48px vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline hero section anchored to the top of the scrollable content. It resizes responsively, collapsing from two columns to a single-column stack on smaller screens while preserving generous padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table details component anchors the main content rail beneath the hero and action area. It is positioned approximately one-and-a-half screen heights down from the very top of the page, following the tagline and action buttons with about 64px of vertical space before it. The component appears as a large, centered card spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, aligned to the same central column as the tagline and buttons. The card has 24–32px internal padding on all sides and a light background (white or very pale neutral) that contrasts gently against the page’s overall background. At its top, a subtle section label (e.g., “TABLE DETAILS”) is centered or left-aligned, set in thin uppercase typography with ample letter spacing. Below that, key data (number of tables, seats per table, total guests) is arranged in a clean, possibly two-column grid or vertical list, each item with generous line spacing. If an illustrative image or mini seating diagram is included, it sits either at the top of the card spanning the full inner width, or on the left half with the numbers on the right, but always staying within the central column so it visually echoes the large imagery of the blog mockup. A comfortable margin (about 48–64px) separates this card from any content below (status banners or navigation). On smaller screens, the card remains full-width within the page margins, with its internal grid collapsing into a single vertical list for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, card-like block within the central column. It scrolls with the page and expands vertically as needed to accommodate details while preserving consistent outer margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This action button is placed in a centered horizontal action bar directly beneath the tagline band and divider, visually echoing the social icon row from the original mockup. After about 32–40px of vertical space below the tagline, the button appears in the middle of the page, within the same central column (about 60–70% of the viewport width). It is styled as the primary CTA and sits on the left side of this small bar, with the secondary ‘save layout’ button to its right. The trigger has generous padding (approximately 12px vertical × 24px horizontal), a slightly rounded rectangular shape, and stands out via a higher-contrast pastel or muted dark tone against the light background. The two buttons are separated by around 16–24px of horizontal space, with enough white space above and below (24–32px) to keep the area light. On narrow screens, the buttons stack vertically, with the sample chart trigger on top, each centered and occupying about 70–80% of the screen width, separated by 12–16px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, part of the action bar in the central column. It does not stick or float; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page. When the related modal opens, this button remains in place in the background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When activated, the sample seating chart viewer appears as a modal overlay centered in the viewport, temporarily overriding the editorial scroll with a focused, gallery-like experience. A semi-transparent, soft-dark backdrop covers the entire viewport from edge to edge, dimming the hero and main content beneath while still hinting at their presence. At the center, a large rectangular modal card (roughly 70% of the viewport width and up to 70% of the viewport height on desktop) showcases the sample seating chart. The chart image or diagram dominates the modal, occupying the majority of its interior space, presented on a white or very light background with 24–32px padding around it. At the top, there may be a slim header row: a small, delicate title like “Sample Seating Chart” left-aligned, and a subtle close icon aligned to the top-right, spaced away from the edges by about 16–24px. Additional notes or legends, if any, appear beneath the chart in smaller type with ample line spacing. On smaller screens, the modal narrows to about 90% of the viewport width, with height adapting to content and enabling vertical scrolling if the chart is tall, while still maintaining at least 16px padding from each screen edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Centered modal overlay that sits above all other content with a dimmed backdrop. It is fixed relative to the viewport, not the page scroll, and typically locks background scrolling while open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save layout trigger shares the same horizontal action bar as the sample chart trigger, reinforcing their pairing as the main interactions. It appears directly to the right of the sample chart button within the central column, centered on the page. Visually, it is treated as a secondary CTA: similar size and padding (around 12px vertical × 24px horizontal) but with a lighter outline style or softer fill color that is less prominent than the primary button. The two buttons together occupy only the central 40–50% of the page width, allowing significant white space on both sides. On small screens, this button stacks beneath the sample chart trigger with a 12–16px vertical gap, both remaining centered and maintaining the airy, spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the central action bar. It scrolls naturally with the rest of the content and, when pressed, causes the save form component to appear (typically as a modal) without altering its own position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save layout form appears as a refined, editorial-style modal when the user chooses to save the layout. Mirroring the chart viewer’s overlay, a soft darkened backdrop covers the full viewport, and a centered card presents the form. The modal card is slightly smaller and more vertically oriented than the chart viewer—around 50–60% of the viewport width and auto height up to about 60% of the viewport height, with 24–32px internal padding. At the top, a left-aligned headline such as “Save to Planning Board” appears in medium-large type, with a subline in smaller, muted text explaining what saving does, both spaced with generous line-height. Below, form fields (e.g., board selection dropdown, optional notes) are stacked vertically in a single column, each with ample vertical spacing (16–24px) between label and field, and between fields. The primary form submit button spans about half the modal width and is left-aligned or centered near the bottom, with a secondary ‘cancel’ or close action in smaller, understated text. The edges of the modal maintain at least 24px distance from content, preserving a calm, roomy aesthetic. On mobile, the form stretches to about 90% of the viewport width, anchored centrally with top and bottom margins so it never touches screen edges, and scrolls within the viewport if content exceeds height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Centered modal overlay with a dim backdrop, fixed to the viewport. It temporarily takes focus and typically prevents background scrolling until the form is submitted or dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status banner appears inline within the central content rail, just below the action bar and above the main table details card, or immediately above the navigation link when confirming an action. It spans the same central column width (about 60–70% of the viewport) and is centered on the page, aligning with the edges of the main details card. The banner is a low-height horizontal strip with 16–24px internal vertical padding and slightly rounded corners, using a soft pastel background to indicate success, warning, or error (e.g., pale green for success, rose or peach for error) while keeping text in a readable, medium-dark tone. The message is left-aligned within the banner, with plenty of whitespace on the right; if a small icon is used, it sits to the left of the text with 8–12px spacing. There is a 24–32px vertical gap above and below the banner, preventing it from feeling cramped between components. On smaller screens, it remains full-width within the mobile margins, preserving the same padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky banner within the central column. It appears and disappears without shifting the fundamental layout structure beyond adding or removing its own vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation link is placed toward the lower portion of the central content column, beneath the main details card and any status banner, acting as a gentle exit point after interacting with the layout. It is centered horizontally and styled as a minimal text link with perhaps a subtle underline or chevron, using a muted but legible color. The link occupies a single line and does not extend beyond the width of the content column. There is generous vertical spacing above it (around 32–48px from the component above) and below it (at least 48px before the page footer or bottom margin), so it feels like a soft, optional next step rather than a primary call to action. On mobile, it remains centered with the same spacious margins, ensuring it doesn’t crowd other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline navigation element at the bottom of the main content rail. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or overlay other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When a loading error occurs, this component temporarily takes over the central content rail, similar to how an article-not-found state would in the blog. It appears in place of the hero’s data-dependent text and main details, centered within the page’s content column. The error handler may display a concise message within a tall, card-like block aligned centrally (about 60–70% of the viewport width), with 24–32px padding and ample empty space around the text. The message is center-aligned both horizontally and vertically within this block, possibly accompanied by a small, delicate icon above it. Above this block, the page can still show the basic hero frame or a simplified header; below it, optional recovery actions (like a ‘Retry’ button) can appear, centered with generous breathing room. The overall presentation maintains the calm, editorial style, avoiding aggressive colors—using instead a soft, muted alert tone. On smaller screens, the error state simply fills the available content width within the margins, maintaining the same padded card feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement state within the central column. While active, it occupies the space that would otherwise hold data-dependent content, and it scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

name: DIY Projects List
title: DIY Projects List
description: Page for browsing DIY & decor project tutorials with filters for time, cost, difficulty, and style, and for opening individual project detail pages.

core_features: Load available filter options for time required, project cost, difficulty, and style tags using the getDIYProjectFilterOptions interface and render them as selectable controls.

Read current filter and pagination state from URL parameters (timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, styleTags, page, pageSize) and convert them into the filters, page, and page_size arguments for the listDIYProjects interface.
List DIY projects returned by listDIYProjects, showing each project’s preview image, title, short description (if available), time label, cost label, difficulty, and style tags.
Allow users to change filters (time, cost, difficulty, style tags) and update the URL parameters to reflect the new state, then reload the projects list via listDIYProjects.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and trigger reloading of projects via listDIYProjects.
Allow users to open a specific DIY project tutorial by clicking on its card, title, or image, navigating to diy_project.html with diyProjectId set to the selected project’s id.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Initial page load without explicit filters: 1) User navigates to diy_projects.html without URL parameters. 2) Page calls getDIYProjectFilterOptions to retrieve available filter options. 3) Page reads URL parameters (none are set) and applies default logic for timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, styleTags, page, and pageSize. 4) Page constructs a filters object from the effective parameter values and calls listDIYProjects with filters, page, and page_size. 5) Projects grid renders the returned projects with associated metadata and paginated controls.

Workflow 2 – Initial page load with filters from URL: 1) User lands on diy_projects.html with some of timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, styleTags, page, and pageSize provided in the URL. 2) Page calls getDIYProjectFilterOptions to get the set of valid options. 3) Page parses the URL parameters and validates them against the options returned by getDIYProjectFilterOptions (where applicable). 4) Page constructs a filters object using only valid parameter values and calls listDIYProjects with filters, page, and page_size based on the URL. 5) Filter controls are initialized to reflect the current URL state and the projects list is rendered accordingly.

Workflow 3 – User changes a single filter (e.g., difficulty): 1) User selects a different difficulty option from the difficulty filter control. 2) Filter component updates the difficulty URL parameter to the selected value, removes it if the user resets that filter, and resets the page parameter to the default first page. 3) The browser navigates to the updated URL or the page logic re-parses the new URL state. 4) Page re-runs the data loading flow: calls getDIYProjectFilterOptions (if not already available in memory), reads URL parameters, constructs filters, and calls listDIYProjects. 5) Projects grid re-renders with filtered results and updated active filter indicators.

Workflow 4 – User applies multiple filters including style tags: 1) User selects or toggles multiple style tags in the style filter control and optionally adjusts timeRequiredKey and costKey. 2) Filter component builds the styleTags URL parameter as a comma-separated list of selected tags and updates timeRequiredKey and costKey parameters as needed, resetting page to the default first page. 3) Page re-reads URL parameters, constructs a filters object (including an array of style tags parsed from styleTags), and calls listDIYProjects. 4) Projects list shows results matching all active filters, and a summary of active filters is updated to match.

Workflow 5 – Pagination: 1) User clicks a pagination control (next page, previous page, or specific page number). 2) Pagination component updates the page URL parameter to the selected page number while preserving all other filter-related parameters (timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, styleTags, pageSize). 3) Page re-parses URL parameters and calls listDIYProjects with updated page and existing filters. 4) Projects grid updates to display the projects for the requested page, and pagination controls update their active state.

Workflow 6 – Change page size: 1) User selects a different page size in a page-size selector. 2) Component updates the pageSize URL parameter and resets page to the default first page, preserving active filter parameters. 3) Page re-reads URL parameters and calls listDIYProjects with the new page_size and page values. 4) Projects grid re-renders with the updated number of projects per page and updated pagination controls.

Workflow 7 – Open project detail: 1) User clicks on a project card, title, or preview image in the projects grid. 2) Projects list component retrieves the id of the clicked DIYProject. 3) Browser navigates to diy_project.html with diyProjectId set to the project id as a URL parameter. 4) The project detail page uses diyProjectId to load and display the selected project (handled on that page).

Workflow 8 – Clear filters and return to unfiltered list: 1) User clicks a 'Clear all filters' control in the active filters summary or filter bar. 2) Filter component removes timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, and styleTags parameters from the URL (and may reset page and pageSize to defaults). 3) Page re-parses the simplified URL state and constructs a filters object with no filter constraints. 4) Page calls listDIYProjects with the unfiltered filters object and current or default pagination. 5) Projects grid re-renders showing an unfiltered or minimally filtered list of projects.
interactions: Interaction – Time filter change: User selects a time option from the time filter control. The component updates the timeRequiredKey URL parameter to the corresponding key, resets page to the default first page, and triggers a reload via listDIYProjects using the new filters.
Interaction – Cost filter change: User selects a cost option. The component updates the costKey URL parameter, resets page, and triggers a projects reload using listDIYProjects.

Interaction – Difficulty filter change: User selects or clears a difficulty level. The component updates or removes the difficulty URL parameter, resets page, and triggers a reload via listDIYProjects.

Interaction – Style tag selection: User toggles one or more style tags (via checkboxes, chips, or similar controls). The component updates the styleTags URL parameter to a comma-separated list of selected tags or removes it when no tags are selected, resets page, and triggers reloading via listDIYProjects.

Interaction – Clear single filter: User removes a specific filter from the active filters summary (e.g., clicking a close icon on an active filter chip). The component updates the relevant URL parameter (e.g., clears difficulty or removes a style tag from styleTags), resets page if appropriate, and reloads projects using listDIYProjects.

Interaction – Clear all filters: User clicks a control to clear all filters. The component removes timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, and styleTags from the URL, may reset page and pageSize to defaults, and reloads projects using listDIYProjects.
Interaction – Pagination click: User clicks a pagination control. The component updates the page URL parameter to the selected page and re-calls listDIYProjects with the current filters and new page.
Interaction – Page size change: User changes the page size selector. The component updates the pageSize URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and reloads projects via listDIYProjects.
Interaction – Project card click: User clicks on a project’s title, image, or card surface. The component reads that project’s id and navigates the browser to diy_project.html?diyProjectId=<id>.

Interaction – Browser navigation (back/forward): User uses the browser’s back or forward buttons. The page initialization logic re-reads the URL parameters, reconstructs filter and pagination state, and calls listDIYProjects accordingly, ensuring the UI reflects the historic state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is entirely derived from URL parameters; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. On every load or navigation, the page reads: (1) timeRequiredKey: if present, it is treated as the selected time filter; if absent or not matching any key returned by getDIYProjectFilterOptions, no time constraint is applied. (2) costKey: if present, it is treated as the selected cost filter; if absent or invalid, no cost constraint is applied. (3) difficulty: if present and matching one of the difficulty values returned by getDIYProjectFilterOptions, it is applied; otherwise it is ignored. (4) styleTags: if present, the string is split on commas to form an array of style tag identifiers. Each token is validated against the style_tags list from getDIYProjectFilterOptions; invalid tags are dropped. If styleTags is absent or becomes empty after validation, no style filter is applied. (5) page: if present and a valid positive number, it is used as the current page; otherwise, a default first page is assumed internally, but this default does not need to be reflected back into the URL unless triggered by an interaction. (6) pageSize: if present and a valid positive number, it is used as the page_size argument to listDIYProjects; otherwise a default page size is used internally. After parsing and validation, the page constructs a filters object that may contain timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, and an array of style tags based on the URL. This filters object, along with page and pageSize, is passed to listDIYProjects. Whenever the user changes filters or pagination via interactions, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (by adjusting the query string), optionally resets page to the first page where relevant, then re-runs the state-parsing and listDIYProjects call. Because state is URL-driven, browser back/forward, direct linking, and sharing URLs preserve the exact filter and pagination state.

components.functionality: On page load or refresh, calls getDIYProjectFilterOptions to retrieve the available time, cost, difficulty, and style tag options, then exposes these options to filter-related components.

Displays selectable controls for time required, cost, difficulty, and style tags based on options from getDIYProjectFilterOptions, synchronizes control states with URL parameters, and triggers updates to the projects list when filters change.

Reads current filter-related URL parameters (timeRequiredKey, costKey, difficulty, styleTags) and options from getDIYProjectFilterOptions to display a summary of active filters as removable chips or labels; updates URL when filters are cleared individually or all at once.

Parses URL parameters into a filters object, page, and page_size, then calls listDIYProjects and passes the resulting projects, pagination information, and total_count to child components for rendering.

Renders a grid or list of DIYProject entries returned from listDIYProjects, including each project’s title, description (if provided), time label, cost label, difficulty, style tags, and preview image, and exposes click actions to open project details.

Displays pagination elements based on current page, page_size, and total_count from listDIYProjects, updates the page URL parameter when a pagination control is used, and triggers list reload via URL-driven state.
Allows the user to choose how many DIY projects are shown per page, updates the pageSize URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers reloading of projects via listDIYProjects.
Handles navigation from a selected DIY project in the projects grid to the project detail page by constructing the target URL diy_project.html with diyProjectId set to the selected project’s id.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow. Above these components, the existing asymmetric hero (logo left, title right), tagline band, and social bar remain unchanged. Directly beneath that, this page’s specific content begins with a centered, lightweight active-filters summary band that visually echoes the tagline treatment (text centered with subtle horizontal rules extending left and right). Below this band, the main content rail appears: a wide, centered column that houses a left-aligned vertical filter sidebar and, to its right, a large image-driven DIY projects grid. At the very bottom of this rail, pagination and page-size controls form a slim utility band. Data-loader and navigation-handler components are non-visual and conceptually wrap or precede their related UI elements.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow. Above these components, the existing asymmetric hero (logo left, title right), tagline band, and social bar remain unchanged. Directly beneath that, this page’s specific content begins with a centered, lightweight active-filters summary band that visually echoes the tagline treatment (text centered with subtle horizontal rules extending left and right). Below this band, the main content rail appears: a wide, centered column that houses a left-aligned vertical filter sidebar and, to its right, a large image-driven DIY projects grid. At the very bottom of this rail, pagination and page-size controls form a slim utility band. Data-loader and navigation-handler components are non-visual and conceptually wrap or precede their related UI elements.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero and global tagline/social area occupy the top ~40–50% of the first viewport. Within the remaining vertical space, the active filters summary band takes a light 10–15% slice with ample white space above and below, acting as a transition. The main content rail beneath occupies the majority of the remaining height: roughly 70–80% of the scrollable area is dedicated to the projects grid, with the filter sidebar taking about 30–35% of the rail width on the left and the grid taking 65–70% on the right. The bottom pagination band is visually slim but spaced generously from the grid (48–64px top margin) to preserve the editorial breathing room. Horizontally, the central content rail spans about 70% of the viewport on large screens, centered, leaving wide side margins that mirror the mockup’s airy editorial framing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low to medium throughout. The filter sidebar is structured but not crowded: filter groups are separated by 24–32px vertical gaps, and the sidebar has 24–32px internal padding and a lot of white space within each filter cluster. The projects grid uses relatively large cards with generous internal padding (24px) and substantial gaps (24–32px) between cards, favoring large imagery and legible titles over packing many items on screen. Pagination and page-size controls are compact but surrounded by 24–32px of vertical breathing room. Overall typography retains ample line spacing and letter spacing, especially in filter labels and small headings, consistent with the soft editorial wedding aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The viewer’s eye first lands on the pre-existing hero title on the right, then drifts left to the logo, then down through the site’s tagline and social icons as in the mockup. As the user scrolls into this page’s specific region, the eye is caught by the centered active-filters summary band, which acts like a secondary tagline for this page context. From there, the eye follows the vertical flow downward into the main content rail, where the large DIY project images on the right dominate attention first. After noticing these, the viewer naturally glances left to the filter sidebar for refinement options. Scrolling continues down through the grid of projects, and the eye finally rests on the pagination row at the bottom, with the page-size selector quietly positioned to the right, mirroring the subtle, utilitarian treatment of navigation elements in editorial layouts.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing asymmetric hero, centered tagline, and social bar, the DIY Projects List page introduces its own content with a slim, elegant band that summarizes the currently active filters. This band sits in the middle of the page, inside the same wide, centered content column as the blog feed in the mockup, and uses a thin horizontal rule extending left and right of the summary text to echo the blog’s tagline styling. Beneath this, the layout opens into a calm, two-column composition contained within the central rail: on the left, a softly framed filter sidebar occupies about one-third of the rail, with vertically stacked filter groups separated by generous white space; on the right, a spacious two-column grid of DIY project cards—with tall, romantic imagery and simple titles—fills the rest of the width. The entire block floats in an airy field of white space, with large vertical gaps between the summary band, the filters/projects region, and the bottom utility row. At the bottom of the grid, pagination controls sit to the left and page-size options to the right, forming a quiet horizontal band that caps the content. On smaller screens, this entire structure gracefully collapses into a single centered column: the active-filters summary at the top, then the filter controls, and finally a single-column stack of large project cards with pagination centered underneath, all maintaining the generous, editorial spacing and romantic, minimalist aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is non-visual and occupies no intentional space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits just above the filter region in the DOM hierarchy, initializing data for the filter sidebar and the active-filters summary before those components render. It should not alter the visual spacing: the vertical gap between the global tagline/social band and the active-filters summary band remains a generous 48–64px regardless of this component’s presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height/width behavioral component that runs before the filter UI; it should not influence layout flow or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar is placed within the main centered content rail, directly below the active-filters summary band and aligned to the left edge of this rail. On large screens, it occupies roughly 30–35% of the rail’s width (equivalent to about 3–4 columns of a 12-column grid within the central content area) and extends vertically from just beneath the summary band down alongside the projects grid. The top edge of the sidebar is positioned about 24–32px below the active-filters summary band to maintain a clear but connected relationship. The sidebar itself sits on a very light, nearly white background (or remains on white but can be distinguished by a subtle border on its right edge), with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. Filter groups (e.g., time, cost, difficulty, style) are stacked vertically, each separated by 24–32px of white space, with their headings in small uppercase text and ample letter spacing, mirroring the mockup’s label treatments. On desktop, the sidebar’s content starts aligned at the top of the first row of project cards and ends above the pagination row, with its right edge about 24px away from the left-most column of project cards to keep the layout airy. On medium and small screens (tablet and mobile), the filter bar drops out of the side-by-side arrangement and becomes a full-width element that sits directly below the active-filters summary band and above the projects grid, retaining its 24–32px internal padding and a 24px vertical gap to the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On large screens, the sidebar can optionally be sticky: once scrolled into view, its top edge sticks approximately 80–96px below the browser’s top (to clear the global header/hero) so filters remain visible while the projects grid scrolls. On smaller screens, it behaves as a standard block element, scrolling with the content with no fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters summary sits immediately below the global tagline/social bar and above the filter sidebar and projects grid, acting as a transitional band specific to this page. It is centered within the same wide content rail as the main content, spanning about 70% of the viewport width and leaving generous side margins on large screens. The component’s vertical height is visually light: at its core is a single line of text (e.g., 'Showing 12 DIY projects • Budget-friendly • Under 2 hours'), set in a refined, small-to-medium-size serif or clean sans serif, centered horizontally. A thin, hairline horizontal rule extends outward from both sides of the text to the edges of the content rail, mimicking the mockup’s tagline between em-dash-like lines. There is 16–24px of vertical padding above and below this text, resulting in a band that is clearly defined but minimal. Above it, a 48–64px gap separates it from the preceding global elements; below it, a 32–40px gap leads into the combined filter sidebar and projects grid section. On mobile, this band remains centered and full-width, with the rules shortening proportionally so that the composition stays balanced on narrower screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard, full-width block within the central content column; scrolls naturally with the page without any sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is invisible and conceptually wraps the projects grid (and related pagination) to manage loading state. In the layout flow, it sits directly around or just above the projects grid area within the main content rail. While data is being fetched, it may temporarily reserve space for a loading indicator within the same footprint the grid would occupy, approximately the full width of the right-hand portion of the rail and the first one to two viewport heights in height. However, its structural presence should not add extra margins: the gap between the filter sidebar/active-filters summary and the projects grid area should remain around 24–32px whether data is loading or not.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual wrapper; any transient loading UI (like a centered spinner or skeleton cards) appears centered within the projects grid area but does not alter the surrounding section spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The DIY projects grid is the main focal area within this page’s specific content. It is positioned inside the central content rail to the right of the filter sidebar, with its left edge roughly 24px away from the sidebar’s right edge on large screens. The grid occupies about 65–70% of the rail’s width (roughly 8–9 columns of a 12-column inner grid), stretching from just below the active-filters summary band down to just above the pagination controls. The top row of cards aligns vertically with the top of the filter sidebar for a clean, shared baseline. On large desktops, the grid presents project cards in two columns to preserve an editorial, image-forward feel: each card spans about half of the grid width, with a 24–32px gutter between columns and 32px vertical spacing between rows. Cards themselves are tall, featuring generous imagery that nearly spans their width, with 24px internal padding around text content beneath the image. The cards sit on white or very light backgrounds, maintaining ample whitespace and soft drop shadows or fine borders, consistent with the romantic wedding aesthetic. On medium screens, the grid may still maintain two columns but with slightly reduced gutters; on tablets and smaller devices, the grid reflows to a single column, with each card filling most of the screen width within the central rail, and maintaining 24–32px vertical spacing between cards. Horizontal alignment remains centered relative to the page, and there is a 32–40px gap between the bottom of the grid and the start of the pagination row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable block; its height grows with the number of cards displayed per page. On content load or pagination changes, the viewport may optionally scroll so that the top of the grid aligns near the top of the window (just below global navigation and summary), but the grid itself does not use fixed or sticky positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls form the left side of the bottom utility band beneath the projects grid. This band is still within the central content rail and is positioned approximately 32–40px below the last row of project cards, creating a clear separation while visually tying it to the grid as part of the same section. On large screens, the pagination controls are left-aligned with the projects grid, meaning their left edge lines up with the left edge of the grid’s first column. The controls (previous/next buttons and numbered pages) are set in a minimal, elegant style with generous horizontal spacing of 12–16px between page numbers and 24px padding inside each button, keeping them airy and unobtrusive. The entire pagination area is relatively shallow—about one line tall with some vertical padding—so it reads as a light, functional strip. On smaller screens, the pagination controls move to a centered alignment within the content rail, with page numbers potentially wrapping or reducing in count, but maintaining comfortable finger-friendly tap targets and vertical padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline/block element within the bottom band of the content rail. It scrolls with the rest of the page and does not stick. On page change, any scroll-to-top behavior is handled by the navigation handler rather than by this component’s layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-size selector is positioned within the same bottom utility band as the pagination controls, aligned to the right side of the central content rail on large screens. Horizontally, it sits on the same baseline as the pagination controls, with its right edge aligning to the right-most edge of the projects grid above, creating a balanced left-right tension across the bottom of the content. A 16–24px horizontal gap separates it from the nearest pagination element if they approach each other on narrower viewports. The selector itself is a compact dropdown or segmented control with subtle styling—light border, soft text, modest arrow icon—so it doesn’t compete with the grid visually. There is 8–12px of internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, consistent with the button sizing guidance, making it feel touch-friendly yet refined. On tablets and small screens, the page-size selector wraps beneath the pagination controls, centered or right-aligned relative to the smaller width, with a 12–16px vertical gap separating the two rows so they remain visually distinct but still connected as a control cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with pagination in the bottom band on large screens; reflows to its own row on narrower screens as the layout stacks. It scrolls normally and has no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation-handler component is non-visual and does not occupy explicit space in the layout. It conceptually wraps or sits immediately alongside the DIY projects grid, intercepting clicks on project cards to navigate to project detail pages and handling URL updates for pagination or filters. Any navigation transitions (such as smooth scrolling back to the top of the grid when the page or filters change) are orchestrated by this component but occur within the space already allocated to the hero, summary band, filter sidebar, and projects grid. No extra padding or margins should be added for it, and it should not alter the positioning of any visible element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral; it should be implemented as a zero-size layer or logical wrapper around interactive elements in the grid. It may trigger scroll position changes (for example, scrolling the viewport so the top of the projects grid aligns near the top after navigation), but it itself remains non-visual and does not participate in layout.

name: DIY Project Detail
title: DIY Project Detail
description: Displays a single DIY wedding project tutorial with materials, step-by-step instructions, and controls to save the project to the user's My Projects list.

core_features: Load and display full DIY project details using getDIYProjectDetail(diyProjectId), including title, description, image, materials, steps, and metadata.
Show key project constraints (time required, cost estimate, difficulty) so users can quickly assess whether the project fits their needs.
Allow the user to add the current DIY project to their My Projects list via saveDIYProjectToMyProjects(diyProjectId).
Provide feedback on save status (success or error) after attempting to add the project to My Projects.
Allow navigation to the My Projects page after saving, passing highlightSavedProjectId in the URL to focus the newly saved project.
user_workflows:
View a DIY project tutorial:
1) User opens diy_project.html with a diyProjectId parameter in the URL.
2) On page load, the page reads diyProjectId from the URL.
3) The page calls getDIYProjectDetail(diyProjectId).
4) While loading, a loading state is shown.
5) On success, project data (DIYProject) is stored in page state.
6) Components bound to DIYProject render the project title, description, image, materials, steps, time, cost, difficulty, and style tags.
7) If the API call fails or returns no project, an error state is shown instead of the tutorial content.

Save a DIY project to My Projects and view it:
1) With project details loaded, the user clicks the “Add to project list” button.
2) The button triggers saveDIYProjectToMyProjects(diyProjectId), using the same diyProjectId from the URL or project.id from DIYProject.
3) While the save request is in progress, the button shows a saving state (e.g., disabled or with a spinner).
4) On success, the returned SavedProject is stored (including its id) along with the success flag.
5) A success message is displayed to confirm the project was saved.
6) The “View My Projects” link becomes enabled or more prominent.
7) When the user clicks “View My Projects”, the page navigates to projects.html with URL parameter highlightSavedProjectId set to the SavedProject.id value returned from saveDIYProjectToMyProjects.

Handle missing or invalid diyProjectId:
1) User opens diy_project.html without diyProjectId or with an invalid value.
2) On page load, the diyProjectId parameter is checked.
3) If missing, the page skips calling getDIYProjectDetail and displays an error state indicating that no project can be shown.
4) If diyProjectId is present but getDIYProjectDetail returns an error or no project, an error state is shown.
5) Navigation links (e.g., back to DIY projects list) remain available so the user can choose another project.

Switch to another DIY project from within this page (if navigation is triggered externally or via internal link):
1) User triggers navigation (e.g., via browser history or an internal link) to diy_project.html with a different diyProjectId.
2) The page detects the URL parameter change.
3) The loader component calls getDIYProjectDetail with the new diyProjectId.
4) The project view updates to show the newly loaded project tutorial.
interactions: Page load / parameter read: On initial load or when the diyProjectId URL parameter changes, the page initiates a data fetch via getDIYProjectDetail(diyProjectId).

Add to project list button click: When the user clicks the “Add to project list” button, saveDIYProjectToMyProjects(diyProjectId) is called and the button enters a saving state until a response is received.
Save success handling: On successful save, the page stores the SavedProject object, updates the save status UI (e.g., changes button label/state), and shows a success message.
Save error handling: On save failure, the page displays an error message and returns the “Add to project list” button to its normal state.

View My Projects navigation: After a successful save, clicking the “View My Projects” link navigates to projects.html with highlightSavedProjectId set to the SavedProject.id returned by saveDIYProjectToMyProjects.
Materials checklist interaction: Users can optionally mark materials as checked/unchecked within the materials list component (purely UI state with no API calls).
Steps interaction: Users can expand/collapse individual steps or mark a step as completed locally to track progress while following instructions (purely UI state).
Dismiss feedback messages: Users can close/dismiss success or error banners related to loading or saving actions.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by the diyProjectId URL parameter and responses from getDIYProjectDetail and saveDIYProjectToMyProjects. On load, the page reads diyProjectId from the URL. If it exists, getDIYProjectDetail(diyProjectId) is called. While awaiting a response, a loading state is active. If the call succeeds, the DIYProject object is stored in state and bound to display components; if it fails or returns no project, an error state is set. The save state is separate: when the user clicks the save button, the page calls saveDIYProjectToMyProjects(diyProjectId). While this request is in progress, a saving flag is set to disable duplicate submissions. On success, the SavedProject returned is stored in state (including its id), a success flag is set, and this id is used when generating the “View My Projects” navigation link as the value of highlightSavedProjectId in the projects.html URL. On failure, an error flag and message are set. The page does not use localStorage; all navigation-related behavior relies on URL parameters (diyProjectId for this page, highlightSavedProjectId for outgoing navigation). Any per-session UI elements like checked materials or step progress are managed in ephemeral UI state only and reset when the diyProjectId URL parameter changes or the page is reloaded.

components.functionality: Reads diyProjectId from the URL, calls getDIYProjectDetail(diyProjectId), manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded DIYProject data to other components.
Displays the main project header information, including project title, optional primary image, and short description, based on the loaded DIYProject.
Shows the project’s key metadata such as time required, cost estimate, and difficulty using values from the DIYProject object, allowing users to quickly assess feasibility.
Renders the list of required materials from DIYProject.materials and allows users to optionally mark each material as checked/unchecked for their own reference while gathering supplies.
Displays the ordered list of project steps from DIYProject.steps, optionally supporting expand/collapse and local completion tracking for each step.

Provides primary actions for the project, including the “Add to project list” button that calls saveDIYProjectToMyProjects(diyProjectId) and a “View My Projects” link that becomes active once the project is successfully saved.
Shows contextual feedback messages related to saving the project (success or error) based on the outcome of saveDIYProjectToMyProjects, and can be dismissed by the user.
Displays an error message and helpful navigation options when diyProjectId is missing or when getDIYProjectDetail fails or returns no data.
Shows a loading indicator while DIYProject data is being fetched via getDIYProjectDetail(diyProjectId).

Provides navigation links to related pages such as the DIY projects list and the My Projects page. For the My Projects link triggered from the actions panel, it appends highlightSavedProjectId using the id from the SavedProject returned by saveDIYProjectToMyProjects.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the editorial, top-to-bottom flow from the mockup. The DIY project header becomes the asymmetric hero at the top: on large screens, the left side shows the primary project visual (or logo/cover) and the right side holds the tall typographic block with the project title and key labels. Below this hero, the layout recenters into a single-column content rail: first a centered meta band (time, difficulty, etc.), then a compact actions bar (save/share). Any save feedback banner appears directly under the actions, followed by vertically stacked content sections: materials list, then the step-by-step instructions as the main content body, and finally the project navigation links at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the editorial, top-to-bottom flow from the mockup. The DIY project header becomes the asymmetric hero at the top: on large screens, the left side shows the primary project visual (or logo/cover) and the right side holds the tall typographic block with the project title and key labels. Below this hero, the layout recenters into a single-column content rail: first a centered meta band (time, difficulty, etc.), then a compact actions bar (save/share). Any save feedback banner appears directly under the actions, followed by vertically stacked content sections: materials list, then the step-by-step instructions as the main content body, and finally the project navigation links at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero (project-header) occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height, with generous padding above and below. Within the hero, the left visual block uses about 40–45% of the central column width and the right title block about 55–60%, following a 12-column grid. Below, the meta band, actions, and feedback banner together use about 10–15% of visible vertical space but remain visually light. The materials and steps sections occupy the remaining vertical space, with the steps list taking the largest share as the main reading area. Horizontal content is constrained to a wide central column (max-width ~1040–1120px) with 80–120px side margins on large screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density remains low to medium, mirroring the mockup: generous whitespace around each section, clear separation between bands, and airy line spacing. Major sections (hero, meta+actions band, materials, steps, navigation) are spaced with 48–64px vertical gaps. Text blocks avoid crowding, with comfortable line-height and slightly increased letter-spacing in uppercase labels. Interactive elements such as buttons and the actions panel are not tightly packed; they use 16–32px internal padding to keep the layout feeling editorial and calm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Attention first lands on the bold project title in the right hero panel, then moves left to the project visual/mark, echoing the mockup’s reverse Z-pattern. From there the eye flows downward to the centered meta band (mirroring the tagline between lines), then to the horizontally arranged actions panel (analogous to the social icon row). Next, the user’s gaze naturally continues down into the main tutorial content: materials list first, then the vertically stacked numbered steps. The page ends with the project navigation links, providing a clear endpoint and path to the next project. Loading and error states appear centered and full-width, temporarily replacing or overlaying this flow while preserving the same sense of spaciousness.

layout.overall_description: The DIY Project Detail page presents as a refined, editorial single-column layout framed by generous side margins. At the very top, a tall hero band stretches nearly full-width within the central content column, split asymmetrically: on the left, a softly framed project image or emblem sits in a roughly square panel; on the right, a light-toned vertical panel holds the project title in large, confident typography, with delicate labels and a short intro beneath. Below this hero, the layout recenters: a thin horizontal rule runs across the column, with the project’s key meta information (time, difficulty, cost) centered between subtle line segments, echoing the tagline treatment from the mockup. Immediately under this, a compact, centered actions bar offers save and share controls in a single row. If the user saves the project, a slim, soft-colored feedback banner appears just below, stretching across the content column without feeling heavy. The main tutorial content flows downward in spacious vertical bands: first a materials section, then a tall, scrollable stack of numbered step cards that read like an editorial feature. At the bottom, a simple, balanced navigation row lets users move to previous or next projects, visually anchored by ample whitespace. Throughout, loading and error states appear centered within this same content frame, maintaining the airy, romantic tone of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is a non-visual wrapper that controls whether the main content or a loading/error state is shown. Spatially, it occupies the entire central content area from just below any global site header down to the typical page bottom. When active with a loading or error state, it effectively replaces the hero and all subsequent sections, treating the whole DIY detail page as a single, vertically centered canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-page controller for its children; when in loading or error mode, it hides the normal layout and yields space to the loading-indicator or load-error-state, both centered within the same content bounds.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project header forms the main hero band at the top of the DIY page. It sits below any global navigation, with a generous top padding of about 64–80px and bottom padding of 48–64px. On large screens, it uses an implicit 12-column grid across the central content width (max ~1040–1120px). The left panel spans roughly 5 of 12 columns (~40–45% width) and holds a near-square project image or emblem, vertically centered within its area, with 24–32px inner padding to create a framed, card-like feel. The right panel spans about 7 of 12 columns (~55–60% width) and is backed by a tall, pale gray or muted pastel block that extends slightly taller than the text to mimic the mockup. Inside this right panel, the project title is right-aligned or flush-left within the panel but visually dominant, using large, bold typography. Above or near the top-right of this panel, a small uppercase label (e.g., ‘DIY PROJECT’) provides context. Beneath the title, a soft peach or muted subheading or short description runs in 1–2 lines with ample line spacing. The two panels share a common baseline and sit side by side with a moderate gutter (~24–32px) between them, surrounded by plenty of white space on all sides. On tablets and mobile screens, this hero collapses into a single column: the image/emblem panel appears first, full-width, followed by the typographic panel stacked beneath with preserved vertical padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow section at the top of the page; participates in the responsive grid by switching from a 2-column asymmetric layout on large screens to a single-column stacked layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project meta summary appears directly below the hero, separated from the project header by about 48px of vertical space. It is centered within the same main content column and uses the full column width but remains visually light. A thin horizontal line spans almost the entire column width. In the middle, the line breaks to allow a central meta block that contains brief, uppercase or small-caps labels such as duration, difficulty level, and estimated cost in a single row or compact wrapped block. This meta text is center-aligned, with increased letter spacing and a light weight to echo the tagline band in the mockup. The horizontal line continues on both sides of the text, creating the sense of the meta info being held between two em-dash-like rules. Vertical padding of 16–24px above and below the text within this band ensures it feels airy, not cramped. On smaller screens, meta items can wrap to two lines while remaining centered, and the horizontal line shortens proportionally to the available width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow band directly beneath the hero; it maintains a full-width horizontal rule with a centered break, adapting gracefully as text wraps on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The materials list sits below the meta+actions cluster as the first substantive content section. It begins roughly 64px beneath the project-actions or save-feedback-banner (if present), preserving strong separation between the intro band and the tutorial body. The section is left-aligned within the central column and spans about 80–90% of that width, leaving subtle side gutters for breathing room. At the top of the section, a simple heading like ‘Materials’ appears in a medium-weight serif or clean sans-serif, aligned left, with generous spacing (16–24px) below it. The list of materials is displayed in a clean, vertically stacked list with 8–16px between items. On large screens, the internal layout can optionally split into two narrow columns for very long lists, but the overall section remains visually one block. The vertical padding inside the section is around 24–32px, and there is clear spacing of 48–56px between the end of the materials list and the beginning of the steps section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow section as part of the main content rail; it stretches with content height and reflows from a possible two-column item layout on desktop to a single column list on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The steps list is the main body of the tutorial and occupies a tall, scrollable vertical region that follows the materials section. It sits about 48–56px below the materials heading and fills the central column, typically spanning 80–90% of its width to keep line lengths comfortable. The section begins with a subtle heading such as ‘Steps’ or an elegant numeral-based title, left-aligned and visually subordinate to the main project title but still clear. Below this heading, each step is rendered as a distinct card-like block in a vertical stack: a large step number (e.g., ‘01’, ‘02’) sits at the top-left of each card, with the step title and descriptive text aligned below or to the right. If step images are present, they occupy the full card width above the text, with 16–24px padding between image and text. Each step card has internal padding of about 24–32px and is separated from the next by 32–40px of whitespace, maintaining the spacious editorial feel. The overall steps section may visually feel like a long article feature, encouraging a linear, downward reading flow. On mobile, step cards become full-width and maintain the same vertical rhythm, with step numbers stacked above titles to preserve clarity in narrower space.
