component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow section that can become quite tall; its internal step cards form a consistent vertical rhythm and simply extend the page length as needed without special sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project actions panel appears directly below the project-meta-summary band, with approximately 32–40px of vertical space separating them. It is centered within the main content column and limited to about 60–70% of the column width on large screens to keep it compact and refined. The primary call-to-action button (e.g., ‘Save to My Projects’) sits at the center or slightly right of center, with generous padding (about 12px vertical × 24px horizontal) and rounded corners that echo the romantic aesthetic. To its left or right, secondary actions such as share icons or a subtle ‘Print’ link appear in a single horizontal row, spaced 16–24px apart. The panel itself may sit on a very faint, almost imperceptible background tint or simply float on white with a small amount of top and bottom padding (16–24px), mirroring the lightness of the social icon row in the mockup. On smaller screens, the primary button spans nearly the full width of the content area, with secondary actions stacking below in a centered row or short column, preserving comfortable touch targets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow component positioned immediately under the meta band; it is not sticky but remains near the top of the page so that the save action is accessible before scrolling deep into the steps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the save-feedback-banner appears inline just beneath the project-actions panel, with only 16–24px of vertical separation so it feels directly connected to the action that triggered it. The banner spans the full width of the main content column but is relatively low in height, similar to a discreet notification strip. It uses a soft pastel background (e.g., pale peach or blush) with rounded corners and 16–24px internal padding, allowing the message text and small icon (if used) to breathe. Text is center-aligned or slightly left-aligned, using a light to regular weight, and does not dominate the page. There is clear vertical spacing of about 32–40px between the bottom of the banner and the top of the materials section, so the banner reads as its own gentle band between meta/actions and the main tutorial. On mobile, the banner remains full-width within the content gutter with the same internal padding for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow element that appears and disappears without overlaying other content; when present, it pushes subsequent content downward rather than covering it, maintaining the calm, editorial layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state takes over the central content area when project data fails to load. It is vertically and horizontally centered within the viewport’s available space under the global header, creating a calm, focused message. The error content is constrained to about 50–60% of the main content column width on large screens, ensuring ample white space around. A simple icon or decorative mark appears at the top, followed by a clear heading (‘Something went wrong’) in medium-large type, and then a shorter explanatory paragraph, all center-aligned with generous line spacing. A retry button, styled similarly to the primary CTA elsewhere but smaller, appears centered below with 24–32px of spacing. The surrounding region remains blank or softly tinted, emphasizing the message without feeling harsh. On smaller screens, the entire block simply shrinks to fit within the narrower column while staying centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed in place of the normal page content when an error occurs; it effectively becomes the main in-flow section under the global header, with all other components hidden while it is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears when the DIY project is being fetched. Visually, it sits in the middle of the available content area, centered both horizontally and vertically within the central column, typically against a clean white background. The indicator itself is compact—a spinner or subtle animated glyph—surrounded by at least 32–40px of empty space on all sides. Optionally, a small line of text (‘Loading project…’) may sit below the spinner, center-aligned and set in light typography to maintain the refined tone. The rest of the page behind it remains minimal or hidden, so the eye focuses on the center of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the page body within the project-detail-loader; it is centrally positioned and non-scrollable while active, giving a clear transitional state before the full layout appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project navigation links sit at the very bottom of the page, beneath the last step in the steps-list. There is a generous top margin of about 64px separating them from the final step card, often with a thin top border line to indicate the end of the current tutorial. The navigation appears as a horizontal strip within the central column, spanning about 70–80% of the column width to keep it visually balanced. On large screens, the ‘Previous Project’ link aligns to the left and the ‘Next Project’ link aligns to the right, creating a subtle tension across the width, with both links styled in light, uppercase or small-caps text, sometimes accompanied by small arrows. The center area can remain empty or hold a small ‘Back to all projects’ link. The entire strip is low-density, with 16–24px vertical padding and ample space around the link text. On smaller screens, the links can stack vertically, centered and separated by 8–12px, while retaining the same minimalist styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow footer-like section at the end of the page’s main content; it naturally appears as the final element in the scroll, with no sticky or fixed behavior.

name: Color Palettes
title: Color Palettes
description: Page for browsing wedding color palettes, filtering them by tags and palette type, and adding selected palettes to user moodboards.

core_features: Load available palette tags and palette types using getColorPaletteFilterOptions and expose them as filter controls.
List color palettes using listColorPalettes with filters derived from the URL parameters (tags, paletteType) and pagination parameters (page, pageSize).
Allow users to adjust tag and palette type filters; updates are reflected in the URL parameters and trigger reloading of the palette list.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and reload palettes via listColorPalettes.
Display a global "Create New Moodboard" action that collects a name (and optional description) and calls createMoodboard.
Load the user’s existing moodboards using listMoodboards for use in add-to-moodboard workflows.
Allow users to add a palette to an existing or newly created moodboard using addPaletteToMoodboard from each palette card.
Provide navigation to moodboards.html and, immediately after creating a moodboard, to moodboard.html with the new moodboardId.
user_workflows:
Browse and filter color palettes:
1. User lands on color_palettes.html.
2. Page-controller reads URL parameters tags, paletteType, page, and pageSize (if present).
3. Page-controller calls getColorPaletteFilterOptions and uses the response to populate filter controls (tags list and palette type options).
4. Page-controller calls listColorPalettes with a filters object constructed from URL parameters and with page and page_size derived from page and pageSize.
5. Palette gallery displays the returned palettes along with pagination based on total_count, page, and page_size.
6. If the user changes tag or palette type filters, the filter component updates the URL parameters accordingly (including resetting page to the first page), which triggers the page-controller to call listColorPalettes again with the new filters.

Create a new moodboard from the page-wide action:
1. User clicks the page-level "Create New Moodboard" button.
2. A moodboard-create modal opens, prompting for a moodboard name (and optionally description).
3. On submit, the component calls createMoodboard with the entered name and description.
4. On success, the modal shows a confirmation state and a "View moodboard" link.
5. If the user clicks "View moodboard", the component navigates to moodboard.html with moodboardId set to the id of the created moodboard.
6. If the user closes the modal instead, they remain on color_palettes.html; the listMoodboards data may be refreshed to include the newly created moodboard.

Add a palette to an existing moodboard:
1. User finds a palette in the gallery and clicks its "Add to moodboard" action.
2. The add-to-moodboard modal opens and stores the paletteId in its internal state.
3. If moodboards have not been loaded yet or are stale, the page-controller calls listMoodboards and passes the results to the modal.
4. The user selects one moodboard from the list in the modal.
5. The user clicks the confirm/add button, and the modal calls addPaletteToMoodboard with the selected moodboardId and the stored paletteId.
6. On success, a notification is shown (e.g., “Palette added to moodboard”), and the modal closes or stays open according to the design. The moodboard data in memory may be updated with the returned moodboard object.

Create a new moodboard while adding a palette:
1. User clicks "Add to moodboard" on a palette card.
2. The add-to-moodboard modal opens with the paletteId stored.
3. In the modal, the user chooses an option to create a new moodboard instead of selecting an existing one.
4. The modal either reveals an inline form or opens the moodboard-create modal, where the user enters a moodboard name (and optional description).
5. On submit, createMoodboard is called. On success, the new moodboard is added to the in-memory moodboards list.
6. Immediately after creation, addPaletteToMoodboard is called with the new moodboardId and the stored paletteId.
7. On success, the UI displays a success notification and may offer a "View moodboard" link that navigates to moodboard.html with moodboardId set appropriately.

Navigate to My Moodboards and back:
1. User clicks the "My Moodboards" link in the header.
2. The header-navigation component navigates to moodboards.html (no additional parameters required by this specification).
3. From moodboards.html, if the user navigates back to color_palettes.html (via header or browser navigation), the page re-reads any URL parameters (tags, paletteType, page, pageSize) to restore the same filtered/paginated view when possible.

Share or revisit a filtered palette view:
1. User adjusts tag and palette type filters and pagination; each change updates the URL parameters tags, paletteType, page, and pageSize as needed.
2. User copies the page URL or bookmarks it.
3. When the URL is opened later or on another device, page initialization reads the parameters and reconstructs the same filter and pagination state before calling listColorPalettes, resulting in the same selection of palettes.
interactions: Changing selected tags in the filter bar updates the tags URL parameter (as a delimiter-separated string), resets the page parameter to the first page, and triggers re-fetching palettes via listColorPalettes with the updated tag filter.

Changing the selected palette type in the filter bar updates the paletteType URL parameter, resets the page parameter to the first page, and triggers re-fetching palettes via listColorPalettes with the updated palette type filter.
Clicking a "Clear filters" control removes tags and paletteType from the URL parameters, resets page, and triggers listColorPalettes with an empty or default filters object.

Clicking a pagination control (specific page number, next, previous) updates the page URL parameter while preserving tags, paletteType, and pageSize parameters, then triggers listColorPalettes with the new page value.
Changing the number of palettes per page (if exposed as a control) updates the pageSize URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and triggers listColorPalettes with the new page_size value.
Clicking the global "Create New Moodboard" button opens the moodboard-create modal and focuses the name input field.

Submitting the moodboard-create form calls createMoodboard; on success, the modal shows a confirmation state and optionally offers a "View moodboard" link that navigates to moodboard.html?moodboardId={createdId}. On failure, an error message is displayed with a retry option.

Clicking the "Add to moodboard" action on a palette card opens the add-to-moodboard modal with the paletteId stored in modal state and triggers loading moodboards via listMoodboards if they are not already available.

Selecting a moodboard within the add-to-moodboard modal and confirming the action calls addPaletteToMoodboard with the chosen moodboardId and stored paletteId. On success, the UI shows a success notification. On failure, the modal displays a retryable error state.

Within the add-to-moodboard modal, selecting the option to create a new moodboard opens the moodboard-create flow. After successful creation, the new moodboard is selected automatically and addPaletteToMoodboard is invoked for the current palette.
Clicking a "View moodboard" link shown immediately after creating a moodboard navigates to moodboard.html with moodboardId set to the created moodboard’s id.
Clicking the "My Moodboards" header link navigates to moodboards.html.

Using browser back/forward buttons modifies the URL parameters; the page-controller listens for URL changes and re-applies filters and pagination by calling listColorPalettes with filters derived from the updated URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
On initialization, the page parses URL parameters tags, paletteType, page, and pageSize. Tags are parsed from a single string into an array, using the application’s chosen delimiter rules, and used to populate the currently selected tag state. The paletteType parameter is mapped directly to the current palette type filter state. The page and pageSize parameters are parsed into numbers and used to define the current pagination state; if absent, implementation-defined defaults are used before calling listColorPalettes.

The page-controller component first calls getColorPaletteFilterOptions to obtain available tags and palette_types. These values are used both to render filter controls and to validate/normalize filter state derived from the URL when appropriate. After filters are determined, the page-controller calls listColorPalettes with an object (filters) that includes the active tag list and palette type filter, and with page and page_size parameters taken from state.

Whenever the user changes filters or pagination, the corresponding URL parameters are updated (for example, tags is updated to reflect the chosen tags, paletteType is updated to the selected palette type, page is adjusted for pagination changes, and pageSize is updated when page size is changed). Once the URL is updated, the page-controller re-reads the URL-derived state or uses a centralized state update, then calls listColorPalettes again with the new filters and pagination values.

Moodboard-related state is not stored in the URL for this page. The listMoodboards interface is invoked when needed (for example, when opening the add-to-moodboard modal or after creating a new moodboard) and its results are kept in in-memory page state. The result of createMoodboard updates this in-memory moodboard list and, when the user chooses to view the new moodboard, its id is passed as the moodboardId URL parameter to moodboard.html. The addPaletteToMoodboard call updates the in-memory representation of the moodboard using the returned moodboard object, ensuring subsequent operations reflect the latest palette_ids.

All state synchronization is URL-based for filters and pagination, ensuring that reloading the page, sharing the URL, or using browser navigation reconstructs the same ColorPalette listing via listColorPalettes. Other transient UI state (open modals, selected moodboard in a modal, and notification visibility) is maintained in client state and not encoded in URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL state, data loading, and communication between filter controls, palette gallery, pagination, and moodboard-related modals on color_palettes.html.
data_binding: URL parameters: tags, paletteType, page, pageSize
listColorPalettes response (page, page_size, total_count, palettes: ColorPalette[])
event_handlers: parses URL parameters, calls getColorPaletteFilterOptions, then calls listColorPalettes with derived filters and pagination.
listens for URL changes (including browser back/forward) and re-syncs filters/pagination before calling listColorPalettes.
called by the filter-bar when filters change; updates URL parameters and re-fetches palettes.
called by pagination-controls; updates page and/or pageSize in the URL and re-fetches palettes.
called when a moodboard list is needed (e.g., opening add-to-moodboard modal); triggers listMoodboards if data is not already loaded or needs refresh.
called by moodboard-create-modal; invokes createMoodboard and updates in-memory moodboard list with the result.
called by add-to-moodboard-modal; invokes addPaletteToMoodboard and updates in-memory moodboard data.
handles success notifications for createMoodboard and addPaletteToMoodboard.
handles error notifications and exposes retry mechanisms to child components.

components.functionality: Provides primary navigation links, including a link to the current Color Palettes page and a link to moodboards.html (My Moodboards).
data_binding: Current URL path for active link highlighting

components.functionality: Displays and manages filters for the palette gallery, including tag selection and palette type selection. It reflects current filter state derived from the URL and notifies the page-controller when filters change.
event_handlers: toggles a tag in the selected tags list and calls onFiltersChange on the page-controller.
changes the selected palette type and calls onFiltersChange on the page-controller.
clears all tag and palette type selections and calls onFiltersChange on the page-controller.

components.functionality: Renders the list of color palette cards based on listColorPalettes results. Each card presents the palette name and visual preview and exposes an action to add the palette to a moodboard.
event_handlers: invoked with a paletteId when a user clicks the add-to-moodboard action on a palette card; notifies the page-controller to open add-to-moodboard-modal with this paletteId.

components.functionality: Displays pagination for the palette gallery and allows the user to change pages and optionally the number of palettes shown per page.
event_handlers: called when the user selects a specific page index; passes the new page value to the page-controller’s onPaginationChange.
increments the current page and passes the new value to onPaginationChange.
decrements the current page and passes the new value to onPaginationChange.
updates pageSize selection and passes the new pageSize (and reset page) to onPaginationChange.

components.functionality: Global control that opens the moodboard-create-modal to create a new moodboard independently of any specific palette.
Collects a moodboard name (and optional description) and creates a new moodboard via createMoodboard. Can be invoked either from the global button or from within the add-to-moodboard workflow.
data_binding: createMoodboard.moodboard (for last successful creation, if needed)
Form inputs: moodboard name, moodboard description
event_handlers: initializes the form, optionally with context (e.g., whether called from add-to-moodboard).
validates form input and invokes the page-controller’s onCreateMoodboard. Receives success or error state.
handles successful creation, optionally notifying add-to-moodboard-modal if it is waiting for a new moodboard.
displays error feedback and allows retrying the submission.
navigates to moodboard.html with moodboardId set to the created moodboard’s id.

components.functionality: Allows the user to add a specific palette to an existing moodboard or to a newly created moodboard. It works in coordination with listMoodboards, createMoodboard, and addPaletteToMoodboard.
data_binding: Currently selected moodboardId within the modal
addPaletteToMoodboard.moodboard (for latest updated moodboard after an add)
event_handlers: called with a paletteId when the modal is opened; requests moodboard data via page-controller’s onRequestMoodboards if needed.
sets the current moodboardId selection within the modal.
calls the page-controller’s onAddPaletteToMoodboard with the selected moodboardId and stored paletteId.
handles successful addition, triggers a success notification, and optionally closes the modal.
displays an error message and allows the user to retry.
opens moodboard-create-modal with context indicating that, after creation, the new moodboard should be used for addPaletteToMoodboard.

components.functionality: Shows transient success or error messages for operations such as createMoodboard and addPaletteToMoodboard.
Displays a contextual message when listColorPalettes returns no palettes for the current filters.
data_binding: Current filter state from URL (tags, paletteType)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial sequence: a light, airy top region with brand/navigation, followed by an asymmetric hero for the “Color Palettes” page title, then a centered control band (divider + tagline + filters + primary CTA), and finally a vertically flowing palette gallery with pagination. The hero mirrors the original two-column structure (left visual brand panel, right typographic page header). Below that, the layout recenters into a single, narrow content rail for filters and gallery, preserving the blog-like editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial sequence: a light, airy top region with brand/navigation, followed by an asymmetric hero for the “Color Palettes” page title, then a centered control band (divider + tagline + filters + primary CTA), and finally a vertically flowing palette gallery with pagination. The hero mirrors the original two-column structure (left visual brand panel, right typographic page header). Below that, the layout recenters into a single, narrow content rail for filters and gallery, preserving the blog-like editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero (nav + page header band) visually occupies about the top 40–50% of the first viewport on large screens, with roughly 40–45% width given to the left brand panel and 55–60% to the right page title block. Immediately underneath, a slim horizontal band (around 10–15% of the initial viewport height) contains a centered divider + tagline and the filter row / moodboard CTA. The gallery below fills the remaining vertical space and continues down the page, keeping a generously wide central column (about 70–80% of page width) framed by broad side margins (80–120px on large screens). Vertical gaps between sections (hero, filter band, gallery, pagination) are generous (roughly 48–64px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low-to-medium. Hero text is large and well-spaced, with ample whitespace around the brand mark and title. Filter controls are arranged with breathing room so no chip or dropdown feels cramped. Palette cards maintain 24–32px gaps between them, with comfortable internal padding and generous image space, giving each palette the feeling of an editorial feature rather than a cramped tile. Pagination and toasts are minimal and unobtrusive, keeping the overall layout airy and calm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The primary visual anchor is the bold, right-aligned hero title “COLOR PALETTES” with its supporting subtitle. From there, the eye naturally moves left to the soft, square logo/brand panel, completing the reverse Z-pattern from the mockup. Next, the gaze drops down to the centered horizontal divider and tagline, then along the row of filters (echoing the original social icon bar) and over to the right-aligned ‘Create Moodboard’ button. From there, attention cascades downward into the first row of large palette cards and continues vertically through the gallery to the pagination. When modals appear, they softly override this flow by centering themselves in the viewport, while toasts briefly catch attention near the top center and then recede.

layout.overall_description: The Color Palettes page feels like a refined editorial spread for a wedding magazine. At the very top, a slim navigation bar with the delicate script logo and calm links sits against a clean background. Below it, the hero header stretches across the full width: on the left, a roughly square, softly tinted panel houses the circular gold mark and subtle brand details; on the right, a taller, pale vertical panel frames the large, uppercase “COLOR PALETTES” title and its gentle descriptive copy, surrounded by generous white space. As the hero ends, a thin horizontal line runs across the page, drawing the eye to the center where a short, romantic tagline about discovering wedding color palettes is set in light, elegant type. Directly beneath this, a single horizontal row of filters and tags is centered within a narrow content rail, echoing the social icon bar from the mockup, while a prominent yet graceful ‘Create Moodboard’ button sits aligned to the right edge of this rail. Below this band, the main gallery cascades down the page in a spacious grid of image-led palette cards, aligned to the central column with broad side margins. The cards feel like mini editorial features, each with a generous image, soft labels, and ample room to breathe. At the bottom of the gallery, centered pagination controls conclude the column. When actions occur, modals rise gently to the center of the viewport with plenty of padding and muted overlays, and slim toasts appear near the top, all maintaining the same soft, minimalist aesthetic and preserving the airy, romantic atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the overall canvas and structural grid for the Color Palettes page. It spans the full browser width with a soft, neutral background and centers all main content within a max-width content rail (roughly 1100–1200px). On large screens, this central rail aligns to an implicit 12-column grid, with broad left and right margins (80–120px) so the layout feels spacious. Within this controller, the top region is dedicated to the hero band: immediately below the header navigation, a two-column area uses about 40–45% of the width on the left for the logo/brand panel and 55–60% on the right for the page title block. Beneath the hero, the page controller introduces a full-width horizontal divider line with generous top margin (~48px from the hero) and bottom margin (~24px), followed by a centered, single-line tagline and a bit more space before the filter bar. The main content column (filters, gallery, pagination, empty state) stays centered beneath this divider/tagline band, maintaining consistent side paddings (~32px internal padding on smaller screens) so content feels like a continuous editorial column. On smaller screens, the controller collapses the hero into a single vertical stack (brand panel on top, title block below), and the central rail expands to nearly full width (with ~16–24px side padding) while preserving the generous vertical spacing between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page wrapper that defines the central content rail and vertical stacking of hero, control band, gallery, and pagination; scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation sits at the very top of the page, spanning 100% of the width, and is relatively shallow in height (~64–80px) compared to the hero beneath it. It uses the same wide central content rail as the rest of the page, with the script logo aligned to the far left and a concise set of navigation links aligned to the right, all positioned on a single horizontal baseline. The nav sits on a clean, mostly white or very light background so it feels like part of the editorial masthead rather than a heavy app bar. Below it, a generous vertical gap (~32–48px) creates clear separation before the start of the hero’s asymmetric two-column layout. On scroll, the navigation remains at the top of the document (not fixed), allowing the hero to occupy the upper viewport unobstructed; on longer pages, scrolling returns the user to the nav when they reach the top, reinforcing the blog-like reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the page (non-sticky), always appearing above the hero on initial load; scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The palette-filter-bar is positioned directly beneath the tagline/divider band, forming the functional counterpart to the mockup’s social icon row. It is horizontally centered within the main content rail and constrained to roughly 60–70% of the page width on large screens so it feels like a neat, compact strip rather than stretching edge-to-edge. The filters (e.g., dropdowns for season, style, palette type, and pill-shaped tag chips) are arranged in a single row that runs from left to right within this band, with consistent gaps (~16–24px) between each control, keeping the layout airy. The vertical height of the filter bar is modest, with comfortable top and bottom padding (~16–24px) so controls don’t feel cramped. Above the filter controls, the page-level horizontal divider and tagline (defined by the page controller) occupy the same vertical column, with about 16–24px of whitespace separating them from the filter row, creating a cohesive band. On narrower screens, the filters wrap gracefully into two or more lines, still centered under the tagline, with each row left-aligned within the central column so it’s easy to scan without feeling dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky control band on larger screens: once the user scrolls past the hero, the filter bar pins itself just below the top edge of the viewport, spanning the width of the central column, so filters remain accessible while scrolling the gallery. On small screens, it can either remain sticky or scroll normally depending on available vertical space, but maintains its centered, band-like appearance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The palette-gallery sits directly below the filter band, with a generous vertical gap (~48px) separating it from the filters to signal the transition into content. It occupies the full width of the central content rail (about 70–80% of page width on large screens), with equal left and right margins relative to the page edges. Inside this area, palettes are laid out in a spacious grid that still feels editorial: on large desktops, 3 columns of cards align to the implicit 12-column grid (each card occupying roughly 3–4 grid columns with 24–32px gutters between them); on medium screens, the layout transitions smoothly to 2 columns, and on small screens to a single column. The very first row of cards can feature slightly taller or more image-forward tiles (e.g., one or two cards with portrait-orientation photography) to echo the tall first article image from the mockup and create a strong entry point into the content. Each card has generous internal padding (~24px) around text labels and swatches, with the palette imagery or swatch strip taking up the upper portion of the card, followed by palette name, tags, and subtle action icons (like ‘add to moodboard’) below. Vertical spacing between rows of cards mirrors horizontal spacing, creating a consistent grid rhythm. The entire gallery scrolls vertically as a continuous stream, reinforcing the blog feed feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable page flow; expands vertically as more palettes are loaded or paged through, while maintaining its central alignment and responsive column behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned directly below the palette gallery, separated from the last row of cards by a generous vertical gap (~40–48px) to clearly mark the end of the current set of palettes. They are centered within the same main content rail, occupying roughly the middle 40–50% of the width so they feel compact and elegant. A combination of minimal page numbers, previous/next arrows, and possibly a ‘Showing X–Y of Z palettes’ label is arranged on a single horizontal line with comfortable spacing (~12–16px between page numbers). The controls use light-weight typography and thin outlines to avoid competing visually with the gallery; they serve as a subtle footer to the content column. Additional whitespace (~48px or more) is placed below the pagination to create a soft, open bottom to the page, consistent with the editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the end of the gallery within the main scrolling flow; remains centered and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ‘Create Moodboard’ button is visually tied to the filter band as the primary page-level action. On large screens, it is aligned to the right edge of the central content rail, sharing the same horizontal band as the palette-filter-bar but slightly offset downward or upward (~8–12px) so it doesn’t visually collide with the filters. The filters occupy the center-left of the band, while this button anchors the right, forming a balanced, asymmetric arrangement. The button itself is a pill or softly rounded rectangle with generous padding (~12px vertical × 24px horizontal), set in a soft accent tone that stands out from the muted background but still feels romantic and refined. There is ample whitespace (~24–32px) between the button and the nearest filter control, so the bar doesn’t feel crowded. On tablets, the button can drop to a second line beneath the filters but stays right-aligned within the content rail; on small screens, it shifts below the filters and spans most of the width (around 70–90%), centered horizontally with preserved generous top/bottom padding so it feels like a strong but graceful CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the filter band on large screens. Optionally becomes a subtle sticky/floating CTA on mobile: when the user scrolls beyond the hero, it can dock toward the bottom of the viewport as a compact pill aligned to the right or center, ensuring easy access without obscuring content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The moodboard-create-modal appears as a centered overlay above the rest of the page, dimming the background with a soft, semi-transparent veil that maintains the romantic, low-contrast feel. The modal dialog itself is a vertically oriented card with rounded corners, occupying roughly 30–40% of the viewport width on large screens (max-width around 480–560px) and about 50–60% of the viewport height at most, never touching the edges thanks to generous outer margins (~32–48px on all sides). Inside, content is stacked from top to bottom: a calm heading (“Create a Moodboard”) at the top, followed by a few form fields (name, optional description, privacy, etc.) spaced with 16–24px vertical gaps, and a bottom action area with primary and secondary buttons. Internal padding is generous (~32px all around) so the form feels light and uncluttered. The modal is centered both horizontally and vertically; on smaller screens, it expands to near full width with reduced side padding (~16–24px) and scrolls internally if content exceeds viewport height, while still keeping a visible margin from the screen edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, with a dimmed backdrop that prevents interaction with underlying content until dismissed; the modal itself can scroll internally if needed while the backdrop remains fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-moodboard-modal also appears as a centered overlay, visually similar to the create modal for consistency but slightly more compact since it is selection-focused. It sits above the dimmed page with the same soft background overlay and rounded-corner card. On large screens, it occupies about 30% of the viewport width (max-width around 420–480px) and up to 50% of the viewport height. The content is vertically stacked: a concise title (“Add to Moodboard”), a short explanatory line in light typography, and a scrollable list of existing moodboards. Each moodboard entry is presented as a horizontally aligned row with a name and perhaps a subtle icon or color indicator, spaced evenly with 12–16px vertical gaps and comfortable left/right padding. A primary button (e.g., “Add”) and a secondary action (e.g., “Create New Moodboard”) are placed at the bottom, separated from the list by a small divider or extra spacing. Overall, the modal preserves generous margins and line spacing so the list feels curated and not dense, even if many moodboards are present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, sharing the same dimmed backdrop as the create modal. If the list of moodboards is long, the list area becomes scrollable within the modal while the header and action buttons remain anchored, ensuring the card itself does not exceed the viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The operation-feedback-toast appears as a slim, unobtrusive notification near the top of the viewport, aligned with the central content rail so it feels integrated with the page rather than an app-style banner. On large screens, it is horizontally centered just below the header navigation or just under the sticky filter bar (when present), occupying about 30–40% of the page width with modest padding (~12–16px vertical, ~24px horizontal). The toast card has gently rounded corners and a soft shadow, with a single line of concise feedback text (and optionally a small icon) in a medium-weight type; the text is center-aligned or slightly left-aligned depending on length. There is a comfortable gap (~16px) between the toast and adjacent elements (nav or filter bar) so it has room to breathe. After a short duration, it fades out smoothly. On smaller screens, the toast expands to near full width of the viewport minus side margins (~16px each side) and sits near the top, still maintaining a light, airy presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position within the viewport while visible, anchored to the top center area (just below the header or filter bar), overlaying but not shifting the page layout; automatically disappears after a brief interval or can be dismissed manually.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message occupies the same space where the palette-gallery would normally appear, ensuring users remain oriented within the content column. When there are no palettes (either global empty state or after applying filters), the empty-state content is centered horizontally within the main content rail and vertically positioned to roughly the middle of the visible gallery area—often around the center of the viewport beneath the filters. A delicate illustration or icon (e.g., a faint bouquet or color wheel) sits at the top of the message, followed by a short, empathetic headline and a line or two of explanatory text. A primary action (such as ‘Clear Filters’ or ‘Create Your First Palette’) is centered beneath the text with ample top margin (~24–32px). The entire block is constrained to a narrow width (about 40–50% of the page width on large screens) to maintain a calm, column-like feel, with plenty of white space around it so it reads as a single, composed editorial element rather than a system error. On smaller screens, it scales to nearly full width with comfortable side padding (~16–24px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll flow, replacing the palette-gallery content when empty. It remains centered under the filter band and scrolls normally with the page if the viewport is shorter than the message height.

name: Moodboards List
title: Moodboards
description: Page where users can view, create, rename, and delete their moodboards, and open a specific moodboard for detailed editing.

core_features: Load and display all moodboards for the current user using the listMoodboards interface.
Create a new moodboard by submitting a name (and optional description) via the createMoodboard interface, then injecting the new moodboard into the list.
Rename an existing moodboard inline using the renameMoodboard interface, updating the list without a full page reload.
Delete an existing moodboard using the deleteMoodboard interface, removing it from the list after confirmation.
Open a specific moodboard’s detail view by navigating to moodboard.html with the selected moodboardId URL parameter.
Provide a call-to-action to discover color palettes by navigating to color_palettes.html, supporting the next step of filling moodboards with palettes.
Support URL-driven state for sorting and focusing a moodboard (e.g., which moodboard is highlighted or in rename mode) using query parameters instead of local storage.
user_workflows:
View all moodboards:
1. User navigates to moodboards.html (via header/footer "My Moodboards" or a direct link).
2. On page initialization, the page calls listMoodboards().
3. The returned moodboards array is bound to the moodboard list component.
4. The list is sorted based on the current URL parameter (e.g., ?sort=name, ?sort=created_at, ?sort=updated_at) if present.
5. The user scrolls through the list and sees each moodboard’s name, optional description, thumbnail (if available), number of associated palettes (derived from palette_ids length), and management actions.
6. If there are no moodboards, an empty-state message is shown with a prominent "Create moodboard" action and a secondary CTA to go to color_palettes.html.

Open a moodboard detail view:
1. In the moodboard list, the user clicks on a moodboard card or its name.
2. The click handler reads that moodboard’s id from the bound data.
3. The browser navigates to moodboard.html?moodboardId={id}.
4. The detail page (outside this spec) uses the moodboardId URL parameter to load and display that specific moodboard.

Create a new moodboard:
1. User clicks the "Create moodboard" button on the moodboards page.
2. This opens a create form (inline panel or modal) where the user can enter a required name and an optional description.
3. On form submission, the component validates that a name is provided.
4. If valid, the page calls createMoodboard({ name, description }).
5. On success, the new Moodboard object returned from the API is appended to the in-memory moodboard list.
6. The list re-renders, respecting the current sort URL parameter (e.g., sorting by updated_at may place the new moodboard at the top).
7. The page updates the URL to include highlightMoodboardId={newMoodboard.id} (using history.pushState or similar) so that the new moodboard is visually highlighted and scrolled into view.
8. The create form resets and closes.

Rename an existing moodboard:
1. In the moodboard list, the user clicks a "Rename" action on a specific moodboard card.
2. The component enters rename mode for that moodboard (e.g., replacing the name with an input field) and updates the URL query parameter to renameMoodboardId={id}.
3. The user edits the name in the input field.
4. On rename form submission or blur, the component validates that the new name is not empty.
5. If valid, the page calls renameMoodboard({ moodboardId: id, newName }).
6. On success, the corresponding Moodboard object in the local list is updated with the new name and (optionally) updated_at value from the API response.
7. The list re-renders; if sorting uses name or updated_at, the moodboard may move position.
8. The page removes renameMoodboardId from the URL (or sets it to a different moodboard if that one is still in rename mode).

Delete an existing moodboard:
1. In the moodboard list, the user clicks a "Delete" action on a moodboard card.
2. A confirmation prompt appears, ensuring the user understands the moodboard and its palette associations will be removed.
3. If the user confirms, the page calls deleteMoodboard({ moodboardId: id }).
4. On success (success === true), the moodboard is removed from the local moodboard list and the list re-renders.
5. If the deleted moodboardId is present in any relevant URL params (e.g., highlightMoodboardId or renameMoodboardId), those params are cleared from the URL.
6. If this was the last moodboard, the empty state is shown instead of the list.

Navigate to color palettes from the moodboard hub:
1. The user sees a persistent CTA such as "Find palettes" or "Add palettes" on the moodboards page (available both in empty and non-empty states).
2. On click, the browser navigates to color_palettes.html (no parameters required by this page).
3. The color palettes page (outside this spec) handles further actions like adding palettes to specific moodboards.

Use URL parameters to control page state:
1. User (or other page) navigates to moodboards.html with optional query parameters, for example: moodboards.html?sort=updated_at&highlightMoodboardId={id}.
2. On initialization, the page parses the query parameters.
3. The sort parameter determines how the listMoodboards() results are ordered client-side.
4. If highlightMoodboardId is present, once listMoodboards() resolves, the component attempts to find that moodboard in the loaded array.
5. If found, the list scrolls to that moodboard and applies a transient highlight state.
6. If renameMoodboardId={id} is present, the specified moodboard card directly enters rename mode on initial render.
7. Changes to these states triggered by user actions (e.g., leaving rename mode) update the URL via history.pushState or replaceState, keeping the URL in sync with the current interaction context.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- Event: Page load / route enter for moodboards.html.
- Handler: onInit of the data provider component.
- Action: Call listMoodboards().
- Result: Populate a reactive moodboards collection that is bound to the list component; apply client-side sorting based on URL parameter sort if present.

Click a moodboard card or name:
- Event: onClick on the card container or moodboard name link.
- Handler: onClickOpenMoodboard.
- Action: Read the moodboard id from the bound Moodboard item.
- Navigation: Set window.location to moodboard.html?moodboardId={id}.

Create moodboard button:
- Event: onClick on "Create moodboard" button.
- Handler: onClickOpenCreateForm.
- Action: Toggle visibility of the create-moodboard form component and optionally set a URL parameter such as createMode=true to reflect that the form is open.

Create moodboard form submission:
- Event: onSubmit of the create-moodboard form.
- Handler: onSubmitCreateMoodboard.
- Action: Validate the name field; if valid, call createMoodboard({ name, description }).
- Result: On success, add the returned moodboard to the in-memory list, update the URL with highlightMoodboardId={newId}, close the form, and re-render the list.

Open rename mode:
- Event: onClick on a "Rename" action associated with a moodboard card.
- Handler: onClickRenameMoodboard.
- Action: Switch that card into rename mode (show text input instead of static name); update URL parameter renameMoodboardId={id}.

Rename form change and submission:
- Event: onChange on the rename input.
- Handler: onChangeRenameName.
- Action: Update local pendingName state for that moodboard.
- Event: onSubmit or onBlur of the rename form.
- Handler: onSubmitRenameMoodboard.
- Action: Validate newName; if valid, call renameMoodboard({ moodboardId: id, newName }).
- Result: On success, update the corresponding Moodboard in the list and exit rename mode; remove or update renameMoodboardId in the URL.

Delete moodboard action:
- Event: onClick on a "Delete" action associated with a moodboard card.
- Handler: onClickDeleteMoodboard.
- Action: Show confirmation dialog.
- Event: onConfirm in the dialog.
- Handler: onConfirmDeleteMoodboard.
- Action: Call deleteMoodboard({ moodboardId: id }).
- Result: If success === true, remove the moodboard from the list and clear any related URL parameters (highlightMoodboardId, renameMoodboardId) if they match the deleted id.

Change sort order:
- Event: onChange on a sort dropdown (e.g., options like name, created_at, updated_at).
- Handler: onChangeSort.
- Action: Update the URL parameter sort to the selected value; re-apply client-side sorting to the current moodboards array; re-render the list without re-calling listMoodboards().

Highlight moodboard from URL:
- Event: onLoad completion of listMoodboards.
- Handler: onListLoaded.
- Action: If a highlightMoodboardId URL parameter exists, find the matching item in the loaded list.
- Result: If found, scroll it into view and set a transient highlight state (e.g., for a few seconds) in the list component.

Navigate to color palettes:
- Event: onClick on "Find palettes" / "Add palettes" CTA.
- Handler: onClickGoToPalettes.
- Action: Navigate to color_palettes.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The moodboards.html page uses URL query parameters to manage view and interaction state without relying on localStorage. Supported parameters include: (1) sort: determines client-side sorting of the moodboards after listMoodboards() returns. Expected values might be "name", "created_at", or "updated_at". If omitted or invalid, a default sort (e.g., updated_at) is used. The sort control UI both reads from and updates this parameter via history.pushState/replaceState when the user changes the sort dropdown. (2) highlightMoodboardId: if present, after listMoodboards() completes the page attempts to find the moodboard with this id. If found, the list component scrolls that moodboard into view and applies a transient highlight state. This parameter is commonly applied immediately after creating a moodboard so that the new item is emphasized; once the highlight animation finishes, the parameter can be removed or left as-is without further effect. (3) renameMoodboardId: if present on page load or after navigation/interaction, the moodboard with this id is rendered in rename mode, with an input for editing the name instead of static text. Entering rename mode for a moodboard sets renameMoodboardId to its id; successfully completing or canceling the rename clears this parameter. (4) createMode: an optional parameter that, if true, opens the create-moodboard form on initial load. Toggling the form open/closed updates this parameter to keep the URL in sync. All parameters are read once on initialization and then kept synchronized with UI state whenever the user initiates actions that change sort order, open/close the create form, or enter/exit rename mode. Unknown or unsupported parameters are ignored and do not affect functionality.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling listMoodboards(), stores the returned moodboards array in reactive state, and exposes it to child components. Applies client-side sorting based on the current URL sort parameter and triggers highlight/rename logic once data is loaded.

Displays all moodboards from the data provider as a list of cards. Each card shows the moodboard name, optional description, thumbnail_image (if present), and a derived count of palettes from palette_ids.length. Provides actions to open, rename, and delete each moodboard, and visually handles highlight and rename states based on URL parameters and internal state.

Represents a single moodboard row/card within the list. Binds to one Moodboard instance and manages per-item states such as isHighlighted, isInRenameMode, and pendingNewName. Delegates rename and delete operations to dedicated components, and handles navigation when the item is clicked.

Provides inputs for creating a new moodboard (required name and optional description). Validates inputs on submit, calls createMoodboard with the user-provided values, and on success appends the returned Moodboard to the data provider’s list. Updates the URL to set highlightMoodboardId to the new moodboard’s id and optionally closes itself and clears the createMode parameter.

Handles inline renaming of a single moodboard. When enabled for a given moodboard, replaces the static name with an input field. On submit or blur, validates the newName and calls renameMoodboard with moodboardId and newName. On success, updates the corresponding Moodboard in the list and exits rename mode, also ensuring the URL renameMoodboardId parameter is cleared or updated.

Displays a delete trigger for each moodboard and manages the confirmation flow. When the user confirms deletion, calls deleteMoodboard with the selected moodboardId. On success, removes the Moodboard from the data provider’s list and clears any URL parameters (highlightMoodboardId, renameMoodboardId) that refer to the deleted id.

Observes the moodboards array from the data provider and displays an empty-state message and actions when the array is empty. Provides a primary button to open the moodboard-create-form component and a secondary navigation link to color_palettes.html. When the array is non-empty, this component is hidden.

Allows the user to change the display order of moodboards (e.g., by name, created_at, or updated_at). Reads the current sort value from the URL on initialization, updates the visual selection accordingly, and when changed by the user, updates the URL sort parameter and notifies the data provider to reapply client-side sorting.

Provides a persistent CTA to navigate from the moodboards hub to the color palettes page so users can find palettes to later add to their moodboards. On click, navigates to color_palettes.html without modifying moodboard data.

Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters related to this page, including sort, highlightMoodboardId, renameMoodboardId, and createMode. On page initialization, parses the current URL and exposes the parsed state to other components. When components change interaction state (e.g., entering rename mode, changing sort, opening the create form), they call this router to update the URL via history.pushState or replaceState, ensuring UI state and URL remain synchronized.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same editorial, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. A global asymmetric hero (logo left, page title right) sits at the top. Directly beneath the hero, the global tagline band and social bar re-center the eye. The Moodboards List page-specific content begins below this with a centered utility rail (create form trigger, sort control, and a subtle 'Add palettes' navigation link) at the top of the main content column. Below that, the moodboard list (or empty state) stacks vertically as large, image-led cards aligned to the central content column, echoing the blog feed structure from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same editorial, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. A global asymmetric hero (logo left, page title right) sits at the top. Directly beneath the hero, the global tagline band and social bar re-center the eye. The Moodboards List page-specific content begins below this with a centered utility rail (create form trigger, sort control, and a subtle 'Add palettes' navigation link) at the top of the main content column. Below that, the moodboard list (or empty state) stacks vertically as large, image-led cards aligned to the central content column, echoing the blog feed structure from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On large screens, the hero continues to occupy roughly the top 40–50% of the viewport. The tagline + social bar together occupy another 10–15% but remain visually light. The moodboard-specific area then uses the remaining vertical space. Within this area, a centered main content column spanning about 8 of 12 columns (roughly 60–70% of the page width) is used for the utility rail and the moodboard list/empty state. Side margins are generous (80–120px), with 48–64px vertical spacing between major bands (hero, tagline, social, utility rail, list), and 24–40px spacing between cards, preserving the airy editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains low to medium. Each interaction element (create form, sort control, moodboard cards, empty state) is given generous padding and line spacing. Cards have substantial internal padding (around 24px), large imagery, and clearly separated text and controls, mirroring the spacious blog cards in the mockup. Controls are not crammed together; rename and delete actions are aligned with ample breathing room, likely in a calm, right-side cluster on each card.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow keeps the mockup's sequence: hero title draws attention first, then logo, then the centered tagline and social bar. From there, the eye moves down into the moodboard utility rail (create/sort/Add palettes), which lightly frames actions without overpowering the content. The first moodboard card—ideally featuring a large, portrait-style preview image—becomes the next major focal point. Within each card, attention moves from the image to the moodboard name (editable via inline rename), then to the secondary details and finally to the subtle action cluster with delete and navigation to detailed editing. The vertical reading flow remains strong and uninterrupted.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s asymmetric hero and the centered tagline with social icons, the Moodboards List page resolves into a single, centered content column that feels like a refined editorial rail. At the top of this rail, a slim horizontal utility band stretches across about two-thirds of the page, centered: on the left sits a subtle but primary 'Create new moodboard' control, while the right side balances a light-weight sort control and an understated 'Add palettes' navigation link. Generous white space surrounds this band, separating it clearly from both the social bar above and the list below. The main body consists of tall, image-driven moodboard cards stacked vertically down the page, each aligned to the central column with wide left and right margins and 32–40px gaps between them. Each card leads with a large moodboard preview image that nearly spans the column width, followed by the moodboard name and metadata, and a calm, right-aligned cluster of rename and delete controls plus navigation into the moodboard. When there are no moodboards, this list is replaced by a single, prominent empty-state card centered in the same column, framed by ample negative space. Throughout, the layout preserves the mockup’s modern, romantic editorial feeling: airy spacing, strong vertical rhythm, and a clear hierarchy that gently guides the eye from the hero down into the moodboard management tools without ever feeling dense or utilitarian.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is non-visual and sits logically at the top of the moodboards page’s content tree, just beneath the global hero and before any visible moodboard content. It occupies no explicit space in the layout and does not affect the visual grid; it simply ensures that when the user scrolls past the tagline and social bar into the moodboard area, the list, empty state, and controls have the data they need to render.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual presence; behaves as an invisible wrapper around the moodboard list and related controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state router sits conceptually around the moodboards-specific region, just below the global tagline and social bar. It has no visible footprint but determines whether the central column presents the moodboard list, the empty state, loading indicators, or error treatments. Visually, the user simply sees that, in the same centered content column under the utility band, either cards appear or a single empty-state card fills that space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible structural controller; no fixed or floating behavior, just conditionally switches which child content occupies the main central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The moodboard list occupies the main content column beneath the moodboard utility band. On large screens, it is centered horizontally, spanning roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (about 60–70% of the page width) with 80–120px side margins. There is about 48px of vertical space separating it from the utility band above. Within this column, individual moodboard list items are stacked vertically in a single column, with each item/card separated by 32–40px of vertical whitespace. The list extends downward as the user scrolls, maintaining the same centered alignment and generous spacing to preserve the editorial, low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content; it grows vertically and scrolls with the page, with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each moodboard list item is presented as a spacious card aligned to the central content column. On desktop, the card width matches the column width (around 8 of 12 grid columns) and is centered horizontally. The card has generous internal padding (around 24px) and a clear vertical structure: a large preview image fills the full card width at the top, occupying roughly the upper half of the card’s height—similar in presence to the tall portrait photo in the blog feed. Below the image, the moodboard title appears left-aligned, set in larger type with comfortable line spacing and some breathing room (about 16–24px) above and below. The inline rename control occupies the same title area, keeping edits visually anchored. Beneath the title, any secondary metadata or brief description sits in smaller, lighter text. On the right side of this lower text zone, aligned with the baseline of the title or description, a compact horizontal cluster of actions (including rename trigger when not editing, delete, and a link or button to open the moodboard) is right-aligned. The action cluster is visually light and spaced so that icons or labels do not touch, preserving the calm, airy aesthetic. The whole card is separated from adjacent cards by a generous vertical margin (32–40px), reinforcing the editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the list; no fixed behavior. On smaller screens, the card collapses to full-width, with the action cluster stacking beneath the title to maintain legibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The create form is positioned in a slim utility band at the very top of the moodboard-specific area, directly below the global social icon row and about 48–64px beneath it. This utility band is centered within the same main content column (spanning about 8 of 12 columns) and runs horizontally. The create form’s primary control—such as a soft-edged button or compact inline form—is left-aligned within this band, occupying roughly the left half of the column. The control has comfortable padding (around 12px vertical x 24px horizontal for a button, or similar for an inline field) and is surrounded by ample whitespace so it feels like a confident but gentle call-to-action, not a crowded toolbar. There is 16–24px of vertical padding within the band itself, clearly separating it from the list of cards that begins about 24–32px below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow; it scrolls with the page. On small screens, it remains at the top of the moodboard content area, centered with full-width alignment and the form elements stacking if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline rename control lives inside each moodboard card, occupying the exact visual footprint of the moodboard title. When inactive, the title appears as standard text, left-aligned under the image with 16–24px of margin from the image above. When active, the title transforms into an inline text field in the same position, retaining the same width as the title text block (roughly 60–70% of the card width on the left side), with subtle underlining or a soft border. The field has comfortable internal padding (around 8–12px) and preserves the airy spacing above and below so that editing does not disrupt the calm layout. Any confirmation or cancel affordances are tucked just to the right of the field or slightly below it, aligned with the left text column and spaced 8–12px away to avoid visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and contextual; it appears and disappears within the card without changing the card’s overall alignment. On narrow screens, it expands to nearly full card width but remains directly under the image, preserving the vertical reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each moodboard card, the delete control is positioned in the lower-right corner of the textual area, grouped with other item-level actions. It is aligned horizontally next to the open/edit action within a small action cluster that sits flush to the card’s right padding edge, about midway vertically between the title and the bottom of the card. The control is visually subtle—likely an icon or small-text button with generous clickable padding (8–12px), minimal border treatment, and adequate spacing (8–16px) from neighboring actions so that the cluster does not feel cramped. This placement keeps the destructive action clearly visible but not dominant, maintaining the serene editorial tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the card; scrolls naturally with the list. On small screens, it may drop to a second line beneath the primary open/edit action, still right-aligned, preserving touch-friendly spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no moodboards, the empty state occupies the same central content column as the list, appearing directly below the utility band (create form + sort + navigation). It is presented as a single, spacious card centered on the page, spanning roughly 8 of 12 columns on desktop with wide side margins. The card has generous internal padding (32px or more) and a vertical composition: an optional soft illustration or icon at the top, a prominent headline centered beneath it, and a short explanatory paragraph below, all aligned to the center and separated by 16–24px. The primary create action is included inside this card, centered with generous button padding and 24–32px space above and below, so the user’s next step is clear. Ample whitespace surrounds the entire empty-state card (at least 64px above and below), making it feel like a calm, intentional focal element rather than a gap in content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the list in the main column; scrollable only if its content exceeds viewport height. No fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control resides in the same horizontal utility band as the create form, positioned to the right side of the central content column. On desktop, the create control anchors the left half of the band while the sort control sits on the right, with a comfortable gap (16–24px) between it and the 'Add palettes' navigation link. The sort UI itself is compact—likely a dropdown or segmented pill—occupying only as much width as needed (roughly 20–25% of the column width on the right) so that the band still feels light and uncluttered. Vertically, it aligns with the create form, giving the band a clean, balanced top edge. There is about 48px below this band before the first moodboard card, reinforcing the sense of separation between tools and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the utility band; scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, it stacks below the create control, centered with full-width or near-full-width presentation, with 8–16px vertical spacing to maintain readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Add palettes' navigation link is a tertiary, text-based control placed in the utility band at the top of the moodboard area, adjacent to the sort control on its right. On large screens, it sits right-aligned within the main content column, forming a subtle pair with the sort control: sort on the left of the pair, 'Add palettes' on the far right edge, separated by about 16px. The link is styled lightly (e.g., understated text or a ghost button) so that it is discoverable without competing with the primary create CTA. The surrounding whitespace in the utility band (16–24px top and bottom) allows this navigation link to breathe and maintains the overall airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the utility band and scrolls with the page. On small screens, it may appear under the sort control, still right- or center-aligned, with a small vertical gap to keep the stack legible and touch-friendly.

name: Moodboard Detail
title: Moodboard Detail
description: View and manage a single moodboard, including its title and the list of saved color palettes, with options to remove and reorder palettes.

core_features: Load and display a specific moodboard using the moodboardId URL parameter and getMoodboardDetail.
Show the moodboard title and description so users can confirm they are viewing the correct moodboard.
Allow inline renaming of the moodboard title using renameMoodboard.
Display all palettes associated with the moodboard in an ordered list using data from getMoodboardDetail.
Allow users to remove individual palettes from the moodboard using removePaletteFromMoodboard.
Allow users to reorder palettes in the moodboard and persist the new order using reorderMoodboardPalettes.
Provide an 'Add more palettes' navigation action that sends the user to color_palettes.html with the current moodboardId in the URL.
user_workflows: View a moodboard and confirm contents: 1) User opens moodboard.html with a moodboardId in the URL. 2) Page reads moodboardId and calls getMoodboardDetail. 3) On success, the page shows the moodboard title, description, and the ordered list of palettes. 4) User visually confirms the list of palettes currently saved to this moodboard.

Rename a moodboard: 1) On the Moodboard Detail page, user clicks an 'Edit name' control in the header. 2) An inline text input appears prefilled with the current moodboard name. 3) User edits the name and submits. 4) Page calls renameMoodboard with moodboardId and the newName. 5) On success, the header updates with the returned Moodboard.name and the inline editor closes. 6) If the request fails, an error message is shown and the previous name is restored in the UI.

Remove a palette from the moodboard: 1) User locates a palette in the list and clicks a 'Remove from moodboard' control on that palette card. 2) A confirmation interaction (inline message or dialog) appears. 3) On confirmation, the page calls removePaletteFromMoodboard with moodboardId and paletteId. 4) On success, the palette is removed from the current list in the UI (or the page re-calls getMoodboardDetail to refresh the list). 5) If the list becomes empty, the empty-state component is shown with an option to navigate to add more palettes.

Reorder palettes for presentation: 1) User activates a 'Reorder palettes' mode from the toolbar. 2) The palette list switches into reorder mode (e.g., showing drag handles or move controls). 3) User changes the order of palettes. 4) When satisfied, user clicks 'Save order'. 5) Page builds a paletteOrder array from the new order (list of palette IDs) and calls reorderMoodboardPalettes with moodboardId and paletteOrder. 6) On success, the page updates its moodboard data (either by using the returned Moodboard or re-calling getMoodboardDetail) and exits reorder mode. 7) If the user cancels before saving, the UI reverts to the last saved order.

Add more palettes to the current moodboard: 1) From the Moodboard Detail toolbar or empty state, user clicks 'Add more palettes'. 2) The page navigates to color_palettes.html, including the current moodboardId in the query string. 3) The color_palettes.html page can then use the moodboardId to add palettes back to this moodboard (handled on that page).

Handle invalid or missing moodboardId: 1) User attempts to open moodboard.html without moodboardId or with an invalid moodboardId. 2) The page detects that moodboardId is missing or getMoodboardDetail fails. 3) An error-state component is shown indicating that the moodboard could not be loaded and optionally suggesting navigation back to the moodboards list (handled outside this page definition).
interactions: Page load: On initial page load or whenever the moodboardId URL parameter changes, the page triggers getMoodboardDetail to fetch moodboard and palettes data.
Rename trigger: Clicking the 'Edit name' control in the moodboard header toggles between view mode and an inline edit mode for the moodboard name.
Rename submit: Submitting the rename form calls renameMoodboard with moodboardId and the newName, then updates the displayed title on success.
Rename cancel: Cancelling the rename inline editor discards unsaved changes and restores the last saved moodboard name in the input.
Remove palette request: Clicking 'Remove' on a palette triggers a confirmation interaction and, on confirmation, calls removePaletteFromMoodboard with moodboardId and paletteId.
Remove palette UI update: On successful removePaletteFromMoodboard response, the palette is removed from the in-memory list of palettes or the data is refreshed using getMoodboardDetail.
Enter reorder mode: Clicking a 'Reorder palettes' control toggles a reorder state for the palette list (reflected in a URL parameter) and enables drag-and-drop or move-up/move-down controls.
Change palette order: Dragging a palette handle or clicking move controls updates the local order of palettes in the component state without immediately calling the API.

Save new order: Clicking a 'Save order' button collects the current palette order into a paletteOrder array and calls reorderMoodboardPalettes with moodboardId and paletteOrder, then exits reorder mode on success.
Cancel reorder: Clicking 'Cancel' in reorder mode discards any local ordering changes and restores the palettes to the last saved order, also updating any reorder-related URL parameter.
Add more palettes navigation: Clicking 'Add more palettes' navigates to color_palettes.html with the current moodboardId included as a URL parameter.

Error handling: If any API call (getMoodboardDetail, renameMoodboard, removePaletteFromMoodboard, reorderMoodboardPalettes) fails, an error message is displayed in context (header for rename, inline near palette list for remove/reorder, or full-page for initial load), and the UI remains in the last known consistent state.
Empty state handling: When the moodboard has no palettes, an empty state is shown with text explaining that no palettes are saved and a prominent 'Add palettes' navigation control.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state is driven by the moodboardId URL parameter. On page initialization, the application reads moodboardId from the query string (for example, moodboard.html?moodboardId=<id>). If moodboardId is present, getMoodboardDetail is called with that value to load the Moodboard object and its associated ColorPalette items. If moodboardId is missing or the API call fails, the page switches to an error state instead of attempting other operations.

The page can use additional URL parameters to represent UI state without touching localStorage. For example, a parameter such as mode=reorder can be set when the user enters reorder mode. When mode=reorder is present in the URL, the palette list renders with reorder controls enabled. When the user saves or cancels, the page either removes mode or sets mode=view and updates the URL via navigation or history API.

All API calls on this page reuse the same moodboardId from the URL to avoid desynchronization. When renameMoodboard, removePaletteFromMoodboard, or reorderMoodboardPalettes succeed, the page updates its in-memory state. It may optionally call getMoodboardDetail again with the same moodboardId to ensure that the UI reflects the canonical data source. The current palette order displayed in the UI is always derived either directly from the palettes array returned by getMoodboardDetail or from a local copy that is synchronized back to the server using reorderMoodboardPalettes.

Navigation away from this page (for example to color_palettes.html) always includes the current moodboardId as a query parameter so that other pages can act on the same moodboard context without using localStorage.

components.functionality: Reads the moodboardId URL parameter, calls getMoodboardDetail, and provides the loaded Moodboard and ColorPalette list to child components along with loading and error states.
Displays the moodboard title and description, and allows inline editing of the title using renameMoodboard.
Provides controls related to the palette list including displaying the current palette count, entering or exiting reorder mode, and navigating to add more palettes.

Renders the ordered list of palettes associated with the moodboard, supports removal of palettes, and in reorder mode supports drag-and-drop or manual reordering before saving with reorderMoodboardPalettes.
Represents a single ColorPalette within the moodboard list, showing palette information and providing a remove action (and a drag handle or move controls when in reorder mode).
Confirms that the user wants to remove a palette from the moodboard before calling removePaletteFromMoodboard.
Shown when the page is in reorder mode; provides 'Save order' and 'Cancel' actions that commit or discard the local palette order using reorderMoodboardPalettes or state reset.
Displays when the moodboard has no palettes, explaining that there are no palettes saved yet and providing a navigation control to add palettes.
Displays when the moodboard cannot be loaded due to a missing or invalid moodboardId or a getMoodboardDetail error.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow: a visually strong, asymmetric hero-style moodboard header at the top; directly beneath it, a centered, lightweight control band (the palette toolbar) that echoes the tagline + social bar arrangement; below that, a single central content rail containing the palette list with large, image-like palette cards.
