When needed, the empty or error state occupies the same central content rail. Global overlays (loader, confirmation dialog) sit above this flow without disturbing the underlying layout. A bottom action band for reordering behaves like a persistent footer control, aligning with the central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom editorial flow: a visually strong, asymmetric hero-style moodboard header at the top; directly beneath it, a centered, lightweight control band (the palette toolbar) that echoes the tagline + social bar arrangement; below that, a single central content rail containing the palette list with large, image-like palette cards. When needed, the empty or error state occupies the same central content rail. Global overlays (loader, confirmation dialog) sit above this flow without disturbing the underlying layout. A bottom action band for reordering behaves like a persistent footer control, aligning with the central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The moodboard header (hero) visually takes up roughly the top 35–45% of the initial viewport on desktop, stretching full width but with a centered content area and generous side margins (80–120px). The palette toolbar band beneath uses about 10–15% of the vertical space, but remains visually light. The remaining 40–55% of the viewport is reserved for the scrolling palette list. Each palette card is tall and image-driven, nearly as wide as the central content column. Major vertical separations between bands (header, toolbar, list, footer actions) follow the 48–64px spacing system, with internal padding of 24–32px per component to preserve the airy, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low to medium: the header typography is large and well spaced, with generous white space around the moodboard title and any meta labels. The toolbar presents a small number of clearly separated controls. Palette cards are not tightly stacked; each is separated vertically by 32–48px and has ample internal padding, allowing color swatches and labels to breathe. Empty and error states are visually minimal, with one primary message and plenty of white space to keep the mood calm and refined.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is first drawn to the strong moodboard title within the header on the right side of the hero band, then naturally moves left to any supporting graphic or subtle decorative area, mirroring the reverse Z-pattern of the original mockup. From there, the eye moves downward into the slim palette toolbar band centered on the page. The vertical reading flow continues into the first prominent palette card, then down through subsequent cards in the central content column. When active, the reorder footer anchors attention at the bottom as a clear next-step area, while overlays like the confirmation dialog and loader temporarily become focal points by dimming the background and centering key actions.

layout.overall_description: The Moodboard Detail page reads like a refined editorial feature. At the very top, a full-width hero-style header band introduces the moodboard with a large, right-aligned title and supporting details, balanced by a more minimal left side so the overall composition feels asymmetrical but stable. This header sits within a generous white frame, with a wide central content column and large side margins on desktop. Below, the layout recenters: a slim, airy toolbar band stretches across the central column, aligning controls horizontally and echoing the tagline + social bar from the mockup. Underneath, the page transitions into a vertically scrolling column of palette cards, each spanning most of the central width and reading like large, image-forward editorial cards. The entire stack is separated by comfortable vertical spacing, allowing each palette to feel special and considered. If the moodboard is empty or in error, a single, centered message and call-to-action replaces the list, maintaining the same central column and generous margins. When reordering is active, a subtle but clearly defined action bar hugs the bottom of the viewport, aligned with the central column to feel integrated rather than separate. Throughout, overlays such as the loader and confirmation dialog gently float above the page, centered and softened by a dimmed backdrop, preserving the calm, romantic tone of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-page overlay that sits above all other page content (header, toolbar, palette list, and footer controls). It covers the viewport from edge to edge, but the visual focus remains centered over the main content column. A semi-transparent, soft white or light-gray scrim dims the underlying layout while still letting the hero and cards faintly show through, preserving context. At the vertical and horizontal center of the viewport, aligned with the central content rail, a minimal loading indicator (such as a small spinner and a subtle label) appears, surrounded by ample white space on all sides. On wide screens, this center point visually lines up with the moodboard title and the center of the palette cards, reinforcing the main axis of the page. On smaller screens, the overlay still fills the screen, with the loader centered within the available space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily covers the full viewport as a fixed-position overlay; scroll is generally disabled while active. Its content is centered both vertically and horizontally, aligned with the central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The moodboard header forms the hero band at the top of the page. It stretches full width from left to right edge of the viewport, but its content is constrained to a wide central column with generous left and right margins (approximately 80–120px on large screens, tapering down to 16–24px on mobile). Inside this band, the content is arranged in an asymmetric two-column layout: the left region occupies about 40–45% of the central width, and the right region occupies about 55–60%. The right side is the typographic focal point, holding the large moodboard title in bold, dark type, any context label (e.g., 'MOODBOARD') in lighter uppercase above or near it, and optional soft-colored subtext beneath. This right block sits on a subtly tinted or very light-gray vertical panel that is taller than the typography, echoing the mockup’s tall hero panel and giving the text breathing room with 32px internal padding. The left side feels calmer and more decorative—this area can host a simple preview of the moodboard (like a compact grid or strip of colors) or remain a softly framed block to balance the hero visually. Vertical padding around the header content is generous (48–64px top and bottom), so overall the hero fills roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height on desktop. On smaller screens, the two columns collapse into a single vertical stack: the decorative/preview area appears above, the typographic block below, both centered within the same generous margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the page content. It scrolls away with the rest of the page but visually anchors the top view; internal layout shifts from two-column to single-column on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The palette toolbar sits immediately below the moodboard header as a distinct but lightweight horizontal band. It spans the same central content width as the header, maintaining the wide side margins so it feels part of the same editorial column. Vertically, it is relatively shallow, occupying roughly 10–15% of the viewport height at most, with 16–24px of vertical padding inside. At the top of this band, there may be a thin divider line or a subtle shift in background tone that visually separates it from the hero above, similar to the horizontal line that frames the tagline in the mockup. Controls (such as sort, filter, or view toggles) are arranged in a single horizontal row, centered or lightly justified within the central column. The most important controls (e.g., 'Add palette', 'Reorder') are given enough horizontal spacing (16–24px between elements) so the band feels airy, not cramped. On desktop, controls sit roughly in the middle of the page width; on mobile, they may wrap into two lines but still remain centered with consistent padding above and below. The toolbar reads as a calm, editorial control strip rather than a busy app bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band directly below the header. It scrolls with the content and maintains alignment with the central content column; no sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The palette list occupies the main content area beneath the palette toolbar. It is laid out as a single vertical column of palette cards centered within the page. On large screens, the list content column spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous left and right margins matching the header and toolbar. Each card in the list spans nearly the full width of this central column, creating a continuous editorial rail of content. There is substantial vertical spacing above the first card (about 32–48px below the toolbar) and between each subsequent card (about 32–48px), reinforcing the spacious rhythm of the page. The list itself can extend below the fold, with the page scrolling naturally; the background around the central column stays clean and predominantly white to keep the focus on the cards. On smaller screens, the list continues as a single-column stack with side margins reduced to 16–24px, so each card still has clear separation from the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls as a standard vertical list within the main document flow, anchored to the central content column beneath the toolbar. It expands in height depending on the number of cards, pushing the page length downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each palette card reads like a large, image-centric editorial card in the central column of the page. Horizontally, a card spans almost the full width of the palette list column, leaving a modest inset (about 8–16px) from the column edges if needed, so edges feel soft rather than hard. Vertically, cards are relatively tall, with the color swatches or preview area dominating the upper portion like a hero image. Below or beside this visual area, text details (palette name, metadata, actions) are arranged with generous padding (24–32px inside the card) and clear hierarchy using varying type sizes and weights. The overall card has rounded corners and sits on a subtle raised or bordered surface to stand out from the white page background. Between cards, there is 32–48px of vertical space, allowing each palette to feel distinct and avoid visual crowding. On desktop, internal content may follow a loose two-column feel (visual on the left, text/actions on the right) while still belonging to the single-content rail; on mobile, elements stack vertically while maintaining the same generous padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Child items within the scrollable palette list. They remain aligned with the central column and are fully responsive, adjusting internal layout from more horizontal (desktop) to more vertical (mobile) without changing their overall card footprint in the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-palette confirmation dialog appears as a centered modal floating above a dimmed version of the entire page (header, toolbar, list, and any footer controls). The overlay scrim covers the full viewport, with a subtle opacity to keep the background recognizable but non-distracting. The dialog itself is a compact, rectangular card positioned at the visual center of the viewport, aligned along the same vertical axis as the central content column. On desktop, it occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport width (around 360–480px) and is surrounded by ample whitespace. The dialog’s content is arranged with a clear, concise message at the top, optional supporting text beneath, and primary/secondary buttons aligned horizontally at the bottom right or centered, all inside 24–32px internal padding. Vertical spacing within the dialog is roomy, keeping the tone soft and considered rather than alarming. On smaller screens, the dialog may stretch to 80–90% of the viewport width but remains centered with comfortable margins from screen edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal overlay centered in the viewport, with a full-screen scrim beneath it. It stays in place even when the background document would otherwise scroll; background interactions are disabled while the dialog is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reorder controls footer appears as a horizontal action bar anchored to the bottom of the viewport when the user enters a reorder mode. It spans the full width of the page, but its inner content is constrained to the same central column width as the header and list, ensuring consistent alignment. The bar has a light background and a subtle top border to distinguish it from the main page content, echoing the thin divider used in the tagline area of the mockup. Vertically, the bar is modest yet comfortable—around 64–80px tall—with 16–24px of padding above and below the controls. Within this band, the primary action (e.g., 'Save order') is visually emphasized and placed near the right side of the central column, with secondary actions (e.g., 'Cancel') nearby but less prominent. There is generous horizontal spacing between actions to keep the bar from feeling cluttered. As the user scrolls through the palette list, this footer remains visible at the bottom, providing a persistent sense of control over the reordering state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport while active, overlaying the very bottom of the scrollable content. It maintains the central column alignment and remains visible regardless of scroll position, without obscuring critical content above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty moodboard state occupies the same central area where the palette list would normally appear, directly beneath the palette toolbar. When active, the standard palette cards are absent, and instead a single empty-state module is centered within the main content column. On desktop, this module is horizontally centered and does not exceed 60–70% of the viewport width, aligning with the central column. Vertically, it may sit slightly below the midpoint between the toolbar and the bottom of the initial viewport so that it feels anchored to the existing flow rather than floating aimlessly; there is generous whitespace above and below (48–64px). The content consists of a simple, friendly illustration or icon, a concise headline, brief supportive text, and a primary call-to-action button, all stacked with 16–24px spacing. The surrounding white space reinforces a calm, inviting tone, encouraging the user to start adding palettes. On mobile, the module maintains the same centered alignment with reduced side margins (16–24px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the palette list region within the normal document flow when the moodboard has no palettes. It scrolls with the page if content above pushes it below the fold, but typically fits within the initial viewport on most devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The moodboard error state also occupies the central content area beneath the header and toolbar, replacing the palette list when an error occurs. Like the empty state, it is centered within the main content column, respecting the same left and right margins. The error block is visually compact but given plenty of white space on all sides (at least 48px above and below) to keep the page from feeling overwhelmed. The messaging is arranged in a clear vertical stack: an understated icon or symbol indicating an issue, a short, direct headline, one or two lines of explanation, and a primary action (such as 'Retry') with an optional secondary link (like 'Go back to moodboards'). Internal spacing between these elements follows the 16–24px rhythm. The visual treatment remains elegant and soft—no harsh colors or heavy borders—so it feels consistent with the romantic editorial style even while conveying an error. On smaller screens, it remains centered with 16–24px side margins and enough top and bottom space to prevent crowding against the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the palette list within the main scrollable flow when an error is present. The state is static within the column and scrolls naturally with the rest of the content, without fixed or overlay behavior.

name: Checklists
title: Checklists
description: Page for configuring and generating a customized wedding planning checklist based on timeline, budget, and guest count, and adding checklist tasks into the central My To-Dos list.

core_features: Load available checklist filters for timeline length, budget ranges, and guest count ranges using getChecklistFilterOptions.
Allow the user to select a planning timeline, budget range, and guest count range via a filter form.
Generate a customized checklist by calling generateChecklist with the selected timeline, budget, and guest count, either based on URL parameters or the current filter selection.
Display the generated checklist grouped into timeline sections, highlighting the section identified as current_section_name.
Allow the user to add a single checklist task to the central My To-Dos list using addChecklistTasksToToDos from a per-task control.
Allow the user to multi-select checklist tasks and add them in bulk to the central My To-Dos list using addChecklistTasksToToDos.
Provide feedback when tasks are added to My To-Dos and offer a navigation link to todos.html (View My To-Dos).
Maintain and reflect the current filter state and generated checklist in the page URL using the timelineMonths, budgetOptionKey, and guestCountRangeKey parameters.
user_workflows: Initial page load with URL parameters: (1) User opens checklists.html with some combination of timelineMonths, budgetOptionKey, and guestCountRangeKey in the query string. (2) Page calls getChecklistFilterOptions to obtain available filter option lists. (3) Page validates URL parameter values against the loaded options and sets the filter controls to the matching options where valid. (4) If all three parameters are valid, the page calls generateChecklist with timeline_months=timelineMonths, budget_option_key=budgetOptionKey, and guest_count_range_key=guestCountRangeKey. (5) On success, the page renders the checklist_template information and tasks_by_section, expanding the section whose name matches current_section_name.

Initial page load without URL parameters: (1) User opens checklists.html without query parameters (or with incomplete/invalid ones). (2) Page calls getChecklistFilterOptions to populate filter controls. (3) Page sets initial filter selections based on a non-hardcoded rule such as the first available option in each returned array. (4) No checklist is generated until the user clicks the Generate checklist button. (5) The page shows an empty or instructional state in the checklist area until generateChecklist is called.

Generating a new checklist from the filter form: (1) User adjusts filter controls for timeline, budget, and guest count. (2) User submits the filter form via the Generate checklist button. (3) The form handler validates that each selected value exists in the filter options from getChecklistFilterOptions. (4) Page calls generateChecklist with the selected timeline_months, budget_option_key, and guest_count_range_key. (5) On success, the page updates the checklist view with the returned checklist_template and tasks_by_section and scrolls or focuses the checklist region. (6) The page updates the browser URL query string so that timelineMonths, budgetOptionKey, and guestCountRangeKey reflect the currently used values.

Browsing checklist sections and tasks: (1) After generateChecklist returns data, the page renders a list of sections from tasks_by_section. (2) The section whose section_name matches current_section_name is emphasized and its tasks are expanded by default. (3) The user can expand or collapse sections with a section-level control, which controls visibility of the tasks arrays for those sections. (4) Within each section, tasks are shown in the order defined by their order field (if present), with title and optional description visible.

Adding an individual task to My To-Dos: (1) User finds a task within a section they want to track centrally. (2) User clicks an Add to my to-dos control attached to that specific task. (3) The page constructs a single-element checklistTaskTemplateIds array containing that task's id and calls addChecklistTasksToToDos. (4) On success, the page uses the returned added_todos list to determine how many ToDo items were created and shows a confirmation message. (5) The task row is marked in the current page state as added so the Add control can be disabled or visually updated. (6) A View My To-Dos navigation control becomes visible or remains visible, allowing the user to navigate to todos.html.

Selecting multiple tasks and adding them in bulk: (1) User navigates through sections and uses per-task selection controls (such as checkboxes) to select multiple tasks across one or more sections. (2) A bulk action toolbar displays the count of selected tasks based on current page state. (3) User clicks Add selected to my to-dos in the toolbar. (4) The page collects the ids of all currently selected tasks into checklistTaskTemplateIds and calls addChecklistTasksToToDos. (5) On success, the page marks those task ids as added in local page state, clears the selection state, and shows a confirmation indicating how many tasks were added, based on the length of added_todos. (6) View My To-Dos link is available so the user can navigate to todos.html.

Navigating to My To-Dos after adding tasks: (1) After at least one successful addChecklistTasksToToDos call, the page shows a View My To-Dos link. (2) User clicks this link. (3) The browser navigates to todos.html without modifying or adding query parameters, relying on the centralized ToDos data to display the updated task list on that page.

Regenerating or adjusting the checklist: (1) After viewing an initial checklist, the user decides to explore a different configuration. (2) The user modifies one or more filter controls. (3) The user clicks Generate checklist again. (4) Page calls generateChecklist with the new values and replaces the previous checklist with the new one. (5) The URL query parameters are updated to reflect the new selection. (6) Previously added tasks remain in My To-Dos at the data level, even though the current page only tracks added status for the current session in memory.
interactions: On page load, call getChecklistFilterOptions, then optionally call generateChecklist based on URL parameters.
On changing filter fields, update local selected filter state but do not call generateChecklist until the form is submitted.
On submitting the filter form via Generate checklist, prevent default browser submission, validate selections, call generateChecklist, update the checklist display, and synchronize URL parameters.
On clicking a section header, toggle its expanded/collapsed state and ensure that tasks for that section are shown or hidden accordingly.
On clicking an Add to my to-dos control for a specific task, call addChecklistTasksToToDos with that task's id and, on success, update the UI state for that task to indicate it has been added.
On toggling a task's selection checkbox, add or remove that task's id from a set of selected task ids maintained in local page state, and update the bulk add toolbar count.
On clicking Add selected to my to-dos in the bulk toolbar, call addChecklistTasksToToDos with all currently selected task ids, then update added status for those tasks and clear the selection state.
On clicking View My To-Dos, navigate to todos.html.
On any interface error (getChecklistFilterOptions, generateChecklist, or addChecklistTasksToToDos), show a functional error state or inline message, without substituting static fallback data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL query parameters and API responses, with no reliance on localStorage. On every load, the page reads timelineMonths, budgetOptionKey, and guestCountRangeKey from the URL. After getChecklistFilterOptions resolves, it validates these parameters against the available options. If all three parameters correspond to valid options, the page calls generateChecklist using those values. If any parameter is missing or invalid, the page does not auto-generate a checklist and instead waits for user input. The currently selected filter values are kept in in-memory state and are considered the source of truth for subsequent generateChecklist calls. After a successful generateChecklist response, the page updates the browser URL query string so that timelineMonths equals the chosen timeline_months value, budgetOptionKey equals the chosen budget_option_key, and guestCountRangeKey equals the chosen guest_count_range_key. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL reconstructs the same filter state and triggers the same checklist generation flow. Task-level state, such as which tasks are selected for bulk actions and which tasks have been added to My To-Dos during the current session, is maintained only in memory and is keyed by the ChecklistTaskTemplate id values returned from generateChecklist. This session state does not modify the URL. All checklist content, including sections and tasks, is obtained exclusively from the generateChecklist API; no static tasks or sections are defined in the page.

components.functionality: Loads available timeline, budget, and guest count options using getChecklistFilterOptions, reads matching URL parameters when available, and lets the user select a combination to generate a checklist. On submission, it calls generateChecklist and updates the URL query parameters.
event_handlers: onLoad: Triggered when the page loads; calls getChecklistFilterOptions and initializes filter state based on URL parameters when possible.
onChange: Triggered when the user changes any filter control; updates in-memory selected filter values without immediately calling generateChecklist.

onSubmit: Triggered when the user clicks the Generate checklist button; validates current selections, calls generateChecklist with timeline_months, budget_option_key, and guest_count_range_key, and updates the browser URL query parameters to reflect the chosen values.

components.functionality: Coordinates the call to generateChecklist based on current filter state or URL parameters, stores the returned checklist_template and tasks_by_section in page state, and exposes the current_section_name to downstream components.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: After getChecklistFilterOptions resolves, checks if timelineMonths, budgetOptionKey, and guestCountRangeKey are present and valid in the URL; if so, calls generateChecklist with those values.

onGenerateChecklistSuccess: Stores checklist_template, tasks_by_section, and current_section_name from the generateChecklist response in memory and signals dependent components (such as checklist display) to render.
onGenerateChecklistError: Handles errors from generateChecklist by clearing or not updating the existing checklist state and informing the user through an error message component.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the currently generated checklist, such as the title, description, and any relevant labels derived from the checklist_template, and indicates the section considered current based on current_section_name.
event_handlers: onChecklistChange: Triggered whenever a new generateChecklist response is received; updates the summary information accordingly.

components.functionality: Renders the checklist tasks grouped by section. Each section corresponds to an element in tasks_by_section with a section_name and an array of ChecklistTaskTemplate items. The section matching current_section_name is expanded by default to emphasize tasks that should be prioritized.
event_handlers: onChecklistLoaded: Initializes internal section-expanded state such that the section whose section_name equals current_section_name is expanded and other sections follow a default expansion rule.
onSectionHeaderClick: Toggles expanded or collapsed state for the clicked section, updating which tasks are visible.
onTaskRender: For each task, passes task data (including id, title, description, section_name, order) down to the checklist-task-row component for per-task interactions.

components.functionality: Represents a single checklist task within a section, displaying its title and optional description and providing controls to add the task to My To-Dos and to select it for bulk operations.
data_binding: Task added state (in-memory, keyed by ChecklistTaskTemplate.id)
Task selection state (in-memory, keyed by ChecklistTaskTemplate.id)
event_handlers: onAddToMyTodosClick: When the user clicks the Add to my to-dos control for this task, constructs a checklistTaskTemplateIds array containing this task's id and calls addChecklistTasksToToDos. On success, updates the task's added state and notifies the add-todos-feedback component.
onSelectionToggle: When the user toggles the selection checkbox for this task, adds or removes this task's id from the global selected-task-ids set maintained by the bulk-add-toolbar.

onAddSuccessUpdate: Responds to a successful addChecklistTasksToToDos call (whether single-task or bulk) by updating the row to reflect that the task has been added, for example disabling or hiding the Add control in this session.

components.functionality: Tracks which tasks are selected across all sections and provides a bulk Add selected to my to-dos action that sends all selected task ids to addChecklistTasksToToDos in a single call.
data_binding: Selected task ids (in-memory set of ChecklistTaskTemplate.id values)
event_handlers: onSelectionChange: Listens for selection changes from checklist-task-row components and updates the count of selected tasks displayed in the toolbar.

onAddSelectedClick: When the user clicks Add selected to my to-dos, calls addChecklistTasksToToDos with checklistTaskTemplateIds equal to the current set of selected task ids. On success, marks the corresponding tasks as added in in-memory state, clears the selection set, and triggers the add-todos-feedback component.
onAddSelectedError: Displays an error message if addChecklistTasksToToDos fails and leaves the selection state unchanged so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Shows inline feedback about the result of calls to addChecklistTasksToToDos, including how many ToDo items were created or updated, and ensures that the user can see the effect of single-task and bulk additions.
event_handlers: onAddTodosSuccess: Receives the added_todos array returned from addChecklistTasksToToDos and displays a confirmation message based on its length. Also signals that the View My To-Dos link should be visible.
onAddTodosError: Displays a functional error message when addChecklistTasksToToDos returns an error or an unexpected result.
onDismissMessage: Allows the user to dismiss feedback messages so the main checklist remains uncluttered.

components.functionality: Provides a navigational control for the user to open the central My To-Dos page (todos.html) after items have been added from the checklist.
data_binding: Visibility state based on whether any addChecklistTasksToToDos call has succeeded in the current session.
event_handlers: onFirstAddSuccess: When the first addChecklistTasksToToDos call succeeds, updates internal state so that the View My To-Dos link is shown.
onClick: Navigates the browser to todos.html without adding extra query parameters.

components.functionality: Handles error and empty states for both filter loading and checklist generation, informing the user when no tasks are available for the current configuration or when an API call fails.
event_handlers: onFilterOptionsError: Shows an error message and disables the filter form if getChecklistFilterOptions fails, since filter options are required to proceed.

onChecklistEmpty: If generateChecklist returns successfully but tasks_by_section is empty or absent, shows an informative message indicating that no checklist tasks are available for the selected configuration.
onRetryClick: Provides a way to retry failed API calls by re-invoking getChecklistFilterOptions or generateChecklist depending on which call failed.

components.functionality: Synchronizes the page's filter state with the URL query parameters and ensures that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same checklist configuration.
event_handlers: onLoadFromUrl: On initial load, reads timelineMonths, budgetOptionKey, and guestCountRangeKey from the URL and, after getChecklistFilterOptions completes, validates and applies them to the filter form and possibly triggers generateChecklist.
onFilterChange: When selected filter values change via the filter form, prepares updated URL parameters but does not apply them until a successful generateChecklist call completes.

onChecklistGenerated: After generateChecklist succeeds, updates the browser URL to include or overwrite timelineMonths, budgetOptionKey, and guestCountRangeKey so that the current configuration is encoded in the location.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing asymmetric hero, centered tagline, and social bar from the global layout, this page continues in a single, centered content rail. At the top of that rail sits a local summary/header for the checklist tool, followed by the configuration area (filters and generation controls) as a cohesive band. Beneath that, the main checklist content occupies the bulk of the page as a vertically stacked accordion of sections containing task rows. The bulk action toolbar and feedback messages are visually tied to the checklist area, sitting just above the sections. The empty state replaces the accordion region when needed, while URL state sync operates invisibly in the background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing asymmetric hero, centered tagline, and social bar from the global layout, this page continues in a single, centered content rail. At the top of that rail sits a local summary/header for the checklist tool, followed by the configuration area (filters and generation controls) as a cohesive band. Beneath that, the main checklist content occupies the bulk of the page as a vertically stacked accordion of sections containing task rows. The bulk action toolbar and feedback messages are visually tied to the checklist area, sitting just above the sections. The empty state replaces the accordion region when needed, while URL state sync operates invisibly in the background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content rail spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on large screens, centered with generous left/right margins (80–120px). Vertically, the summary header and configuration band together occupy roughly the top 25–30% of the main content area beneath the hero. The accordion checklist takes the remaining 70–75% of the scrollable page height, with large vertical gaps (48–64px) between the summary, configuration area, toolbar/feedback strip, and the accordion body. Inside each component, padding adheres to the 16–32px range to preserve an airy, editorial feeling.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains low to medium: each component breathes with ample white space, and fields, buttons, and rows are not tightly packed. The filter form uses comfortable spacing between field groups; the accordion sections have generous vertical margins and clear separation; task rows are readable with sufficient line height and subtle dividers instead of heavy borders. The bulk toolbar is compact but not cramped, and feedback messages sit in lightly tinted bands with padding rather than cramped badges.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the global page hero, then flows down into the checklist summary header as the local focal point of this tool. From there, the flow continues into the filter form and the prominent generation controls, guiding users to set their parameters and trigger the checklist. Once generated, attention shifts just below to the bulk-add toolbar and any inline feedback, then naturally continues down into the vertically stacked accordion sections and their task rows. The view-todos link is placed to the right of feedback or toolbar areas, subtly inviting the user to jump to their centralized My To-Dos list without breaking the primary top-down reading flow. When an error or empty state is present, it becomes the main visual anchor in the checklist region, replacing the accordion while keeping alignment within the same vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing hero and centered tagline band, the page resolves into a calm, single-column editorial rail that feels like a refined planning worksheet. A centered checklist summary header introduces the tool with large typography, framed by wide side margins and generous top padding. Directly beneath it, a soft-edged configuration band combines the filter form and generation controls, styled like a light card floating in the middle of the page. A comfortable vertical gap then leads into a slim, full-width toolbar and feedback strip aligned to the same central rail, anchoring actions and system responses just above the main checklist. The checklist itself appears as a tall, image-free but card-like accordion, each section spanning the width of the content rail with clear separation and airy padding, and containing neatly spaced task rows that feel more like a curated list than a dense table. Throughout, muted tones, subtle rules, and consistent spacing maintain the romantic editorial aesthetic: the composition feels spacious and calm, while still clearly guiding the user from configuration, to generation, to reviewing sections, to adding tasks into their My To-Dos list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist filter form sits near the top of the main content rail, directly below the checklist-summary-header and occupying the full width of the central column (about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered). It is contained within a soft, light-toned card with 24–32px internal padding on all sides, giving it a generous, editorial feel. On large screens, the form fields are arranged in two columns within the implicit 12-column grid (e.g., timeline on the left 6 columns, budget and guest count stacked or aligned on the right 6 columns), with clear 24–32px horizontal gutters between field groups. On smaller screens, the fields collapse into a single column, each field spanning nearly the full card width with 16–24px vertical spacing between them. There is ample white space above and below the form (around 32–48px), visually separating it from the summary header above and the generation controller below while keeping them clearly related as one configuration band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; it grows vertically as fields wrap on smaller screens but always remains centered in the content rail and aligned to the underlying grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist generation controller is positioned directly beneath the filter form, with a modest 16–24px vertical gap to suggest it is part of the same configuration band. It spans the same central content width as the form, but is slightly shallower in height, resembling a horizontal control strip. Internally, it uses 16–24px padding and aligns its primary call-to-action button toward the right side of the column, echoing the right-weighted emphasis of the original hero title block. Secondary controls (e.g., reset or advanced options toggles) sit to the left or just below the primary button, with ample spacing so nothing feels cramped. The controller reads as the natural end of the setup area: visually lighter than the filter card but more pronounced than the content below, acting as the clear pivot between configuring preferences and viewing the resulting checklist.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; horizontally aligns with the filter form and accordion, and maintains its relative position just above the checklist regardless of scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist summary header is the first page-specific component in the main content area beneath the global hero/tagline/social. It is centered in the content rail and uses large, bold typography for the page title, occupying roughly the top 15–20% of the main scrollable area below the hero. The header text block spans about 60% of the central column width, aligned slightly to the right within the column to subtly echo the mockup’s right-heavy title block, while any small label or breadcrumb (e.g., 'Checklists') can sit in lighter text above or to the left. A short descriptive line or meta summary (e.g., showing selected date range or couple’s name) appears beneath the main title in a softer tone, with generous line spacing and a 16–24px gap from the title. The entire header area has 48–64px padding above and 32–48px below, creating a calm breathing space before the more functional filter and control components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; serves as the local hero for the page’s tool area and does not stick, but establishes the visual rhythm and alignment for all components below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist sections accordion occupies the bulk of the page below the configuration band and toolbar/feedback strip, spanning the full width of the central content column. It begins roughly 48px beneath the bulk-add-toolbar and any feedback messaging, establishing a clear transition from controls to content. Each accordion section appears as a vertical card-like block with a soft border or subtle divider at the top and bottom, and 24–32px internal padding; sections are stacked with 32–40px vertical spacing between them to maintain the spacious editorial feel. Section headers (e.g., '12+ Months Before') are left-aligned within the column, using all caps or small caps with generous letter spacing to echo the mockup’s labels. When expanded, a section vertically reveals its checklist-task-rows, which fill the card edge-to-edge inside the padding. On large screens, the accordion extends down the page as a continuous, scrollable list; on smaller screens, it maintains full-width alignment with consistent lateral margins, giving the impression of a cohesive, elegant list rather than compartmentalized widgets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static but vertically extensible; sections expand and collapse in place without shifting their horizontal alignment. The accordion scrolls within the main page, with no internal scrollbars, preserving the clean vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each checklist task row lives inside an expanded accordion section and spans the full usable width of that section’s card, minus its internal padding. Rows are organized as horizontal bands with 12–16px vertical padding and a subtle bottom divider line or very light rule, keeping them visually distinct yet understated. The left two-thirds of each row are reserved for the task title and any brief description, left-aligned with comfortable line height; the right third contains controls like an 'Add to My To-Dos' checkbox or button, aligned flush right to mirror the right-weighted emphasis seen in the hero typography. There is a small 8–12px horizontal gap between text and controls so the row feels balanced and uncluttered. On smaller screens, the controls naturally wrap beneath the task title, stacking with 8–12px vertical spacing while still maintaining clear separation. The overall effect is of calm, readable lines within a curated list, not a dense spreadsheet.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each accordion section; rows expand the height of their parent section as tasks are added or removed, but do not introduce independent scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk-add toolbar sits immediately above the checklist-sections-accordion, forming a slim yet clear control band that anchors checklist actions. It spans the full width of the central content rail, with 16–24px padding and a light background treatment that differentiates it from both the white page background and the accordion cards below. On large screens, bulk selection controls (e.g., 'Select all in this section', 'Add selected to My To-Dos') align to the left half of the toolbar, while any summary of selected items and a subtle view-todos-link align to the right, creating a balanced horizontal composition. There is 24–32px vertical spacing between this toolbar and the generation controller above, and another 24–32px before the first accordion section below. The toolbar is visually lighter than the summary header and configuration band but clearly more assertive than the task rows, signaling its role as a control layer over the checklist content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Becomes sticky to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls the accordion into view, but only within the main content column. This allows bulk actions to remain accessible while reviewing long lists without breaking the central, vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'add to-dos' feedback component appears as a thin, pastel-tinted band directly beneath the bulk-add-toolbar and above the accordion, sharing the same central width and alignment. It uses 12–16px vertical padding with text centered vertically and left-aligned horizontally, keeping the message clear but understated. The band spans edge-to-edge within the content column, creating a continuous strip that lightly separates the control layer from the checklist content. When present, it introduces only a modest 8–12px vertical gap to the accordion below, so that success or error messages feel closely tied to the actions that triggered them without overwhelming the layout. On small screens, the band maintains its full-width appearance with the text wrapping naturally and maintaining generous line height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline as actions occur; it does not stick or float, but gently pushes the accordion content down while visible, preserving the smooth vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'view to-dos' link is positioned within the same horizontal band as either the bulk-add-toolbar or the add-todos-feedback component, typically aligned to the far right of that band. On wide screens, it sits on the right side, visually counterbalancing left-aligned controls or feedback text and echoing the right-aligned focal points from the hero layout. The link uses simple, understated typography with slightly increased letter spacing and a small arrow or chevron, reading as a secondary navigation affordance rather than a primary button. There is a modest 8–16px horizontal offset from any adjacent controls or message text so it feels deliberately placed rather than crowded. On narrow screens, the link wraps beneath the main toolbar controls or feedback text, aligned left within the column with a 8–12px vertical gap, still clearly associated with the checklist actions above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its parent band; it scrolls away with the toolbar or feedback strip and does not have independent sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there is an error or no checklist can be generated, the error/empty-state panel replaces the accordion region in the main content rail, appearing directly beneath the bulk-add-toolbar and/or feedback areas (or, if generation fails before those render, directly beneath the generation controller). It is centered within the column and uses a light card treatment with 24–32px padding, a soft background tint, and a concise message paired with a gentle icon or illustrative mark at the top. The message text is center-aligned within the panel, with a primary action button (e.g., 'Adjust filters') centered beneath it, and generous vertical spacing (16–24px) between icon, headline, body copy, and button. The panel occupies roughly one-third of the viewport height on large screens, with ample white space above and below so it feels calm and reassuring rather than alarming. Relative to the rest of the layout, it becomes the main focal point in the checklist area while preserving the same central alignment and spacing rhythm as the accordion it replaces.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it expands to fill the vertical space where the accordion would normally be, and scrolls with the page if content above pushes it downward. Only one of accordion or empty-state is visible in this region at a time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is non-visual and does not occupy any spatial area in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the configuration band and checklist region, listening to changes in filters, generation state, and accordion section expansion to reflect them in the URL. This enables deep-linking into specific configurations or sections while preserving the clean, editorial visual presentation with no additional on-screen controls or indicators.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visible spatial behavior; it operates purely in the background without altering the dimensions or placement of any on-screen components.

name: My To-Dos
title: My To-Dos
description: Centralized task list where users can review all their wedding planning tasks, update their status and details, and confirm that newly generated checklist tasks were added.

core_features: Load and display the full list of to-dos for the current user using getToDosList, with clear status (pending or completed) and basic metadata (title, optional description, due date, notes, source_type, section_name).
Allow users to toggle each to-do between pending and completed by calling updateToDoStatus, updating the list in-place.
Allow users to edit basic details for a to-do (due_date and notes) via an inline or side-panel form that calls updateToDoDetails.
Allow users to remove a to-do from the list by calling removeToDo, with a confirmation step before the API call.

Provide filter and grouping controls (e.g., show pending only, show completed, group by checklist section_name or source_type) that are reflected in URL parameters so views can be bookmarked and shared.

When a highlightSource URL parameter is present (for example, after coming from a checklist generation flow), show a dedicated recent-tasks confirmation view that highlights the most recently added checklist-sourced to-dos, confirming that they were successfully added.
Allow users to focus on a single to-do (selectedToDoId in the URL) to quickly review and edit its details without losing their current filter/sort state.
user_workflows:
Review tasks after generating a checklist:
1. User navigates from checklists.html to todos.html with a highlightSource URL parameter.
2. On page load, the controller component reads highlightSource from the URL and calls getToDosList.
3. After receiving todos, the page identifies to-dos that match the expected origin (e.g., source_type = 'checklist_task') and sorts them by created_at.
4. A 'Recent additions' confirmation block displays the subset of most recently created checklist-based to-dos so the user can visually confirm they were added.
5. The user can immediately mark any of these recent tasks as completed, edit their due dates/notes, or remove them; each action triggers the associated interface (updateToDoStatus, updateToDoDetails, removeToDo) and then updates the visible list state.
6. The user can close or dismiss the recent-tasks confirmation view; this does not affect the underlying to-dos, only the local presentation.

Mark a to-do as completed or reopen it as pending:
1. User views the main to-do list, optionally filtered by status from the status URL parameter.
2. The user clicks the status toggle (e.g., a checkbox) on a specific to-do item.
3. The list component calls updateToDoStatus with the to-do's id and the new status value.
4. On successful response, the UI updates that item's status field and, if a status filter is active, includes or excludes the item accordingly without a full page reload.
5. The URL remains unchanged unless the user changes filter controls, so the user can use the browser back/forward buttons to navigate different filtered states.

Edit due date and notes for a to-do:
1. User clicks an 'Edit' control on a specific to-do item.
2. The page sets selectedToDoId in the URL (via a pushState/replaceState update) so that the selected item's details are addressable.
3. A details panel or inline edit form appears for that to-do, pre-populated with its current due_date and notes from the ToDo data.
4. The user modifies the due date and/or notes and submits the form.
5. The details form component calls updateToDoDetails with the to-do id and the new due_date and notes values.
6. On success, the main list is updated with the returned todo object so the edited values are visible everywhere.
7. The user can close the details panel; this clears selectedToDoId from the URL (or sets it to an empty value) using history state updates.

Remove a to-do from the list:
1. User clicks a 'Remove' or delete action on a to-do row.
2. A confirmation dialog or inline confirmation state appears for that specific item, without calling the API yet.
3. If the user confirms, the component calls removeToDo with the to-do id.
4. On success = true, the item is removed from the in-memory list and disappears from the visible list without reloading the page.
5. If the user cancels at step 2, the dialog is closed and no API call is made.

Filter and group to-dos using URL parameters:
1. When the page loads, the controller parses URL parameters such as status (e.g., pending, completed, all), groupBy (e.g., section, source), sort (e.g., created_at, due_date), and selectedToDoId.
2. The controller calls getToDosList and stores the returned todos.
3. The list component applies client-side filters and grouping according to the parsed parameters to derive the visible list.
4. When the user changes any filter or grouping control, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters and re-applies the filter/grouping logic to the already-loaded todos (no new API call is required unless a refresh is desired).
5. If the user shares or bookmarks the URL, reopening it will restore the same filtered/grouped view by repeating steps 1–3.

Focus on a specific to-do via URL:
1. A link from another page or from within the list (e.g., 'View details') points to todos.html with a selectedToDoId parameter.
2. On load, the controller reads selectedToDoId and calls getToDosList.
3. After data is loaded, the details panel component locates the to-do with that id and automatically opens its details view (due date and notes) for review or editing.
4. The user can make updates (updateToDoDetails, updateToDoStatus) or remove it (removeToDo) from within the detail view.
5. Closing the detail view updates the URL by removing selectedToDoId or setting it to a neutral value.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Event: Page load or navigation to todos.html.
- Behavior: A controller component reads URL parameters (highlightSource, status, groupBy, sort, selectedToDoId) and calls getToDosList.
- Result: The full to-do list is stored in memory and passed to child components; any recent-task highlighting or filters are applied before rendering.

Status toggle interaction:
- Element: Status control (e.g., checkbox or toggle) in each to-do row.
- Event: User clicks the status control.
- Behavior: Component determines the new status value (pending or completed) and calls updateToDoStatus with toDoId and status.
- Result: On success, the item’s status is updated in the list; any active filter (such as status=pending) is re-applied to determine whether the item remains visible.

Edit details interaction:
- Element: 'Edit' action on a to-do row.
- Event: User clicks 'Edit'.
- Behavior: selectedToDoId is written into the URL; a detail form is shown, bound to the corresponding ToDo’s due_date and notes.
- Event: User submits the form.
- Behavior: Component calls updateToDoDetails with toDoId, due_date, and notes.
- Result: On success, the updated todo object replaces the previous one in the local list, and the UI shows the new values; the URL can remain with selectedToDoId or be cleared when the panel is closed.

Remove to-do interaction:
- Element: 'Remove' action on a to-do row.
- Event: User clicks 'Remove'.
- Behavior: A confirmation prompt appears for that to-do.
- Event: User confirms removal.
- Behavior: Component calls removeToDo with toDoId; on success, the item is removed from the local list data structure.
- Event: User cancels removal.
- Behavior: Prompt is closed; no API call is made.

Filter by status interaction:
- Element: Status filter control (e.g., dropdown or tabs for All / Pending / Completed).
- Event: User selects a status value.
- Behavior: The page updates the status URL parameter and re-applies client-side filtering to the todos returned by getToDosList.
- Result: Only the todos matching the selected status are visible; the current selection is encoded in the URL so the view is restorable.

Grouping and sorting interaction:
- Element: Group-by selector (e.g., none / section_name / source_type) and sort selector (e.g., by due_date or created_at).
- Event: User changes groupBy or sort value.
- Behavior: The corresponding URL parameters are updated; the list component reorders or groups the in-memory todos according to the new options.
- Result: The visual organization of the list changes without additional API calls; the state is preserved in the URL.

Recent checklist tasks confirmation interaction (highlightSource):
- Element: Recent-tasks confirmation section that appears when highlightSource is present in the URL.
- Event: Page loads and highlightSource is detected.
- Behavior: After getToDosList resolves, the controller filters todos whose source_type indicates a checklist origin and orders them by created_at. A subset of the most recently created such tasks is passed to the recent-tasks component to display as confirmation.
- Event: User dismisses the confirmation section.
- Behavior: The section is hidden for the current page view; highlightSource remains in the URL but is ignored by local state if a 'dismissed' flag is set in memory (no API calls).

Direct navigation to a specific to-do:
- Element: Links pointing to todos.html with a selectedToDoId parameter (from within this page or from other pages).
- Event: User follows the link.
- Behavior: Page loads, getToDosList is called, and once todos are available, the details component opens the to-do whose id matches selectedToDoId.
- Result: User sees and can edit or remove that to-do directly without manually locating it in the list.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters rather than localStorage for all shareable and restorable state. The recognized URL parameters include: highlightSource (string, optional) – when present, indicates that the user arrived after some external action such as generating a checklist; after getToDosList completes, this parameter triggers the recent-tasks confirmation logic, which highlights newly added checklist-sourced todos based on their source_type and created_at values. status (string, optional) – values such as 'pending', 'completed', or 'all' determine which todos are visible; if omitted, the page applies a default status filter in memory but does not assume any content values. groupBy (string, optional) – determines client-side grouping of todos, such as by section_name or by source_type; when provided, the list component groups todos accordingly without additional API calls. sort (string, optional) – controls the ordering of todos, for example by created_at or due_date; sorting is performed client-side on the data returned by getToDosList. selectedToDoId (string, optional) – if present, the details/edit component automatically opens the corresponding to-do for review and editing. On every navigation or filter change, the page reads the current URL parameters, calls getToDosList on initial load (and when a full refresh is requested), and then derives the visible list, grouping, highlighting, and selection purely from the ToDo data and those URL parameters. Updates via updateToDoStatus, updateToDoDetails, and removeToDo are reflected immediately in the in-memory copy of todos, ensuring the UI remains consistent with the backend without relying on any localStorage-based caching.

components.functionality: Initializes the My To-Dos page by parsing URL parameters, invoking getToDosList, maintaining the in-memory todos collection, and passing filtered, sorted, grouped, and highlighted subsets to child components.
Displays the list of to-dos returned by the controller, after applying status filters, grouping, and sorting; supports inline actions to toggle status, open the details editor, and initiate removal.
Represents a single to-do item within the list, showing its title, status, optional due_date, notes preview, and source metadata; exposes actions for toggling status, editing, and removing.
Handles status changes for a single to-do by sending updateToDoStatus requests and updating the bound ToDo object on success.

Provides a focused view for a selected to-do (based on selectedToDoId) where the user can edit due_date and notes, and see full description; uses updateToDoDetails to persist edits and updates the main list state on success.
Confirms a user's intent to delete a to-do before calling removeToDo; on success, notifies the controller or parent list to remove the item from the in-memory collection.

Exposes user controls for filtering by status, grouping by section_name or source_type, and sorting by fields such as created_at or due_date; writes the user's selections into URL parameters and triggers re-computation of the derived view.

When highlightSource is present in the URL, receives the full todo list from the controller, identifies checklist-sourced todos (e.g., source_type indicating a checklist origin), and displays the subset that were most recently created as confirmation that new checklist tasks were added.

Displays an informational message when getToDosList returns an empty todos array after filtering, guiding users to add tasks from other tools or checklists (without creating tasks directly on this page, since no creation interface is available).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page inherits the asymmetric hero from the mockup: a left logo/brand block and a right title/descriptor block occupying the top 40–50% of the viewport. Directly beneath, the composition recenters into a single-column, centered content rail. In this rail, a short explanatory tagline for the My To-Dos page sits first (within the hero area), followed by a horizontal row of filter controls that visually echo the social bar. Below the filters, a recent-todos highlight band acts as the first prominent content card, and then the main to-dos list flows vertically as stacked rows and optional expanded detail panels. The confirmation dialog floats above everything when invoked, and the empty state replaces the highlight and list when there are no tasks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page inherits the asymmetric hero from the mockup: a left logo/brand block and a right title/descriptor block occupying the top 40–50% of the viewport. Directly beneath, the composition recenters into a single-column, centered content rail. In this rail, a short explanatory tagline for the My To-Dos page sits first (within the hero area), followed by a horizontal row of filter controls that visually echo the social bar. Below the filters, a recent-todos highlight band acts as the first prominent content card, and then the main to-dos list flows vertically as stacked rows and optional expanded detail panels. The confirmation dialog floats above everything when invoked, and the empty state replaces the highlight and list when there are no tasks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On large screens, the hero spans full width with about 80–120px side margins framing a 12-column content area. The left hero block (logo/brand) consumes about 40–45% of the width; the right block (My To-Dos title and descriptors) takes 55–60%, both with generous 32px internal padding and 48–64px vertical padding. Below the hero, the content rail is centered and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, maintaining the same wide side gutters. Vertical spacing between bands—filters, recent highlight, list—is generous (48–64px). Individual rows and panels use 16–32px internal paddings to keep an airy, editorial feel. The confirmation dialog occupies a compact 360–420px width centered on screen, with ample padding (24–32px).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout stays low-to-medium density. The hero has large type and lots of white space. Filters are presented in a single, uncluttered row. The recent-todos highlight is a large, open card with only a handful of items or summary content. The main to-dos list uses spacious rows with clear separation (16–24px gaps) and comfortable line heights. The details panel expands with substantial padding around fields to avoid a cramped form feel. Only essential controls are visible per row, and iconography is minimal, in line with the minimalist editorial aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: As in the mockup, the eye first lands on the bold My To-Dos title in the right hero block, then shifts left to the familiar circular wedding brand logo. From there, it moves down to the soft, centered explanatory text at the bottom of the hero, which leads naturally to the centered filter row just below. The next focal point is the recent-todos highlight, treated like a featured article card and drawing attention to new or important tasks. The gaze then proceeds down the single-column list of to-do rows, occasionally expanding into a detail panel directly beneath a selected row, reinforcing a vertical reading flow. When triggered, the remove-confirmation dialog interrupts this flow by dimming the background and centering the user’s attention on the decision. When the list is empty, the empty state occupies the main column as the primary focal element below the hero.

layout.overall_description: The My To-Dos page opens with a full-width hero that mirrors the wedding blog’s asymmetric composition: on the left, a square-ish, softly colored panel holds the circular gold logo; on the right, a tall, light-gray panel frames the large My To-Dos title, a delicate label like CHECKLIST, and a short line describing the purpose of the page. This band takes up nearly half of the initial viewport, surrounded by generous white space. As the hero ends, the layout recenters into a single, vertically stacked column. A thin divider line runs across the content width, with the page’s calming, centered tagline sitting over or between subtle horizontal rules, echoing the original tagline treatment. Immediately beneath, a slim, horizontally arranged set of filter controls is centered in the column, reading like an elegant control bar. Below that, a wide, image-like highlight card for recent tasks anchors the top of the list area, followed by a vertical sequence of spacious, card-like to-do rows that align to the central rail. Selecting a row gently expands a pale detail panel directly beneath it, preserving the page’s vertical reading rhythm. Throughout, the wide side gutters, generous vertical spacing, and restrained use of controls keep the interface feeling light and editorial, while the modal confirmation and empty state calmly take over the central column whenever needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The todos-page-controller acts as the invisible structural wrapper for the entire page. It spans the full browser width and height and defines the 12-column grid and central content rail. At the top, it positions the asymmetric hero: a left panel occupying roughly 5 of 12 columns and a right panel occupying 7 of 12 columns, both aligned to a common top baseline with 80–120px outer margins. Beneath this hero band, the controller centers all subsequent content—tagline, filter controls, recent-todos-highlight, todos-list, and empty state—within a single vertical column that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, with consistent section gaps of 48–64px. All other components sit inside this controller and inherit its spacing and alignment rules.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static page-level container; follows normal document flow and establishes the grid, but has no visible styling or interactive behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The todos-list is positioned in the main centered content rail, directly below the recent-todos-highlight (or immediately under the filter controls if the highlight is not present). It begins approximately 48–64px below the preceding band. On large screens, it spans about 60–70% of the page width, centered horizontally, aligning exactly with the width of the highlight section so the column reads as a continuous editorial rail. Each todo-item-row is stacked vertically within this list with 16–24px vertical gaps. The list’s top and bottom padding within the column are about 24–32px to give the collection of rows breathing space relative to the surrounding sections. As rows expand to reveal the todo-details-panel, the list naturally grows in height but maintains its centered, single-column appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrolling list; participates in normal document flow within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each todo-item-row appears as a full-width, card-like strip within the todos-list, spanning the entire width of the central column. It has generous internal padding—around 16px vertical and 24px horizontal—and sits on a subtly elevated or lightly outlined surface to distinguish it from the page background while still feeling minimal. Within the row, the todo-status-toggle is anchored to the far left, vertically centered. To the right of the toggle, the task title and key metadata (such as due date or category) are arranged in a left-aligned block, with the title on the top line and smaller, slightly letter-spaced meta text below. The right edge of the row may include a small, understated affordance (like a chevron) indicating that details can expand. Rows are separated from each other by 16–24px of vertical whitespace, preserving the airy editorial spacing. When a row’s details are expanded, the todo-details-panel appears immediately below it, indented to align with the text area of the row, visually linking the two elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the list; can expand vertically when paired with an immediately following details panel but does not move or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The todo-status-toggle is placed at the far left edge of each todo-item-row, acting as a visual anchor for the row. It sits on the same baseline as the first line of the task title, vertically centered within the row’s height. The control is compact—roughly 24–28px in size—so it feels delicate rather than heavy, echoing the circular gold logo and soft accents from the hero. There is a small horizontal gap of about 12–16px between the toggle and the task text block, ensuring the control is clearly separated but visually tied to its row. The toggle’s position remains fixed within the row regardless of the row’s height or whether the details panel is expanded below; it does not overlap or float outside the card boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline control within the row; no special positioning beyond left alignment inside the row’s padding box.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The todo-details-panel appears inline within the main content column as an expansion directly beneath the corresponding todo-item-row. It shares the same overall column width as the list but is slightly indented horizontally—about 16–24px—to create a subtle nested effect and emphasize its relationship to the row above. The panel’s background is a light, desaturated neutral (analogous to the hero’s light-gray title block) with a soft edge or faint border, and it uses generous padding of 24–32px on all sides. Form fields, notes, and secondary actions are arranged vertically with ample spacing between them (16–24px), keeping the panel from feeling like a dense form. When open, the details panel increases the vertical height of the list at that point but preserves the uninterrupted, top-to-bottom reading flow; no side-by-side columns are introduced here.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline expanding section; appears and disappears within the normal document flow directly below its parent row, pushing subsequent content down without overlaying it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The todo-remove-confirmation appears as a centered modal dialog that overlays the entire page. When invoked, a semi-transparent veil covers the hero, filters, highlights, and list, slightly dimming them but keeping their shapes subtly visible underneath to maintain context. The dialog itself is a clean, rectangular panel, about 360–420px wide on desktop and capped at roughly 80% of the viewport width on small screens. It is vertically centered within the viewport, with internal padding around 24–32px. The confirmation message is left-aligned within the panel, with comfortable line spacing, and the primary and secondary buttons are aligned horizontally at the bottom right or centered below the text, with 16–24px spacing between them. The overall look is minimal and calm, with no heavy borders, so it feels like a refined editorial overlay rather than a harsh system alert.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal centered in the viewport, with a full-screen dimmed backdrop that prevents interaction with underlying content while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The todos-filter-controls sit directly below the hero/tagline band, acting as the equivalent of the mockup’s social icon row. They are centered horizontally within the main content rail and begin about 32–48px beneath the tagline or divider line at the bottom of the hero. The controls are laid out in a single, horizontal row that spans roughly 60% of the content column width, leaving ample whitespace on both sides. Individual filter elements—such as dropdowns, pill-shaped toggles, or segmented buttons—are evenly spaced with 12–16px gaps, and the entire bar has minimal vertical height to keep it visually light. The bar has either no background or a very subtle underline to avoid feeling like a heavy toolbar, maintaining the refined editorial style while clearly signaling an interactive filter layer before the content list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal bar within the central column; scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-todos-highlight section is placed within the central content rail directly below the todos-filter-controls, starting approximately 48–64px beneath the filter row. It spans the same width as the main content column (around 60–70% of the page width) and is centered, visually echoing the large image-driven card at the top of the blog feed in the mockup. The highlight appears as a tall, card-like band—roughly 1/3 to 1/2 of the viewport height on initial view—with a soft background and possibly a subtle vertical accent or image strip, drawing attention to newly created or time-sensitive tasks. Inside, content is arranged vertically: a small, uppercase label (e.g., RECENTLY ADDED) sits at the top, followed by a larger heading and a short descriptive line, then a compact list or grid of the most recent tasks. Internal padding is generous (24–32px), and there is clear separation from the todos-list below (another 48–64px of vertical space), making this section feel like a featured editorial piece before the standard list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static featured block within the central column; scrolls with the page and does not overlay other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The todos-empty-state replaces the recent-todos-highlight and todos-list when there are no tasks to show. It is positioned in the central content rail immediately below the todos-filter-controls, beginning about 48–64px beneath the filters. The empty state occupies a generous vertical area—roughly the middle third of the viewport—so that it feels substantial but still surrounded by whitespace. It is centered both horizontally and, where possible, vertically within the available space below the hero. The content itself (icon or illustration, title, and explanatory text) is center-aligned, with the main message set in larger type similar in scale to a subheading on the blog, and a softer, smaller line of guidance beneath it. If there is a primary action (e.g., Add your first task), it appears as a single button centered below the text with about 24px of top margin. The overall effect is calm and inviting, reinforcing the editorial aesthetic rather than appearing like a technical error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static central block within the main column; scrolls with the page and only appears when the list is empty, taking the vertical space where the highlight and list would normally sit.

name: Budget Planner
title: Budget Planner
description: Manage your wedding budget by category, compare planned vs. actual amounts, review linked budgeting articles, and update allocations and notes.

core_features: Load and display the full budget overview grouped by categories using getBudgetPlannerOverview, including category metadata, planned and actual amounts, and any linked budget articles.
Show a top-level summary of total planned vs. total actual spend across all categories, derived from the allocations returned by getBudgetPlannerOverview.
Present each budget category (e.g., Catering, Venue, Decor) as an expandable section that displays its planned amount, actual amount, difference, and user notes.
Allow users to edit planned amount, actual amount, and notes for each category and persist changes via updateBudgetCategoryAllocation.
