List all budget article references per category using linked_articles_by_category from getBudgetPlannerOverview, showing article titles and key labels from the Article model.

Highlight and prioritize catering-related articles whose cached average_cost_per_guest (from BudgetArticleLink) is less than or equal to a configured threshold (for example, 60), especially within the Catering category section.
Allow users to remove a linked article from a category using removeBudgetArticleLink and immediately update the visible list of references for that category.
Support navigation to article detail pages by clicking linked article titles, passing the appropriate articleId parameter to article.html.
Honor the optional focusCategorySlug URL parameter on load, automatically scrolling to and expanding the matching category section and visually emphasizing it.
user_workflows: Review overall budget status: (1) User navigates to budget_planner.html (optionally with a focusCategorySlug parameter). (2) Page initialization calls getBudgetPlannerOverview with no parameters. (3) The response is used to render a category list and compute total planned and actual amounts. (4) User reviews the overall totals and per-category differences in the summary and category list.

Update a category’s planned and actual budget: (1) User identifies a category row and expands it if it is collapsed. (2) User edits the planned amount input or actual amount input and optionally updates notes. (3) On form submission or field blur (depending on interaction design), the component calls updateBudgetCategoryAllocation with category_slug, planned_amount, actual_amount, and notes from the current form state. (4) On successful response, the UI updates that category’s allocation values and notes using the returned BudgetCategoryAllocation and recomputes the overall totals.

Focus on a specific category from another page: (1) User clicks a link on index.html or article.html that points to budget_planner.html with focusCategorySlug set to a specific category slug (e.g., catering). (2) Budget Planner loads and calls getBudgetPlannerOverview. (3) After data loads, the component finds the category whose slug matches focusCategorySlug, expands its section, scrolls it into view, and applies a temporary highlight style. (4) User can immediately review or edit that category’s allocations and notes.

Use linked articles to refine the catering budget: (1) User opens the Catering category section, possibly auto-focused via focusCategorySlug=catering. (2) The UI lists all BudgetArticleLinks for category_slug=catering, with associated Article data. (3) Articles where average_cost_per_guest is defined and less than or equal to the configured threshold are visually emphasized (e.g., sorted first or tagged). (4) User clicks an article title, causing navigation to article.html with articleId set to the article.id. (5) After reading, the user can return to the Budget Planner and adjust catering planned_amount or notes based on the article and save changes via updateBudgetCategoryAllocation.

Remove an irrelevant budget article reference: (1) Within a category’s linked-article list, the user decides a particular article is no longer relevant. (2) User clicks a remove action on that article’s entry. (3) The component calls removeBudgetArticleLink with the BudgetArticleLink id. (4) On success, the article entry is removed from the in-memory list for that category, and the category panel re-renders without that reference.

Switch focus between categories and reflect in URL: (1) User clicks a different category in a sidebar or overview list. (2) The component expands the selected category panel, collapses the previously focused one if desired, and updates the browser’s URL to include focusCategorySlug for the newly focused category (using navigation or history APIs). (3) If the user refreshes the page or shares the URL, the page will re-open with that category focused via the focusCategorySlug handling logic.
interactions: Click on a category row or tab to expand or collapse that category’s details (allocation inputs, notes, linked article list).
Edit numeric inputs for planned_amount and actual_amount within a category and submit the changes, triggering updateBudgetCategoryAllocation for that category.
Edit the notes text area for a category and save changes, triggering updateBudgetCategoryAllocation with an updated notes field.
Click on an article title in a category’s linked-article list to navigate to article.html with articleId equal to the article’s id.
Click a remove or delete control next to a linked article entry to trigger removeBudgetArticleLink for that BudgetArticleLink id and update the list on success.

Hover or focus on a highlighted catering article with average_cost_per_guest at or below the threshold to see additional tooltip text sourced from Article fields (for example, budget_per_guest_label) when available.
Change the browser URL’s focusCategorySlug parameter through in-page navigation (such as clicking on a category name in a sidebar) to reflect the currently focused category without reloading data.
Use refresh/reload of the page to reapply the current focusCategorySlug and reconstruct the UI state using only data from getBudgetPlannerOverview and the URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load of budget_planner.html, the page reads the focusCategorySlug query parameter from the URL, if present. The page then calls getBudgetPlannerOverview without parameters and stores the returned categories, allocations, and linked_articles_by_category in memory. After data is loaded, the page derives a mapping from category_slug to BudgetCategory and BudgetCategoryAllocation and a mapping from category_slug to an array of BudgetArticleLink+Article pairs. If focusCategorySlug is present and matches a category_slug returned from the API, that category’s section is programmatically expanded, scrolled into view, and temporarily highlighted. Internal UI state tracks which category is currently focused and which sections are expanded. Whenever the user changes the focused category via interactions, the page updates the URL to set focusCategorySlug to the new category_slug using navigation or history APIs without introducing localStorage. When the user submits changes to a category’s planned_amount, actual_amount, or notes, updateBudgetCategoryAllocation is called. On success, the returned BudgetCategoryAllocation updates the in-memory allocations and any derived totals. When the user removes a linked article, removeBudgetArticleLink is called with the link id; upon a successful response, the link is removed from the in-memory linked_articles_by_category data for that category, and the UI is re-rendered. The page does not rely on any static data or localStorage; all persistent data is sourced from and updated through the assigned interfaces and reflected via URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Budget Planner page, parses URL parameters (including focusCategorySlug), calls getBudgetPlannerOverview, and orchestrates data flow between summary, category list, and detail components.

Displays aggregate totals and differences derived from all category allocations, such as total planned amount, total actual amount, and overall variance, recalculating whenever allocations are updated.

Renders a list of budget categories in a defined order, shows high-level planned vs. actual values for each, and allows users to select a category to focus or expand. It responds to focusCategorySlug by ensuring the corresponding category is brought into view and expanded.

Displays detailed information for a single selected category, including editable planned and actual amounts, notes, and a list of linked budget articles. It coordinates save operations through updateBudgetCategoryAllocation and updates the parent state with the returned allocation data.

Provides input controls for a category’s planned_amount, actual_amount, and notes. It prepares the payload and calls updateBudgetCategoryAllocation with category_slug and current form values, then propagates the updated BudgetCategoryAllocation back to parent components on success.

Shows all BudgetArticleLink entries for the active category along with their associated Article data, ordered and/or visually marked according to properties such as average_cost_per_guest. For the Catering category, entries with average_cost_per_guest defined and below or equal to the configured threshold are prioritized or highlighted.

Represents a single linked article for a category, showing the article title, any relevant budget labels, and a remove control. It routes navigation to article.html with the proper articleId and calls removeBudgetArticleLink when the remove control is activated.

Manages focusing logic for categories based on the focusCategorySlug URL parameter and user interactions. After data is loaded, it identifies the matching category, triggers expansion and scroll into view, and notifies other components of the focused category. It also updates the URL parameter when the user changes focus through the UI.

Centralizes calls to updateBudgetCategoryAllocation. It receives updated values from allocation forms, invokes updateBudgetCategoryAllocation with category_slug, planned_amount, actual_amount, and notes, and then dispatches the returned BudgetCategoryAllocation to update the shared allocation state and trigger UI recomputation of totals.

Handles removal of linked articles by calling removeBudgetArticleLink with the selected BudgetArticleLink id, updating the linked_articles_by_category state and notifying the linked-article-list component to re-render without the removed entry upon success.
Intercepts clicks on linked article titles and performs navigation to article.html, setting the articleId parameter in the URL based on the associated Article id, without altering budget data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the editorial blog hero pattern at the top: an asymmetric two-column hero band (logo-esque block on the left, page title block on the right), followed by a centered, single-column content rail. Within that central rail, the functional budget planner is organized vertically: first a horizontal budget overview summary bar, then a two-column planner workspace (category list on the left, category detail + allocation form on the right), and finally a linked-articles section presented as vertically stacked article rows.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the editorial blog hero pattern at the top: an asymmetric two-column hero band (logo-esque block on the left, page title block on the right), followed by a centered, single-column content rail. Within that central rail, the functional budget planner is organized vertically: first a horizontal budget overview summary bar, then a two-column planner workspace (category list on the left, category detail + allocation form on the right), and finally a linked-articles section presented as vertically stacked article rows.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero band at the top occupies roughly the top 40–45% of the initial viewport height, with generous white space around the logo and title. Below the hero, the main content rail is centered and spans about 70–80% of the page width on large screens, with 80–120px margins on each side. Vertical gaps between hero, budget summary, planner workspace, and linked articles are generous (48–64px). Within the planner workspace, the left category list uses roughly 35–40% of the content rail width and the right category detail panel uses 60–65%, mirroring the asymmetric hero proportions.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density stays low to medium. The hero, summary bar, and linked article rows are airy, with generous padding (24–32px) and open line spacing. The planner workspace is slightly denser for usability, but still respects the editorial feel: clear grouping, 16–24px padding inside cards and panels, and comfortable spacing between category items and form fields. No area feels crowded; there is consistent breathing room around all major blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the large, bold 'Budget Planner' title in the right hero column, then drifts left to the logo block, echoing the reverse-Z pattern of the original mockup. From there, the eye moves down the center to the budget overview summary bar, which acts as a visual and functional anchor. Next, the user’s focus splits into the two-column planner workspace, with the left category list acting as navigation and the right panel as the primary reading and editing area. After interacting with the planner, the eye continues straight down into the linked-article list, which resumes a vertical editorial reading flow similar to the main blog feed.

layout.overall_description: The Budget Planner page opens with a full-width hero band that echoes the blog’s home hero. On the left, occupying about 40% of the hero width, sits a softly framed logo panel: a roughly square, light background with the circular gold brand mark centered, surrounded by ample white space. On the right, taking up the remaining 60%, a tall, light-gray vertical block holds the large, dark page title “Budget Planner” in bold, modern type, with a delicate peach subtitle and small uppercase label above or beside it. This hero breathes, with generous top and bottom padding so it visually fills the top half of the first screen. Below, the design recenters into a wide, but clearly bounded central content rail. First in this rail is a horizontally oriented budget overview bar, spanning most of the central width and feeling like a slim, elegant card. Beneath that, the rail expands into a two-column planner workspace: on the left, a vertically stacked list of budget categories presented in a light panel; on the right, a larger, softly framed detail panel with planned vs. actual amounts, notes, and the allocation form, all aligned in a structured grid. This workspace sits in the middle third of the viewport, with generous white margins on both sides. Finally, below the planner, the layout returns to a pure editorial flow: a centered ‘Related Budget Guides’ style header followed by a vertical stack of linked article rows that echo blog cards—each row running nearly the full width of the content rail, with imagery and text laid out calmly. The overall impression is an elegant budgeting tool embedded seamlessly within a modern, romantic editorial experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page root defines a full-bleed canvas with the wedding blog’s editorial chrome and hero at the top and the planner content centered below. At the very top, it reserves a hero band that spans 100% of the viewport width and roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height. Inside that hero, a 12-column grid supports an asymmetric split: the left 5 columns contain a square-ish logo panel that is vertically centered within the hero and inset from the page edges by 80–120px; the right 7 columns contain a tall light-gray block behind the ‘Budget Planner’ title, subtitle, and label, also vertically centered with about 32–48px padding inside. Below the hero, the root centers a main content rail (about 70–80% of page width on desktop) with equal left and right margins (80–120px). This rail stacks the budget overview summary bar, the two-column planner workspace, and the linked-article list with 48–64px vertical spacing between each. On tablet and smaller screens, the hero collapses to a single column with the logo panel above the title block, and the main rail collapses to a single column so that the category list appears above the detail panel, maintaining generous vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static layout container; governs the 12-column grid and responsive breakpoints. The hero within it scrolls away naturally, with the main content rail flowing underneath. No fixed or sticky behavior at the page-root level.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget overview summary sits directly below the hero, aligned to the center of the page within the main content rail. It appears as a full-width card within that rail, spanning roughly 80–90% of the rail’s width so a thin margin of background shows on either side, reinforcing the editorial framing. The card has 24–32px padding on all sides and a subtle, light background tint to distinguish it from the page. Inside, content is arranged horizontally on desktop: high-level totals (Overall Budget, Planned, Actual, Remaining) run in a single row, left-aligned, with each metric grouped in equal-width segments. The overall title or label (e.g., ‘Budget Overview’) appears at the left edge of the bar; secondary labels or a small donut chart can sit toward the right. There is generous 16–24px spacing between metric blocks. On smaller screens, the summary card remains centered but its internal layout stacks vertically, with metrics listed one per row, preserving the airy feeling with comfortable vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll. Optionally can become a subtle sticky element at the top of the planner workspace section only (not at the very top of the viewport), staying visible while the user scrolls through the category detail, but default behavior is non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category list sits within the planner workspace directly below the budget overview summary, aligned to the left side of the central content rail. On desktop, it occupies approximately 35–40% of the rail’s width, running from just below the summary bar downwards, with its top edge horizontally aligned with the top of the category detail panel on the right. It appears as a tall, light, card-like panel with 24px internal padding and a soft border or shadow, extending as far down as the number of categories requires. A small, uppercase heading (e.g., ‘Categories’) is left-aligned at the top, followed by a vertical stack of category items. Each category item stretches nearly the full width of the panel, with comfortable vertical padding (12–16px) and 8–12px gaps between items, keeping the list legible and airy. The active or focused category is highlighted with a subtle background tint and perhaps a slender accent line on the left. On tablets and mobile, this list moves above the detail panel, becoming a full-width card centered in the rail, while maintaining the same vertical stack and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the planner workspace. It scrolls along with the page; on tall viewports it may fully show, while on smaller ones the list itself can scroll within the page, but it does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category detail panel is positioned to the right of the category list within the planner workspace, sharing the same horizontal band. On desktop, it takes up the remaining 60–65% of the content rail width. Its top aligns with the top of the category list, creating a clean horizontal edge, and its left padding begins about 24px away from the right edge of the category list, preserving a clear gutter between the two columns. This panel appears as a large, softly defined card with 24–32px internal padding. At the top, the selected category name is displayed in a bold, medium-large type, left-aligned, with a lighter subtitle or note line beneath it. Below, planned vs. actual amounts are arranged in a simple, grid-like layout: for example, two or three columns of labeled figures with ample spacing, possibly with a slim progress bar or comparative graphic beneath them. The allocation form continues below these summaries, treated as a cohesive vertical section. The panel’s height grows with content, typically extending further down than the category list; the bottom edge is allowed to flow naturally, creating a soft stagger between the two columns. On smaller screens, this panel becomes full-width and is stacked directly under the category list with a 32–48px gap, maintaining its card-like appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying its spot in the two-column workspace. It scrolls normally with the page; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The allocation form lives inside the lower portion of the category detail panel, forming its functional core. It is laid out as a vertically stacked set of fields that spans the full inner width of the panel minus padding, starting 24–32px below the summary metrics. Fields are grouped logically: first planned and actual amount inputs, then allocation breakdowns (e.g., line items), and finally a notes textarea. Each group is separated by 16–24px vertical spacing, with group labels in small uppercase or lightly tracked text, echoing the blog’s label style. Individual inputs are arranged primarily in a single column to maintain clarity, with occasional side-by-side pairs (e.g., Planned vs. Actual) that share a row and each take roughly half the usable width with 16px between them. Buttons for ‘Save allocation’ or similar actions are right-aligned at the bottom of the form (on desktop), with 16–24px space above them. On narrower screens, all elements collapse into a single column, with buttons expanding to full-width and aligning center to maintain the soft editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the category detail panel; it does not influence the overall page flow beyond expanding that panel’s height. It scrolls as part of the panel content with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The linked article list sits below the planner workspace as a separate, clearly defined band within the central content rail. There is a generous 48–64px vertical gap between the bottom of the planner (category detail panel) and the top of this list, emphasizing a transition from tool to inspiration. The section is centered and spans roughly 70–80% of the page width, aligned with the rail. At the top of the band, a centered, medium-sized heading such as ‘Related Budget Guides’ appears, with thin uppercase styling or subtle tracking, and 16–24px of space below it. Under the heading, linked article items are stacked vertically, each row occupying nearly the full width of this section, separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing. The overall band feels like a continuation of the blog’s article feed, but more compact and clearly associated with budget-related content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the normal scrolling document. The section stays in place and scrolls off-screen as the user moves up or down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each linked article item is a horizontally oriented row within the linked-article list, centered in the main content rail. On desktop, the row stretches nearly the full width of the section (around 90–100% of the linked-article list width) with 16–24px padding inside. The left side (about 30–35% of the row width) can hold a small rectangular or square thumbnail image with soft corners, aligned vertically in the middle of the row. The right side (about 65–70%) houses the article’s title, a brief one- or two-line excerpt, and a subtle meta label (e.g., ‘Budgeting Tips’), all left-aligned. The title uses medium-large type, with ample line spacing; the excerpt uses smaller body text with generous leading, ensuring readability. A delicate chevron or arrow icon may align to the far right to indicate navigability, but kept minimal to preserve the editorial aesthetic. On small screens, the image stacks above the text, all centered, with 16px vertical spacing between them and full-width text spanning the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row element; it does not stick or float. The entire row is clickable (or tap-able) as a navigation target, but spatially it behaves as a block inside the vertical list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual state controller that determines which budget category is currently selected. It influences the layout indirectly by updating the highlighted state of items in the category list and the content displayed in the category detail panel and allocation form. It does not occupy any physical space in the layout and has no visible representation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical; no spatial behavior. It runs in the background and only alters component states (e.g., which list item is highlighted, which panel content is shown) without changing structural layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is an invisible API controller responsible for saving updates from the allocation form and refreshing related summaries. It does not render any visual element, but its actions may cause numerical values in the budget overview summary and category detail panel to update. There is no direct visual footprint or reserved space for this controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It operates behind the scenes, triggered by form interactions, without affecting the page’s physical layout or scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual controller manages removing or detaching linked articles from the budget planner. When triggered (for example, from an icon or action inside a linked-article item), it updates which linked-article rows are rendered. It does not have any direct visual representation or reserved space in the UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical only; it modifies the collection of linked-article items but does not itself occupy any space or change sizing rules. The spatial effect is limited to linked-article rows appearing or disappearing from the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is an invisible navigation controller that handles transitions when a user clicks or taps on a linked article item. It does not display UI elements but initiates navigation to the chosen article, preserving the editorial feel by controlling how the new page is loaded. It occupies no layout space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-spatial; it simply routes user interactions to new pages or views without altering the structure or geometry of the current page.

name: Planning Board
title: Planning Board
description: Centralized view of saved timeline templates and seating layouts, with options to open their detail pages and remove them when no longer needed.

core_features: Retrieve and display all saved PlanningBoardItems (timeline templates and seating layouts) using getPlanningBoardItems.
Group and render each saved item as an interactive card showing its associated TimelineTemplate or SeatingLayout data.
Allow users to open a saved timeline template in timeline.html with the appropriate URL parameter when its card is clicked.
Allow users to open a saved seating layout in seating_layout.html with the appropriate URL parameter when its card is clicked.
Provide a per-item control to remove a PlanningBoardItem via removePlanningBoardItem, updating the list in place.
Support an optional highlight state for a specific PlanningBoardItem based on the highlightItemId URL parameter, e.g., after saving an item from a detail page.
user_workflows:
View all saved Planning Board items:
1. User navigates to planning_board.html (via header, footer, or direct link).
2. On page load, the page reads the highlightItemId URL parameter (if present).
3. The page calls getPlanningBoardItems with no parameters.
4. When the response arrives, the page renders a list/grid of item cards.
5. If highlightItemId matches a returned PlanningBoardItem.id, that card is visually highlighted and optionally scrolled into view.

Open a saved timeline template from the Planning Board:
1. User locates a timeline-type item card in the list (item_type = "timeline_template").
2. User clicks anywhere on the primary clickable area of the card (excluding the remove control).
3. The page reads the associated TimelineTemplate.id (from the item’s timeline_template object or planning_board_item.item_id).
4. The browser navigates to timeline.html with URL parameter timelineTemplateId set to that id.
5. timeline.html can then optionally redirect back to planning_board.html with highlightItemId after user actions on that page.

Open a saved seating layout from the Planning Board:
1. User locates a seating-layout-type item card in the list (item_type = "seating_layout").
2. User clicks the card’s main clickable area.
3. The page reads the associated SeatingLayout.id (from the item’s seating_layout object or planning_board_item.item_id).
4. The browser navigates to seating_layout.html with URL parameter seatingLayoutId set to that id.

Remove an item from the Planning Board:
1. User finds an item card they no longer need.
2. User clicks the remove control (e.g., an icon/button) on that card.
3. A confirmation interaction is shown (inline or dialog) asking the user to confirm removal.
4. If the user confirms, the page calls removePlanningBoardItem with planningBoardItemId set to planning_board_item.id for that card.
5. When removePlanningBoardItem returns success: true, the item is removed from the in-memory list of items and the UI re-renders without that card.
6. If the removed item was the one referenced by highlightItemId, the highlight state is cleared in the UI (the URL parameter can optionally be removed via history.replaceState).

Return from a detail page with a highlighted item:
1. On timeline.html or seating_layout.html, the user saves an item to the Planning Board through that page’s own functionality.
2. After saving, timeline.html or seating_layout.html navigates to planning_board.html including a highlightItemId URL parameter set to the PlanningBoardItem.id of the newly saved item.
3. planning_board.html loads, reads highlightItemId, and calls getPlanningBoardItems.
4. When items are rendered, the card whose planning_board_item.id matches highlightItemId is visually highlighted and optionally scrolled into view.
5. If the user navigates away from the highlight (e.g., clicks another item or dismisses a highlight cue), the page can remove the highlight state and optionally update the URL to remove highlightItemId.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- Event: Page load.
- Behavior: The root component reads the highlightItemId URL parameter. It then triggers getPlanningBoardItems. While waiting, a loading state is shown instead of the item list. When the API returns, the list component receives the items array and renders item cards.

Item card click (open detail):
- Target: Any item card main area (excluding dedicated controls like remove).
- Behavior for timeline_template items: On click, navigate to timeline.html?timelineTemplateId={associatedTimelineTemplateId}.
- Behavior for seating_layout items: On click, navigate to seating_layout.html?seatingLayoutId={associatedSeatingLayoutId}.
- The associatedTimelineTemplateId / associatedSeatingLayoutId is read from the item’s timeline_template.id or seating_layout.id, or fallback to planning_board_item.item_id.

Remove button click:
- Target: Remove control on an item card.
- Behavior: Stops propagation so it does not trigger navigation. Opens a confirmation interaction. If confirmed, calls removePlanningBoardItem with the planning_board_item.id. On success, the item is removed from the local items array and the list re-renders. If the call fails, an error message is displayed on or near the card.

Highlight behavior:
- On render: The list component compares each planning_board_item.id to the highlightItemId value from the URL. Matching card(s) are flagged as highlighted.
- Additional UX: After initial render, if there is a highlighted card, the component may scroll that card into view.
- Clearing highlight: If the user interacts with the page (e.g., clicks a different card, dismisses a highlight message, or removes the highlighted item), the component removes the highlight state from its internal state and may update the URL to remove highlightItemId via history.replaceState, without reloading the page.

Empty state handling:
- When getPlanningBoardItems returns an items array with length 0, the list component displays an empty-state message informing the user they have no saved Planning Board items yet. The page does not assume any specific data content; it only checks the items length.

Keyboard and accessibility interactions:
- Item cards are focusable elements. Pressing Enter or Space while focused on a card triggers the same behavior as a click (navigation to the appropriate detail page).
- The remove control is also focusable. Pressing Enter or Space while focused on the remove control triggers the removal confirmation flow.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state is the highlightItemId URL parameter. On every page load, the root component reads highlightItemId from the URL query string. This value is passed to the list component. After getPlanningBoardItems resolves, the list component compares highlightItemId against each planning_board_item.id in the items array. If a match is found, that card is given a highlighted state (e.g., via an isHighlighted flag in component state). If no match is found or highlightItemId is missing, no card is highlighted. The page state for items is maintained in memory: a single items array populated from getPlanningBoardItems. When removePlanningBoardItem succeeds, the corresponding PlanningBoardItem is removed from this items array; the page does not re-call getPlanningBoardItems unless explicitly desired, avoiding unnecessary API calls. Navigation to timeline.html and seating_layout.html passes IDs via URL parameters (timelineTemplateId or seatingLayoutId). The Planning Board page does not persist any state in localStorage or other client-side storage; all re-derivable state comes from URL parameters and the responses from getPlanningBoardItems and removePlanningBoardItem.

components.functionality: Root component that initializes page state, reads URL parameters, triggers data fetching, and composes all child components for the Planning Board.
Responsible for calling getPlanningBoardItems on initialization and passing the resulting items and loading/error states to child components.
Provides functional controls related to the list as a whole (e.g., display of total count, optional refresh action). It does not modify server-side data; it only affects client-side representation.

Renders the collection of PlanningBoardItem entries as a list or grid of cards. Accepts items from the data-loader and the current highlightItemId, and determines which card is highlighted. Manages empty-state rendering when there are no items.

Displays a single PlanningBoardItem with its associated TimelineTemplate or SeatingLayout details. It exposes separate interactive regions for opening the detail page and for removing the item. It also handles the visual highlighted state when its planning_board_item.id matches highlightItemId.

Handles user confirmation before removing a PlanningBoardItem. It receives the target planning_board_item.id from the item card, and when confirmed, triggers removePlanningBoardItem. It communicates success or failure back to the list/root component.
Displays a message when getPlanningBoardItems returns an empty items array. It is only rendered when the list length is 0 and the data-loader is not in a loading or error state.

Manages the highlight state derived from the highlightItemId URL parameter. It determines which PlanningBoardItem (if any) should be highlighted, instructs the list to apply visual highlighting, and can clear the highlight state and optionally update the URL (removing highlightItemId) after certain user interactions.

Shows error messages when getPlanningBoardItems or removePlanningBoardItem fail, without hardcoding any data content from the APIs. It only displays generic error text or formatted error messages passed in from the data-loader or confirmation dialog.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The Planning Board page uses the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup: a hero-like toolbar band at the very top (acting as the asymmetric hero), followed by a centered tagline/divider band, then a compact control row (filters/sorting behaving like the social bar), and finally a vertically stacked list of planning item cards in a single central content rail. Error and empty states live inside this central rail, while overlays (loader and confirmation dialog) float on top without breaking the underlying structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Planning Board page uses the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup: a hero-like toolbar band at the very top (acting as the asymmetric hero), followed by a centered tagline/divider band, then a compact control row (filters/sorting behaving like the social bar), and finally a vertically stacked list of planning item cards in a single central content rail. Error and empty states live inside this central rail, while overlays (loader and confirmation dialog) float on top without breaking the underlying structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero-style toolbar occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport, split into an asymmetric two-column layout (about 40–45% width for the logo/info block on the left and 55–60% for the title and summary block on the right). Below, the tagline/divider and control row together take about 10–15% of the vertical space but remain visually light. The items list and cards fill the remaining vertical space, constrained to a wide but clearly bounded central column (~70–80% of the page width, max-width around 960–1120px) with generous side gutters. Each section is separated by 48–64px vertical spacing; cards have 24–32px internal padding and 32–48px gaps between them.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is kept low-to-medium, mirroring an editorial wedding blog: large typography for titles and section labels, generous whitespace around the toolbar/hero, wide line spacing for descriptions, and clear separation between cards. Controls (filters, counts, actions) are present but visually quiet, using light lines and subtle pastel accents so they support, rather than crowd, the main content. Functional states (errors, empty state, loader) are padded generously and never butt directly against other components.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the right-aligned bold page title within the toolbar hero block, then moves left to the circular/crest-like Planning Board emblem, echoing the mockup’s reverse Z-pattern. From there, the eye is guided downward to a centered tagline band with a thin horizontal rule, then to a centered row of small controls/filters analogous to the social icons. The eye then naturally follows the vertical column of large, image-forward cards. When present, the error display appears near the top of the content rail just under the hero, briefly intercepting attention without overpowering the main title; the confirmation dialog and loader overlay temporarily become primary focal points when active.

layout.overall_description: The Planning Board page opens with a spacious, editorial hero-style toolbar that stretches across the central content column and fills nearly half of the initial viewport height. On the left, a softly framed, nearly square panel houses a circular gold-toned emblem or icon representing the Planning Board, set against a muted pastel background and centered vertically. On the right, a taller, light-gray block sits behind a large, all-caps “Planning Board” title and a softer description line, with subtle labels or counts arranged around it, creating the same asymmetric two-column tension as the original blog hero. Below this band, the layout recenters: a thin horizontal divider line spans the width of the content rail, with a short, elegant tagline about saved timelines and seating layouts centered within it. Just beneath, a slender horizontal row of small, minimal controls (view toggles, filters, or sorting) is centered and visually light, evoking the social icon bar. The main content column then flows downward into a stack of wide, image-led planning item cards, each aligned to the central rail with generous white space between them. Any errors are surfaced as a slim, calm banner just below the hero; an empty state becomes a gently illustrated, centered message in place of the list; and transient overlays like the loader and confirmation dialog float above the dimmed page, maintaining the serene, romantic, and uncluttered aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The root container spans the full viewport width and height, with a soft, warm neutral or very pale pastel background. A central content rail is established using a max-width of approximately 1120px and horizontal margins of 80–120px on large screens, so the main page sits visually as a wide, centered column surrounded by airy gutters. All primary child sections (toolbar hero, tagline band, controls row, list/errors/empty state) are stacked vertically with 48–64px spacing between them, creating a calm editorial rhythm. On tablet and mobile, the root keeps everything single-column, reducing side margins to 16–24px while preserving generous vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal, scrollable page container establishing the 12-column grid and central content rail; it is not fixed or sticky and simply frames all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader presents as a semi-transparent white or very pale pastel overlay that covers the main content rail from just below the top toolbar hero down to the bottom edge of the viewport, leaving the page header still visible to anchor the user. Within this overlay, a small, elegant spinner or progress indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically in the visible area, with a short, light-weight status label beneath it. The overlay respects the same horizontal bounds as the content rail (it does not stretch edge-to-edge across the entire browser width), maintaining the constrained editorial feel. Underlying content remains faintly visible through the veil, reinforcing continuity while clearly indicating a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an absolute-positioned overlay attached to the root content rail, covering the toolbar’s lower portion and the list area; not sticky during scroll (the page behind can still scroll if needed but is typically locked while loading).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar sits at the very top of the central content rail and functions as the hero band, occupying roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height. It is organized as an asymmetric two-column layout aligned to an implicit 12-column grid: on desktop, the left column spans about 5 of 12 columns (40–45% of the content rail width), and the right spans about 7 of 12 columns (55–60%). The left side contains a nearly square panel, vertically centered within the band, with 32px internal padding; inside, a circular gold or soft metallic emblem and a delicate script or monogram are centered, echoing the wedding logo feel. The right side features a tall, light-gray or very pale neutral background block running nearly the full height of the hero, with 32px padding: towards the top, a small uppercase label (e.g., “PLANNING BOARD”) sits subtly, below which a large, bold, high-contrast page title is right-aligned or slightly right-weighted. Beneath the title, a softer peach-toned line of copy describes the page (e.g., “Your saved timelines and seating layouts in one place”), followed by a quiet row for high-level stats and key actions aligned along the bottom edge of the block. On smaller screens, this hero collapses into a single column: the typographic block appears first, full-width, followed by the emblem panel beneath it, both still padded generously.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow at the top of the page; not sticky by default. It defines the hero region and the top of the scrollable content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The items list occupies the core of the centered content rail below the hero, starting approximately 48–64px under the toolbar. At the very top of this section, a thin, full-width horizontal rule is drawn within the content rail; in its center sits a short, uppercase or small-caps tagline (e.g., “Saved timelines & layouts”) in a fine serif or clean sans-serif, flanked on both sides by the line, closely mirroring the mockup’s tagline treatment. Directly below, separated by 16–24px, a slim horizontal strip of subtle controls (such as filter chips, a view toggle, or sort selector) is centered; this strip is visually delicate, with light icons and fine typography, echoing the social icon bar’s proportion and lightness. The list itself is a vertical stack of item cards aligned to the same central column, each card spanning 100% of the content rail width (minus any inner padding), with 32–48px vertical gaps between cards to maintain an airy editorial rhythm. On long lists, this section scrolls naturally as part of the page; the tagline and control strip scroll off with the content, leaving no fixed elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered as a standard scrolling section in the main flow, constrained to the central content rail; it does not use sticky behavior. It expands vertically as items are added.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each planning board item card is a large, image-forward block that fills the width of the items list’s central column, with 24–32px internal padding and softly rounded corners. At the top of the card, a prominent image or thumbnail representing the timeline or seating layout spans nearly the full card width with a height approximating a 4:3 or slightly taller aspect ratio; the image is edge-to-edge within the card’s inner padding, evoking the large portrait photos in the blog mockup. Beneath the image, a text block with 24px top padding contains a clear, bold title and a softer subtitle or key details line (e.g., guest count, date, layout type), set with generous line spacing. Along the bottom, a thin divider or subtle spacing separates primary actions (such as “Open” and a quiet “Remove” link) which are right-aligned and kept minimal so they don’t overpower the content. Cards are surrounded by ample white space above and below, with a faint shadow or low-contrast border to distinguish them from the background while preserving the minimalist, romantic aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal vertical stacking of the list; cards do not overlap or float. On hover (desktop), the card may slightly elevate with a subtle shadow or border emphasis, but remains fixed within the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove confirmation appears as a centered modal dialog overlaying the entire viewport, dimming the page beneath with a soft, translucent dark veil that still allows the hero and cards to be faintly visible. The dialog itself is a compact, vertically oriented panel approximately 360–480px wide on desktop, with 24–32px of internal padding, rounded corners, and a neutral or slightly warm background. The message is center-aligned with a short, clear heading (“Remove from Planning Board?”) and one or two lines of descriptive text below, set with comfortable line height. At the bottom, two horizontally arranged buttons (primary confirm in a slightly stronger tone, secondary cancel in an outline or ghost style) are right-aligned on desktop but may stack vertically with 16px spacing on mobile, preserving the elegant spacing. The modal sits visually above all other elements and does not attempt to align strictly to the underlying grid, functioning as a floating editorial card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered as a fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, independent of scroll. The background content is inert while the modal is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no items, the empty state content replaces the items list area but maintains the same central column alignment and spacing from the hero. It is vertically positioned so that its main content sits slightly above the vertical midpoint of the viewport (roughly 40% from the top), which feels intentional and balanced. The empty state consists of a small, soft illustration or icon at the top (possibly a light outline of a seating chart or calendar) followed by a moderately large heading and a short, gentle explanatory paragraph, all center-aligned. Below the text, a single primary call-to-action button is centered with ample margin above and below, giving the whole block the feel of a featured editorial panel rather than a technical message. The surrounding space is generous, with no competing UI elements nearby, preserving the calm, romantic tone even when the board is empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline where the items list would normally render, within the main scroll area. It does not overlay other content and scrolls with the page if additional content is present above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight manager subtly enhances specific item cards without changing their layout position. When a card is highlighted (for example, after creation or as a result of an action), that card receives a soft peach or blush-colored border or glow, slightly thicker than the usual hairline border, and the background inside the card may warm by a very subtle amount. Non-highlighted cards remain at normal opacity to maintain the low-density, relaxed aesthetic; at most, they may dim by a small percentage to gently direct attention without creating harsh contrast. This effect is always applied within the existing card bounds and grid, ensuring the overall stack and spacing remain perfectly consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a behavioral layer applied to item cards rather than a discrete visual block; it does not occupy its own space and simply modifies the styling of cards in the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error display appears as a slim, full-width banner within the central content rail, positioned directly beneath the toolbar hero and above the tagline/divider band, with 24–32px vertical spacing separating it from both. It uses a pale rose or peach-tinted background with a fine border and rounded corners, visually soft and in keeping with the wedding aesthetic, and spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (matching the content rail) so it feels integrated rather than intrusive. Error text is left-aligned within the banner, with a concise message and optionally a secondary line for details; a small close icon sits at the top-right corner inside the banner’s padding. The height of the banner is modest, and ample white space above and below prevents it from competing with the main hero or the list, allowing it to be noticed but not dominate the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered inline in the normal document flow; it pushes the tagline and list downward when visible and disappears without leaving a gap when dismissed. It does not stick to the top on scroll.

name: Reading List
title: Reading List
description: Displays the user’s saved wedding planning articles, with options to filter by category, open full articles, and remove items from the list.

core_features: Load and display all saved articles for the current user using getReadingList, optionally filtered by categoryId from the URL.
Show key article information for each saved item, including title, category name, summary or metadata, saved date, and any user note stored on the SavedArticle.
Allow users to open a saved article in the Article Detail view by navigating to article.html with the corresponding articleId.
Allow users to remove articles from the reading list using removeArticleFromReadingList and update the list immediately after removal.
Provide category-based filtering controls that update the categoryId URL parameter and reload the reading list using getReadingList with the selected category.
Optionally group visible saved articles by category based on the category_name or article.category_id data returned by getReadingList.
Provide per-category or per-item links to browse more articles in that category by navigating to articles.html with the selectedCategoryId as categoryId.
user_workflows: View Reading List: (1) User navigates to reading_list.html (with or without a categoryId parameter). (2) On page load, the page reads categoryId from the URL (if present). (3) The page calls getReadingList with category_id set to the URL categoryId (or an empty/undefined value if not provided). (4) The page displays the returned items, optionally grouped by category, along with total_count. (5) If no items are returned, the page shows an empty-state message.

Filter Reading List by Category: (1) User opens reading_list.html and sees category filter controls derived from the categories present in the loaded items or from the current filter. (2) User selects a category filter or clears the current filter. (3) The component updates the browser URL to include or update ?categoryId=<selectedCategoryId> (or remove it when clearing). (4) The page calls getReadingList again with category_id matching the updated URL. (5) The list re-renders to show only items in the selected category and updates any grouping or headings accordingly.

Open a Saved Article: (1) User scans the reading list and clicks on the title (or a dedicated "Open" action) of a saved article. (2) The click handler reads the associated article.id from the item’s data. (3) The browser navigates to article.html?articleId=<article.id>. (4) The Reading List page is left, and the Article Detail page handles display.

Remove an Article from Reading List: (1) User finds an article they no longer want saved. (2) User clicks a "Remove" control associated with the saved item. (3) The click handler calls removeArticleFromReadingList with savedArticleId equal to the SavedArticle.id of that item. (4) When the interface responds with success = true, the item is removed from the in-memory list and the UI updates immediately. (5) The remaining_count value from the response is used to update any count display and to determine if the empty-state view should be shown when remaining_count is 0.

Browse More Articles in a Category from Reading List: (1) User views their saved items, grouped or labeled by category. (2) For a category heading or an individual item, the user clicks a "Browse more in this category" link. (3) The click handler reads the category identifier (e.g., article.category_id) for that context. (4) The browser navigates to articles.html?categoryId=<selectedCategoryId>. (5) The Articles page displays more content in that category, allowing the user to discover new articles to save.
interactions: On page load, a page-initialization interaction reads the categoryId query parameter from the URL and triggers getReadingList with category_id set to that value (or left undefined if no filter).

When the user changes the category filter (via dropdown, pill, or tab control), an interaction updates the categoryId parameter in the URL and triggers a fresh call to getReadingList to re-populate the list.
Clicking on a saved article’s title triggers navigation to article.html with articleId set to the corresponding Article.id from the item returned by getReadingList.

Clicking a "Remove" action on an item calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the SavedArticle.id and, on success, removes that item from the currently displayed collection without requiring a full page reload.
Clicking a "Browse more in this category" link for a category (or specific item’s category) triggers navigation to articles.html with categoryId set to the associated category value.
If getReadingList returns an error or no data, an interaction displays an error or empty-state message, and hides list-specific controls that rely on items being present.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of state for this page is the optional category filter, represented by the categoryId query parameter in the reading_list.html URL. On every page load or URL change: (1) The page reads window.location.search to extract categoryId. (2) The page calls getReadingList with category_id set to the extracted categoryId; when categoryId is absent or empty, category_id is passed as undefined or omitted to retrieve the full reading list. (3) The response from getReadingList (total_count and items) becomes the in-memory state for the list. Any grouping by category is computed client-side from item.category_name or item.article.category_id. (4) When the user interacts with the category filter controls, the page updates the URL (adding, changing, or removing categoryId) and then re-runs the same initialization logic to keep URL and view in sync. (5) When removeArticleFromReadingList is called and returns success, the page mutates the in-memory list state to remove that SavedArticle and decrements any displayed count to match remaining_count, without using localStorage. If the URL’s categoryId does not match any category present in the returned items (e.g., no saved items in that category), the page still treats the URL as the source of truth and simply shows an empty list and an option to clear or change the filter.

components.functionality: Initializes the Reading List page by reading the categoryId URL parameter, calling getReadingList with category_id, and storing the resulting total_count and items in page-level state used by other components.

Displays and manages the current category filter for the reading list. It can show the active filter (derived from the categoryId URL parameter) and available category options inferred from the categories present in the loaded items. Changing the selection updates the URL’s categoryId and triggers re-loading of the list.

Shows a summary of the reading list based on total_count and the current filter, such as the number of saved items and which category (if any) is being viewed. It updates automatically when getReadingList returns new data or when an item is removed.

Renders the saved articles returned by getReadingList, optionally grouped by category. For each group, it displays a category heading (using category_name or a label derived from article.category_id) and a list of items. Each item shows article title, key metadata (such as read time if available), the saved_at value, and any note from SavedArticle.note, along with controls for opening the article, removing it from the reading list, and browsing more articles in that category.

Displays a message and simple guidance when getReadingList returns total_count = 0 or an empty items array for the current category filter. Offers an option to clear the category filter (by removing categoryId from the URL) so the user can see other saved items if they exist in different categories.

Encapsulates the actions available for each saved article, including open, remove from reading list, and browse more in this category. It receives SavedArticle and Article data for the item and wires up the appropriate interface calls and navigations.

Handles the remove operation when the user chooses to remove a saved article. It calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the relevant SavedArticle.id, processes the response, updates the in-memory collection of items to reflect the removal, updates displayed counts using remaining_count, and triggers the empty-state view if no items remain.

Defines and manages navigation behaviors for this page: navigating to article.html with articleId when a title is clicked, and navigating to articles.html with categoryId when the user clicks a "Browse more in this category" link.

Shows transient success or error messages related to operations on the reading list, such as confirming that an article was removed successfully or indicating when a remove operation fails. Messages are driven by the outcomes of calls to removeArticleFromReadingList and by any errors encountered when calling getReadingList.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s editorial top-to-bottom flow. A hero band at the top uses the asymmetric two-column layout: on the left, the brand logo block; on the right, a tall typographic block with the 'Reading List' title and supporting labels. Below the hero, the layout recenters into a single central rail: first a slim summary/tagline band with a centered line + text treatment, then a horizontal filter bar occupying the same central width. Under that, the main reading list (or empty state) appears as vertically stacked content cards within the same centered column. Navigation links sit at the bottom of this column as a light, tertiary band. Feedback messages and action handlers float above or within this structure without disturbing the main editorial flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s editorial top-to-bottom flow. A hero band at the top uses the asymmetric two-column layout: on the left, the brand logo block; on the right, a tall typographic block with the 'Reading List' title and supporting labels. Below the hero, the layout recenters into a single central rail: first a slim summary/tagline band with a centered line + text treatment, then a horizontal filter bar occupying the same central width. Under that, the main reading list (or empty state) appears as vertically stacked content cards within the same centered column. Navigation links sit at the bottom of this column as a light, tertiary band. Feedback messages and action handlers float above or within this structure without disturbing the main editorial flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport on desktop, with the left logo panel taking about 40–45% of the hero width and the right text panel 55–60%. Below it, the summary header and filter bar together use about another 10–15% of the viewport height but remain visually light. The main reading list column then consumes the remaining vertical space and naturally extends below the fold. Horizontally, all post-related content (summary header, filters, list, empty state, navigation links) is confined to a centered column spanning about 60–70% of the viewport, with generous side margins (80–120px) on large screens to maintain the airy editorial feel. Internal paddings for sections and cards mostly sit in the 24–32px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low to medium. Each band (hero, summary, filters, list) has generous vertical spacing between it and the next—around 48–64px. Individual cards in the reading list prioritize large imagery and readable typography with ample line spacing. Filters, navigation links, and item controls are visually light and compact but never cramped: they have enough breathing room to feel deliberate and refined. There is no crowded data-dense table; instead, the saved articles appear as spacious, image-led cards.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the bold 'Reading List' title in the right hero column, then subtly pulled back to the left logo block, mirroring the mockup’s reverse Z-pattern. From there, the user’s gaze moves downward to the centered summary/tagline framed by a dividing line, then to the horizontal filter bar immediately beneath it. Next, the eye travels down the page into the first reading list card image and title, establishing a strong vertical reading flow through the stacked cards. Within each card, the eye flows from image to title and metadata, then to the item controls at the bottom right. Feedback messages appear near the top center as lightweight overlays, briefly intercepting attention without disrupting the core flow.

layout.overall_description: The Reading List page opens with a spacious hero band spanning the full width of the viewport. On the left, about two-fifths of the hero is occupied by a softly framed logo panel with the circular gold mark and ample white space around it; on the right, a taller vertical light-gray panel sits behind the bold 'Reading List' heading and its subtle labels, echoing the blog’s main hero but tailored to saved articles. Beneath this, the layout recenters into a single, wide yet contained column. A thin horizontal rule stretches most of the page width, with a concise summary of the user’s saved items sitting centered along the line, like a caption in an editorial spread. Just below, a horizontal filter bar aligns to this same central column, presenting category chips or tabs in a single row with lots of breathing room. Scrolling further, the main content column displays grouped saved-article cards: each card features a generous image at the top, followed by title, wedding-related metadata, and slim item controls aligned at the bottom. Cards are separated by roomy vertical gaps, making the column feel like a curated reading list rather than a dense archive. If the list is empty, this central area is taken over by a calm, illustration-anchored empty state. At the bottom of the column, understated navigation links provide paths back to the main blog or discovery pages. Throughout, feedback messages appear as delicate, centered toasts near the top of the viewport, and all components respect wide side margins and a consistent, airy rhythm that mirrors the original wedding blog mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component occupies the full viewport width and sets up the main vertical stack. At the very top it defines the hero band, using the same asymmetric two-column structure as the mockup. Within this hero, the left column spans roughly 40–45% of the page width on large screens and holds a roughly square logo block: centered vertically within this area, the circular gold mark appears against a soft, off-white background with about 32–48px padding on all sides. The right column spans the remaining 55–60% of the width and contains a tall, light-gray vertical panel with 32px internal padding; inside it, the 'Reading List' title is set in large, bold type near the upper-left of this panel, with a smaller uppercase label (e.g., 'SAVED ARTICLES') above or to the upper-right, and a soft-colored sublabel underneath describing the purpose of the page. The hero has generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) to occupy around 40–50% of the initial viewport height on desktop. Below the hero, the page-init constrains subsequent content into a centered content rail that is approximately 60–70% of the viewport width (with min/max widths around a typical reading column), aligned to the vertical center line of the page and flanked by wide white margins. All other components (summary header, filters, list, empty state, navigation, feedback) are positioned within this central rail or floating above it, establishing the editorial spine of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the global layout grid and scrolling behavior. The hero scrolls away normally as the user moves down the page; no part of the hero is sticky. The content rail remains centered and responsive, collapsing to a single-column layout on smaller screens where the hero’s two columns stack vertically (logo panel on top, title block beneath) with consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary header sits immediately below the hero within the central content rail, acting as a visual bridge between the hero and the functional elements below. It takes the form of a full-width horizontal divider line with a centered text capsule. The thin line spans roughly 80–90% of the central column width and is positioned at the vertical center of a band about 64px tall. At the center of this line, the summary text (e.g., '12 articles in your reading list') breaks the line, with a small amount of horizontal inset padding (about 8–12px on each side of the text), causing the line to appear as two subtle segments extending to the left and right. The text is center-aligned and uses a refined, slightly letter-spaced style, echoing the tagline treatment from the mockup. There is about 32px of vertical space above and below this summary header band before the next component, keeping it light and unobtrusive while still clearly anchoring the start of the reading list area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page within the central content column. It does not stick to the viewport, but its strong horizontal line acts as a visual breakpoint between the hero and the interactive content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter bar is positioned directly beneath the summary header, aligned to the same central content rail. It occupies a horizontal band roughly 72–96px tall, with 16–24px of padding above and below the filter controls themselves. The filters (chips, pills, or tabs) are centered horizontally in a single row; the row maxes out at about 80% of the column width so that there is comfortable white space on both sides. Individual filter pills have 12px vertical and 24px horizontal padding, with generous 16px gaps between them, creating a light and airy row similar in visual weight to the social icon bar in the mockup. On larger screens, filters are laid out in a single centered line; on narrower screens, they wrap to a second line while remaining center-aligned. The bar itself has a soft bottom edge, with about 32–40px of vertical space separating it from the first reading list group below, reinforcing the hierarchy: hero → summary → filters → content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default the filter bar scrolls with the content, but it is visually separated enough to be quickly re-found when scrolling back up. Optionally, on taller viewports it may become sticky within the central column after the hero scrolls off-screen, staying pinned just below the top edge of the viewport; if implemented this way, it remains centered and does not stretch full-bleed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The grouped reading list occupies the main body of the page, sitting directly beneath the filter bar inside the central content rail. It spans the full width of that rail (roughly 60–70% of viewport width, bounded by wide side margins) and extends vertically as needed. Groups (e.g., by category or date) are stacked from top to bottom, each beginning with a subtle group heading aligned to the left edge of the column. Group headings use small, letter-spaced uppercase text with 16–24px of vertical margin above and 8–12px below. Within each group, saved article cards are stacked vertically with generous spacing: each card is separated by about 32–40px of vertical gap, so individual items feel like distinct editorial entries. Each card is structured like a compact blog post: a large, image-led header spans nearly the full card width at the top (with 24–32px of internal padding around the content), followed by the article title in a bold but not oversized style, then secondary metadata (date, category, reading time). At the bottom of each card, aligned to the bottom-right, sit the item controls. The cards themselves have 24px internal padding and soft corners, and they never touch each other or the column edges; there is always visible whitespace around them. The first card near the top of the list can optionally have a slightly taller, portrait-oriented image to echo the mockup’s prominent first article image, giving the list a visually engaging entry point. Overall, the list reads as a vertical editorial feed, with ample white space maintaining the gentle, romantic aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The grouped list scrolls as the main content of the page. It remains firmly anchored to the central column and never becomes full-bleed. On smaller screens, cards stretch to nearly full screen width (while respecting small side padding), and groups simply stack one after another in a single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same central content area as the grouped list and appears only when there are no saved articles. It is vertically centered within the visible portion of the central column when possible, or otherwise sits high enough that it is fully visible without scrolling on common viewport heights. The empty state container is about 60–70% of the column width (slightly narrower than the list to feel more intimate), centered horizontally. At the top of this container, there may be a soft, minimal illustration or icon related to wedding planning, occupying roughly the upper third of the container’s height, with ample white space around it. Below the illustration, a concise headline is center-aligned, followed by a short explanatory text block with comfortable line spacing and a maximum line length of about 60 characters. A primary call-to-action button (e.g., 'Browse wedding articles') is placed beneath the text, centered and surrounded by at least 24–32px of whitespace. The entire empty state block has a clear buffer from other elements: 48–64px above (from the filter bar or summary header) and similar spacing below (before the navigation links), reinforcing its role as a calm, standalone message rather than part of a feed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the grouped list; when displayed, it replaces the list within the central content column. It scrolls with the page but is typically short enough that most of it is visible above the fold on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The item controls live within each reading list card, at the bottom edge, providing actions like 'Open article' and 'Remove from list'. Within the card’s internal layout, they sit in a horizontal row aligned to the bottom-right corner, opposite any metadata that may sit at the bottom-left. There is a clear separation from the main text: at least 16–24px of vertical space between the article description and the row of controls. The controls themselves use small, refined buttons or text links with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, maintaining the minimal aesthetic. A primary action (e.g., 'Read full article') appears slightly more prominent, while secondary actions (like 'Remove') are outlined or ghost-style to keep the card from feeling aggressive. Buttons are spaced with about 8–12px gaps between them. On narrow screens, the controls row remains right-aligned if space allows; if not, it wraps gracefully to a second line while maintaining clear separation from the content above. The visual weight of these controls is intentionally light, so they are discoverable but do not compete with the imagery and titles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The controls are fully contained within each card and scroll with the corresponding list item. They do not stick or float; their position is always anchored to the bottom region of the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove action handler does not present a visible UI element of its own but underpins the interaction of the 'Remove from list' controls within each card. Visually, its presence is reflected only indirectly: when a user triggers a remove action, the corresponding card subtly animates out of the list (e.g., a brief fade and vertical collapse), and the remaining cards gently slide up to fill the space, maintaining the smooth editorial flow. Optionally, a transient confirmation could appear near the top-center of the viewport via the feedback-messages component, but the handler itself remains invisible in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral and non-spatial. It affects layout only by removing list items and causing the list to reflow; there is no dedicated space reserved for it on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links appear near the bottom of the central content column, beneath either the grouped list or the empty state. They inhabit a slim horizontal band about 64px tall, starting roughly 48px below the last card or empty state content. The links are center-aligned and laid out in a single row on large screens, with 24–32px gaps between each link to keep them airy. Each link uses understated, uppercase or small-caps typography with subtle letter-spacing and no heavy button styling, echoing the minimalist link treatments of an editorial footer. They do not span the full width; instead, the row itself is only as wide as its content, sitting comfortably in the middle of the column with ample white space to the left and right. On smaller screens, links can wrap to a staggered second line while remaining centered. This band serves as a gentle, tertiary navigation aid—paths like 'Browse all articles', 'Back to blog', or 'Explore categories'—without competing with the primary content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page, effectively marking the end of the central content column. It is not sticky and does not form a full-page footer; it simply closes out the reading list section in a light, editorial manner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Feedback messages appear as transient, lightweight overlays near the top-center of the viewport, aligned horizontally with the central content column rather than extending full-bleed. Each message is contained in a pill or slim card with 16–20px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding, subtle shadow or border, and soft corners, maintaining the refined aesthetic. Visually, they sit just below the browser’s top edge—often overlapping the lower edge of the hero or the upper part of the content column—without obscuring critical text. When multiple messages are needed, they stack vertically with 8–12px gaps, but the total height remains modest so as not to dominate the view. Text inside is center-aligned, concise, and uses a slightly smaller type size than body text to keep them understated. Color accents (e.g., soft greens for success, muted peaches for info) tie back to the wedding palette.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Messages float above the page content and are not part of the normal document flow. They appear and disappear after a short duration or user dismissal, without shifting underlying layout. On very small screens they anchor to the top center with full-width padding so they remain readable but still feel like overlays, not full banners.

name: My Projects
title: My Projects
description: Page for viewing and managing all saved DIY & decor projects, including opening tutorials, updating project status, and removing projects.

core_features: Load and display all saved DIY projects using getMyProjects, showing key details from the associated DIYProject and status from SavedProject.
