Highlight a specific saved project (if provided by the highlightSavedProjectId URL parameter) to confirm it was just added from a DIY project detail page.
Allow users to change each saved project's status (planned, in_progress, completed) using updateSavedProjectStatus.
Allow users to remove projects from the My Projects list using removeSavedProject.
Enable users to reopen a project tutorial by clicking on the project title, navigating to diy_project.html with the correct diyProjectId URL parameter.
Optional status-based filtering and sorting of the visible list, controlled via URL parameters, applied client-side on the getMyProjects result.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing My Projects after adding a new project
1. User finishes adding a DIY project on diy_project.html and clicks a link or button that navigates to projects.html, including the highlightSavedProjectId parameter set to the new SavedProject.id.
2. My Projects page loads and the my-projects-page-controller component reads highlightSavedProjectId from the URL.
3. my-projects-page-controller calls getMyProjects with no parameters.
4. When the projects array is returned, the controller passes it to the project-list component.
5. highlight-saved-project-handler receives the projects array and highlightSavedProjectId, finds the matching SavedProject, and marks it as highlighted in the page state (for example, via a transient flag on that item and/or auto-scrolling).
6. User visually confirms the highlighted project in the list. The highlight is purely UI state and is not written back to any API.

Workflow: Changing a project’s status
1. User visits projects.html (with or without URL parameters). my-projects-page-controller calls getMyProjects and populates project-list.
2. Within project-list, user locates a project and uses the project-status-control (e.g., a dropdown or set of buttons) to select a different status value from the allowed SavedProject.status enum (planned, in_progress, completed).
3. project-status-control triggers updateSavedProjectStatus with parameters savedProjectId (from SavedProject.id) and status (chosen status enum value).
4. While waiting for the response, project-status-control may set a local “updating” state to disable repeated changes for that item.
5. On success, updateSavedProjectStatus returns the updated saved_project object. my-projects-page-controller merges this updated SavedProject back into its in-memory list and passes the updated list to project-list.
6. The updated status is reflected in the UI. If a statusFilter URL parameter is active and the new status no longer matches the filter, the item will disappear from the visible list as a result of client-side filtering.

Workflow: Removing a project from My Projects
1. User opens projects.html and sees the list rendered by project-list after getMyProjects completes.
2. For a specific project, user clicks the remove control (e.g., an icon or button) provided by project-remove-action.
3. project-remove-action triggers removeSavedProject with savedProjectId taken from SavedProject.id.
4. While awaiting the response, project-remove-action may set a local “removing” state to provide feedback and prevent duplicate clicks.
5. On success (success = true in the removeSavedProject response), my-projects-page-controller removes the corresponding item from its in-memory projects array and passes the updated array to project-list.
6. If the removal leaves the projects array empty, project-empty-state becomes visible within project-list, indicating that there are no saved projects.

Workflow: Re-opening a DIY project tutorial from My Projects
1. User is viewing projects.html and sees the saved projects list.
2. Within project-list, user clicks on a project title rendered by project-item-row.
3. project-item-row constructs a navigation URL to diy_project.html with the diyProjectId query parameter set to the related DIYProject.id (project.diy_project_id via the SavedProject relationship).
4. Browser navigates to diy_project.html?diyProjectId=<DIYProject.id> where the tutorial is displayed (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Filtering projects by status via URL parameters
1. User wants to view only projects with a certain status (e.g., in_progress). They either use the project-status-filter component controls, or arrive via a link that already contains a statusFilter parameter (e.g., projects.html?statusFilter=in_progress).
2. my-projects-page-controller reads the statusFilter parameter from the URL on load or URL change.
3. my-projects-page-controller calls getMyProjects and receives the full projects array.
4. Before rendering, the controller or project-list applies client-side filtering to include only items whose SavedProject.status matches the statusFilter value. The unfiltered list is maintained in memory so the user can change filters without additional API calls.
5. If the user changes the filter using project-status-filter controls, the component updates the statusFilter URL parameter (without reloading the page when possible), and my-projects-page-controller recalculates the filtered view using the existing projects array.
interactions: On initial page load, the my-projects-page-controller component reads URL parameters (highlightSavedProjectId, plus optional statusFilter and sort) and calls getMyProjects. This interaction is automatically triggered by page load and by URL changes relevant to this page.

Within project-status-filter, when the user selects or clears a status filter, the component updates the statusFilter URL parameter accordingly and triggers a re-computation of the visible list from the in-memory projects array.
Within project-list, clicking on a project title triggers navigation to diy_project.html with diyProjectId equal to the associated DIYProject.id via a standard link or programmatic navigation.

Within project-status-control for each project, changing the selected status triggers updateSavedProjectStatus with the appropriate savedProjectId and new status value. The UI reflects a pending state until the API response is received.

Within project-remove-action for each project, clicking the remove control triggers removeSavedProject with the appropriate savedProjectId and, upon a successful response, instructs my-projects-page-controller to remove that item from its local projects array.

Within highlight-saved-project-handler, after getMyProjects returns and the projects list is available, the component checks for highlightSavedProjectId in the URL and, if a matching item exists, marks it in the local UI state for highlighting and optionally scrolls it into view. This does not cause additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s primary state is derived from API responses and URL parameters. No persistent state is stored in localStorage or other client storage.

URL Parameters:
- highlightSavedProjectId (string, optional): If present, this is treated as the ID of a SavedProject that should be visually highlighted when the projects list is first loaded. On page load or URL change, my-projects-page-controller reads this value. After getMyProjects returns, highlight-saved-project-handler locates the matching SavedProject in the projects array and sets a transient highlighted flag in the in-memory state used by project-list. The flag may be cleared when the user navigates away, refreshes, or the item is removed; no API calls depend on this flag.

Optional URL parameters for intra-page state (defined for functional behavior):
- statusFilter (string, optional): If present, it should correspond to one of the SavedProject.status enum values (planned, in_progress, completed). my-projects-page-controller uses this value to compute a filtered list from the full projects array returned by getMyProjects. Changing this parameter (via project-status-filter or manual URL edit) does not trigger a new API call, only a re-filter of the in-memory list.
- sort (string, optional): Used for client-side sorting of the projects list (e.g., by added_at or title) without additional API calls. my-projects-page-controller reads this parameter and applies the corresponding sort to the in-memory projects array before rendering. The allowed values and their meaning are defined in the client logic and do not require additional interfaces.

State Flow:
1. On page load, my-projects-page-controller parses the current URL to read highlightSavedProjectId, statusFilter, and sort.
2. my-projects-page-controller calls getMyProjects once to retrieve the full projects array, representing the current My Projects state.
3. The raw projects array is stored in memory within my-projects-page-controller. Derived views (filtered, sorted, highlighted) are computed from this array using the URL parameters and passed down to project-list.
4. When updateSavedProjectStatus succeeds, the returned saved_project replaces the existing SavedProject with the same id in the in-memory projects array. Derived views are recomputed based on the same URL parameters.
5. When removeSavedProject succeeds, the corresponding SavedProject is removed from the in-memory projects array. Derived views are recomputed; if the array becomes empty, project-empty-state is displayed.
6. If URL parameters change while the user stays on the page (e.g., via filter changes that push a new URL), my-projects-page-controller re-reads the parameters and recalculates the derived views from the existing projects array without re-calling getMyProjects (unless the implementation chooses to force a refresh).

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter reading, and distribution of project data and view state (filters, sorting, highlighting) to child components.
Invokes getMyProjects when the page loads to retrieve the full list of saved projects and passes the result back to my-projects-page-controller.

Uses the highlightSavedProjectId URL parameter and the loaded projects array to mark a specific SavedProject as highlighted for display (and optionally scroll it into view). This component does not modify any backend data.
Provides controls for filtering the visible projects by SavedProject.status and updates the statusFilter URL parameter to reflect the current filter selection.
Allows the user to select a sorting mode for the projects list (for example, by added_at or title) and updates the sort URL parameter; sorting is applied client-side to the projects array.

Displays the list of saved projects after applying filters, sorting, and highlighting. Renders one project-item-row per saved project and shows project-empty-state if there are no projects to display.

Represents a single saved project row or card, showing key DIYProject details (such as title and metadata) and associated SavedProject fields (status, added_at), and wiring up actions for opening the tutorial, changing status, and removing the project.
Embedded in each project-item-row, this control lets the user select one of the allowed SavedProject.status values and triggers updateSavedProjectStatus for the corresponding SavedProject.

Embedded in each project-item-row, this control removes a project from My Projects by calling removeSavedProject and notifying my-projects-page-controller to update its in-memory list when the call succeeds.
Displays a message or guidance when there are no SavedProject records to show in the My Projects list (after filtering or when the list is truly empty).
Handles navigation from a project-item-row to the corresponding DIY project detail page by constructing a URL to diy_project.html with diyProjectId set to DIYProject.id.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with an asymmetric two-column hero: approximately 5 columns for the logo block on the left and 7 columns for the title block on the right, collapsing to a single column on smaller screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup: an asymmetric hero at the very top (left logo panel, right page title and description), followed by a centered, full-width tagline band for a short explanatory line. Directly beneath, a single horizontal control band houses project-status filters and sort options, visually echoing the social bar from the mockup. Below that, the main content switches into a centered single-column rail that contains either a vertical stack of large project cards (list items) or an empty state message.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup: an asymmetric hero at the very top (left logo panel, right page title and description), followed by a centered, full-width tagline band for a short explanatory line. Directly beneath, a single horizontal control band houses project-status filters and sort options, visually echoing the social bar from the mockup. Below that, the main content switches into a centered single-column rail that contains either a vertical stack of large project cards (list items) or an empty state message.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 40–45% of the initial viewport on desktop, with about 40–45% of its width reserved on the left for the logo block and 55–60% on the right for the 'My Projects' typographic block. The tagline band and filter/sort band together take up another ~10–15% of the vertical space, but remain visually light with thin typography and subtle rules. The project list column below spans around 60–70% of the page width, centered, with generous side margins (80–120px on large screens) and 48–64px vertical separation between sections. On smaller screens, the layout collapses to a single column with the hero stacked: logo above, title below, followed by tagline, controls, and then the project list.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low to medium: each major zone (hero, tagline, filters/sort, list) is clearly separated with 48–64px vertical gaps. Card contents use 24–32px internal padding and comfortable line spacing. Controls like filters and sort use compact but airy horizontal layout with plenty of surrounding whitespace to maintain the editorial, romantic feel rather than a dense dashboard appearance.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the bold 'My Projects' title in the right hero panel, then slides left to the romantic logo block, echoing the mockup’s reverse Z-pattern. From there, attention naturally drops to the centered tagline band, then to the horizontally aligned project-status filter and sort controls, which act like a utility version of the social icon bar. Finally, the eye flows down into the vertically stacked project cards or empty state, reinforcing a strong, scroll-driven vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The 'My Projects' page opens with a generous, airy hero that mirrors the blog’s editorial home: on the left, a softly framed square logo panel with the circular gold mark sits against ample white space; on the right, a tall light-gray vertical band holds the large, dark 'MY PROJECTS' title, a peach-toned sublabel such as 'DIY & Decor', and a small uppercase context label like 'SAVED PROJECTS'. This hero spans nearly half the initial viewport and is framed by wide side margins, creating a calm, magazine-like introduction. Directly beneath, a thin full-width rule stretches across the page, with a short centered tagline about managing saved wedding projects floating between two subtle em-dash–like lines. Below that, a centered horizontal band carries the project-status filter chips and a minimal sort control, visually echoing a social icon bar but with a functional, utility tone. The main content rail then begins: a vertical stack of wide, image-led project cards aligned to the center of the page, each with a tall project photo, title, status control, tutorial navigation, and a light-touch remove action. If there are no projects, the cards are replaced by a calm empty state message centered in the same column. Throughout, the composition relies on a 12-column grid, soft pastel accents, generous white space, and thin typography to keep the experience romantic, organized, and gentle rather than utilitarian.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the full-page structural wrapper. It defines a centered main content area that uses an implicit 12-column grid, with wide gutters on left and right (approximately 80–120px margins on large screens) and a maximum content width of about 1120–1280px. Vertically, it arranges sections in order: hero, tagline band, filter/sort band, and main project content (list or empty state), each separated by 48–64px of vertical spacing to maintain the airy editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that flows with the page scroll and defines the global responsive behavior (hero two-column on desktop, single-column stack on smaller viewports).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render visible UI and therefore occupies no explicit visual space. Its effects are perceived only in how the project list area transitions between loading, populated, and empty states within the main content rail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; sits logically behind the main project list area, triggering content changes without affecting layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state derivation component has no direct visual footprint. Its influence shows subtly within each project item row—for example, by applying a slightly richer border, soft highlight, or accent marker to recently saved or priority projects in the list, without altering the overall card size or spacing rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; applied inline within project item rows, modifying styles but not structural layout or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-status filter appears as a centered horizontal row of pill-like filter chips (e.g., All, In Progress, Completed, Archived) directly below the tagline band. It sits within the main content width (about 60–70% of page width) and is centered horizontally, similar to a social icon bar. There is approximately 24–32px vertical space above and below it. The chips themselves are spaced evenly with 8–16px gaps, creating a light row that visually aligns with the central axis of the tagline above and the project list below. On smaller screens, the chips wrap to multiple lines while remaining centered, preserving the light, airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow, aligned centrally; on scroll it moves up naturally, not fixed, maintaining a simple editorial reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-sort control sits on the same horizontal band as the project-status filter, but aligned toward the right edge of the main content column. On desktop, it appears as a compact text-and-icon dropdown or segmented control, occupying roughly 15–20% of the content width and anchored to the right so that the filter chips visually dominate the center while sort quietly balances the row. There is a shared baseline between the filter chips and the sort control. On tablet and mobile, the sort control drops below the filter row, left-aligned within the same central column, with 12–16px vertical spacing separating the two for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline with the filter row on larger screens and stacks beneath on smaller screens; otherwise behaves as a standard inline control within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project list occupies the main content rail beneath the filter/sort band, starting after about 48px of vertical spacing. It forms a single centered column of project cards that spans approximately 60–70% of the page width on desktop, with consistent left and right padding (24–32px inside the column). Each card (project-item-row) is separated vertically by 32–48px, maintaining generous white space between projects. The list extends downward as the user scrolls, preserving the vertical reading flow with the topmost card slightly closer to the filters to visually connect them. On smaller screens, the list expands to nearly full width (with 16–24px side margins) while keeping the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable vertical stack within the page flow; no fixed height, expanding naturally with the number of projects.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each project item is presented as a large, card-like row within the project list, visually similar to a blog post card. The card spans the full width of the list column and includes a subtle shadow or border with 24–32px internal padding. At the top of the card, a wide project image (or thumbnail placeholder) stretches edge-to-edge within the card, typically occupying about the top 40–50% of the card height and maintaining a portrait or 4:3 ratio to echo the image-driven feel of the mockup. Below the image, the project title appears left-aligned in a larger, editorial type size, with a brief one-line description or metadata beneath it in lighter text. At the bottom of the card, a horizontal control row contains the project-status control on the left, the projects-to-tutorial-navigation primary link/button just to its right, and the remove action aligned to the far right. Vertical spacing between image, title section, and control row follows the 16–24px scale, preserving a calm, structured layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a flexible block-level card within the list, growing vertically with content; the internal elements align to a simple vertical stack with a final horizontal control row at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside each project card, the project-status control sits in the lower section, aligned to the left edge of the card’s content area. It appears as a small but refined pill or dropdown (e.g., 'In Progress', 'Completed') using thin uppercase or lightly tracked text to echo the mockup’s labels. It sits in the same horizontal row as the tutorial navigation and remove action, with about 8–16px spacing between it and the adjacent tutorial link. Vertically, this control row is anchored 16–24px below the project description and 16–24px above the bottom edge of the card, maintaining consistent padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the card’s bottom control row; it does not change the card’s overall size dramatically when its value changes, maintaining a stable layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project remove action is positioned at the far right of the bottom control row in each project card, horizontally aligned with the project-status control and tutorial navigation. It appears as a light, text-based link or small icon-button with minimal visual weight—perhaps a thin outlined icon or understated text like 'Remove'—so it is clearly available but does not dominate the card. There is at least 16–24px of space between this action and the right edge of the card, aligning with the card’s internal padding. On smaller screens, if horizontal space is tight, this action may wrap to a second line beneath the status and tutorial actions, still right-aligned and separated by 8–12px of vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline action within the card’s control row; it does not alter surrounding layout beyond potential line-wrapping on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no projects, the empty state occupies the same central column used by the project list, beginning in the space where the first card would normally appear. It is centered both horizontally and, if content height permits, visually biased toward the upper half of the remaining viewport to maintain proximity to the filters and tagline. The empty state might include a light illustration or icon at the top, a medium-weight headline ('No saved projects yet') in the center, and a brief explanatory sentence below, all stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. A primary call-to-action (using the projects-to-tutorial-navigation link) sits beneath this text, centered, with generous padding (12px x 24px) to echo the button style from the mockup. Surrounding white space is ample, with at least 48px of margin above and below the block to preserve the airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the project list region entirely when active; behaves as a single centered content block within the main column, scrolling with the page if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation link is expressed in two contexts. Within each project card, it appears in the bottom control row just to the right of the project-status control, as the primary action (e.g., 'Open tutorial'), styled more prominently than the remove action—perhaps as an understated filled or outlined button using a soft pastel accent that matches the wedding palette. It aligns on the same baseline as the status control and remove action, with 8–16px spacing on either side. In the empty state, this component becomes the main centered call-to-action button beneath the empty-state text, spanning about 25–35% of the content width on desktop (auto-width on mobile) and surrounded by plentiful white space. In both cases, it reinforces the vertical flow by inviting users deeper into project tutorials without overwhelming the minimal aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the project card control row, and block-level, centered within the empty state; in both uses it flows naturally with surrounding text and controls without fixed or sticky behavior.
